Overall, this is competent-to-good YA fiction with genuine wit, consistent characterization, and some strong emotional moments, but with structural gaps relative to its own plan and a few tell-don't-show issues. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four: The Official Version The next morning, the school held an assembly about rumours. This was how Maya knew things had become serious. The school only held assemblies about rumours when the rumours were true enough to be inconvenient. Students filed into the hall in damp blazers. The heating had been turned up too high, so everyone smelled faintly of wet wool and panic. On the stage, beneath a banner reading **RESPECT, RESPONSIBILITY, RESILIENCE**, stood Martin Ward, the headteacher, and a cardboard display about online safety that had lost one of its corners. Maya sat between Finn and Elsie. Elsie had not spoken to them since the message with four blood drops. She had arrived at school early, handed the memory card to nobody, and spent registration staring at the locked drawer of her desk. Finn leaned towards Maya. “Do you think the assembly is about Leah?” “It’s about rumours.” “That wasn’t my question.” “It’s always your question.” He considered this. “Fair.” Martin Ward stepped forward. He was a handsome man in the polished, careful way of people who had spent years learning how to appear approachable. His grey hair was neat. His tie had tiny blue squares. He smiled as if the school hall were a room he had personally persuaded to exist. “Over the past few days,” he began, “there has been some unhelpful speculation surrounding the absence of a pupil.” Nobody moved. Maya felt the hall change. Even the pupils who had not known about Leah understood now that there was something to know. “Leah Mercer is currently at home with her family,” Martin continued. “We ask that students respect her privacy and refrain from sharing material online.” A boy near the front whispered, “She’s not at home.” His friend elbowed him. Martin’s smile remained in place. “Rumours can cause real distress. They can also make it more difficult for staff to support those involved.” Maya thought of Leah’s bag, left in the locker. She thought of the message. She thought of the school marking her absent as “other authorised circumstances,” as if a person could disappear into a dropdown menu. Martin continued. “Responsible communication means checking facts before repeating allegations.” At the side of the stage, two teachers were whispering. One of them glanced at Martin and stopped. Finn wrote something in the margin of his planner and pushed it towards Maya. **They should have called it “How Not To Make People Curious.”** Maya did not smile. The assembly ended with a reminder that students should speak to a trusted adult if they were worried. As they filed out, a Year Ten boy asked a teacher whether the trusted adult could be someone outside school. The teacher said, “Ideally, no.” In the library, Maya put the first-aid photocopy on the table beside the video still. “They want us to stop asking questions,” she said. Finn sat opposite. “That’s usually how you know the questions are quality.” Elsie stood by the window, looking down at the courtyard. Maya turned to her. “What happened after Aaron fell?” Elsie did not turn around. “You heard Leah say to call an ambulance,” Maya continued. “What happened next?” “Mr Venn told someone to get the first-aid kit.” “Who?” “I don’t know.” “You were there.” “I know I was there.” “Then stop saying you don’t know.” Elsie’s hands tightened around the edge of the windowsill. “Aaron was conscious. He was bleeding, but he was conscious. Mr Venn said not to move him. Then the headteacher came.” “Your father.” “Yes.” “What did he do?” “He asked who had filmed it.” Finn frowned. “Not how Aaron was?” “He asked that after.” “But first he asked about the video.” Elsie looked at them. “There were three videos. Maybe four. Everyone had their phones out.” Maya thought of the old group chat, the deleted files, the frozen thumbnail. “Why did the school say Aaron was alone?” “Because they said it was simpler.” “Who said?” “My father.” The words came out flatly. Elsie sat down. For a while, nobody spoke. Outside, pupils crossed the courtyard in groups, heads bent against the rain. Maya messaged Aaron. She wrote and deleted several versions before settling on: **Hi. I’m sorry to bother you. We found part of the video from October. We won’t share anything without asking. Can I ask what you want people to know?** He replied twenty minutes later. **That I didn’t do it alone.** Maya waited. Another message appeared. **But I don’t want the video of me falling anywhere.** She showed Finn and Elsie. Finn nodded. “That’s fair.” “More than fair,” Maya said. Aaron sent one more message. **They told me if I made a formal complaint I could lose my place on the apprenticeship programme. Mum said we couldn’t afford lawyers. So I signed the statement.** Maya read it again. The school’s version had not needed a grand conspiracy. It had needed a frightened boy, tired parents, and adults who knew exactly how much pressure to apply. Finn looked sick. “Can we use that?” he asked. “No.” “Why not?” “Because he said what he wants people to know. He didn’t say we could pass it around.” Finn rubbed his face. “Then what do we do?” Maya looked at the photocopy. The missing page. The altered account. The adults asking about phones before ambulances. “We find the original.” Elsie gave a small, humourless laugh. “You think it still exists?” “If it didn’t, Leah wouldn’t be sending messages.” “Leah sends messages because she likes people to think she has a plan.” Maya looked at her. “You’re afraid she does.” Elsie did not answer. Finn picked up his phone. “There’s a local journalist who covers the council. My mum knows her.” “No.” “I haven’t said anything yet.” “You’re thinking it.” “It’s evidence.” “It’s Aaron’s life.” “I’m not sending his medical records. I’m sending the audio.” “Without asking?” Finn hesitated. Maya knew that hesitation. It was the exact length of time before he decided that urgency made permission unnecessary. “Finn.” “I’ll send it anonymously.” “That doesn’t make it yours.” His face reddened. “And what are you doing? Collecting it? Keeping it safe until everyone forgets?” The accusation landed because it had already been waiting inside her. Finn uploaded the short recording. Maya watched the progress bar move. When it reached one hundred percent, the journalist replied almost immediately. **Interesting. Is there footage of the injury?** Finn looked at the message. Maya closed her eyes. Her phone buzzed. A message from Leah’s number. **You’re making it worse.** No emoji this time. No code. Just words. For some reason, that frightened Maya more than the blood.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Four is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Prose Quality** The prose is controlled and dry, with the authorial voice maintaining consistent distance. The opening line — "The next morning, the school held an assembly about rumours. This was how Maya knew things had become serious. The school only held assemblies about rumours when the rumours were true enough to be inconvenient." — is an excellent example of the intended tone: understated, observant, and quietly comic. The logic is circular in a way that feels true to institutional life. This is the kind of writing that earns its lightness. The description of Martin Ward — "a handsome man in the polished, careful way of people who had spent years learning how to appear approachable" — is precise and characterful without being overwrought. Similarly, "His smile remained in place" during the assembly is economical and effective. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "She thought of the school marking her absent as 'other authorised circumstances,' as if a person could disappear into a dropdown menu" is good but sits at the edge of being too neat, too writerly. It works, but just barely. The line "The accusation landed because it had already been waiting inside her" is slightly more purple than the surrounding prose, though not egregiously so. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange between Maya and Finn at the assembly opening is natural and characterful: "Do you think the assembly is about Leah?" "It's about rumours." "That wasn't my question." "It's always your question." This captures their dynamic efficiently. Finn's planner note — "They should have called it 'How Not To Make People Curious'" — is exactly the right kind of joke: funny enough to be plausible, not so funny it breaks the tone. The interrogation of Elsie is well-handled. The staccato rhythm of Maya's questions and Elsie's reluctant answers creates genuine tension. "He asked who had filmed it" / "Not how Aaron was?" / "He asked that after" — this is economical and devastating. The dialogue does real work here. Aaron's text messages are particularly well-judged. "That I didn't do it alone" is a perfect line — simple, loaded, and character-consistent. The follow-up about the apprenticeship programme and the signed statement is the chapter's most emotionally effective moment, delivered through the most restrained means. **Character Consistency** All characters behave consistently with their profiles. Finn's impulsive decision to send the recording without permission is well-prepared and feels inevitable rather than contrived. His rationalizations ("I'll send it anonymously," "It's evidence") are exactly right for his character. Maya's fury and her recognition that the accusation "had already been waiting inside her" shows self-awareness without being too tidy. Elsie's gradual, reluctant disclosure — "He asked who had filmed it" — is appropriately painful and consistent with someone dismantling a carefully constructed self-deception. Martin Ward's assembly speech is pitch-perfect: the language of concern deployed to suppress inquiry. "Responsible communication means checking facts before repeating allegations" is exactly the kind of institutional doublespeak the plan called for. **Plot Execution** The chapter follows its plan closely. The assembly, the library scene, Aaron's messages, Finn's mistake, and Leah's final text all land in the right order and with the right weight. The plan called for the cover-up to be revealed as emerging from small decisions rather than grand villainy, and the chapter delivers this: "The school's version had not needed a grand conspiracy. It had needed a frightened boy, tired parents, and adults who knew exactly how much pressure to apply." This is the chapter's thematic core, stated clearly but not over-explained. The journalist's reply — "Interesting. Is there footage of the injury?" — is a nicely chilling detail that shows how quickly the story could escape their control. **Weaknesses** The chapter is slightly thin on the comic elements the plan promised. The assembly scene has good comic potential but doesn't fully exploit it — the plan mentioned "pupils are told not to speculate while teachers visibly exchange rumours at the side of the hall," and while there's a gesture toward this ("At the side of the stage, two teachers were whispering"), it's underdeveloped. The Year Ten boy asking whether the trusted adult could be someone outside school is a good touch, but the chapter could have leaned slightly harder into the institutional absurdity. The ending — Leah's message without emoji — is effective, but "For some reason, that frightened Maya more than the blood" is a slightly weak final line. It tells us what to feel rather than trusting the image to do the work. The plan's chapter ending was stronger in conception: the message itself should carry the weight without the explanatory coda. The chapter also doesn't quite deliver on the plan's note that "Maya compares the official account with the recording" in a detailed way — this is somewhat glossed over in favor of the Elsie interrogation, which is the right choice dramatically but means one planned beat is soft. **Overall Assessment** This is confident, well-crafted YA fiction that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterization, and earns its emotional moments through restraint rather than melodrama. The prose is clean, the dialogue is strong, and the moral complexity is handled with appropriate subtlety. Minor weaknesses in the final line and some underdeveloped comic potential keep it from being exceptional, but it is genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five: Come Alone For the rest of the day, Leah’s message sat on Maya’s phone like an accusation with no punctuation. **You’re making it worse.** Maya showed Finn. He read it, then looked away. “I know,” he said. “You don’t know that.” “I sent the recording.” “You sent it to a journalist.” “That’s worse than sending it to the group chat, yes.” Maya locked her phone. “Did you tell her about Aaron?” “No.” “Did you tell her about the memory card?” “No.” “Then how does she know?” Finn rubbed the bruise on his chin. The window had left it purple at the edges. “Maybe she’s watching us.” “From where?” “School’s full of cameras.” “Not in the toilets.” “I didn’t mean—” “I know.” They were standing outside the library, where a display of recommended books included three thrillers about missing girls. Maya had noticed this before. She had never thought of it as an omen. Now she wondered whether the library had always been making fun of them. Elsie came towards them carrying a stack of prefect forms. “Did you send it?” she asked Finn. Maya turned. “How did you know?” “Because you’re both standing like people who have just made a decision they regret.” Finn raised a hand. “In fairness, I regret it less than she does.” Elsie looked at Maya. “Leah’s phone connected to the school network last night.” Maya stared at her. “The school network?” “It connected for seventeen seconds at 1:14 a.m.” “Can you see where?” “Not exactly. The network logs the access point, not the device’s room.” “Why can you see the logs?” Elsie looked at the prefect forms. “Because my father’s assistant left the admin tablet unlocked.” “That is not an answer.” “It’s the answer you’re getting.” Finn leaned against the wall. “Could she be in school?” “She could have left the phone in school,” Elsie said. “Or connected from outside. The signal reaches the front gates.” “Why would she do that?” “To make us look.” Maya thought of Leah’s habits. She used to hide notes in obvious places and then accuse Maya of being unobservant when she found them. Leah had always believed the best hiding place was somewhere people had emotional reasons not to search. Maya looked at the words on her screen. **You’re making it worse.** “She’s still trying to control what we do,” Maya said. Elsie’s expression flickered. “That doesn’t mean she’s wrong.” “No. It means she’s Leah.” They found the phone’s connection again that night. Elsie had borrowed the admin tablet. Finn had brought a torch, two cereal bars, and a small screwdriver he claimed was “for circumstances.” Maya had brought nothing except her phone, which was at twelve percent. The school gates were locked, but the side entrance beside the sports hall had a broken latch. The building was dark except for the security lights, which made every wet surface shine. The admin tablet showed three connection times: 1:14 a.m. 2:06 a.m. 3:32 a.m. Each from the same access point. “Reception,” Elsie said. Maya stared at the map. The old reception area was near the main entrance, but the signal could also reach the waiting room and the front steps. “Maybe she came in through the gate,” Finn said. “Why would she keep connecting the phone?” Maya asked. “To tell us she’s clever.” “Or to tell us where she is.” Elsie looked at Maya. “You said she hides things where people have emotional reasons not to search.” “I said that in my head.” “Your face said it.” Maya thought of the places she and Leah had used when they were younger. The cupboard beneath the stairs. Room 3. The empty bus shelter behind the leisure centre. The upstairs waiting area at the town bus station, where they had once sat until midnight after Leah’s mother’s boyfriend had thrown the plate. They had missed the last bus home on purpose. Leah had said, “If we stay here, no one can make us go anywhere.” Maya had replied, “That’s not how bus stations work.” The memory arrived with such force that she stopped walking. “What?” Finn asked. “Bus station.” Elsie frowned. “What about it?” “It’s where we used to go.” “Used to go when?” “When we wanted to disappear.” Finn’s phone made a soft chime. A location pin appeared in the Year Eleven group chat. Maya’s phone buzzed a second later. The message was from Leah. **Come alone. Platform four.** Finn looked at her. “No,” he said. Maya had not said anything. “You’re not going alone.” “She asked me to.” “She also asked you to decode blood emojis and break into a science room.” “She didn’t ask me to do those things.” “You did them anyway.” Elsie took the phone from Maya and examined the pin. “This is live.” “So?” “So if she sent it from the bus station, she’s there now.” Maya reached for the phone. Elsie did not give it back. “You cannot go alone,” she said. “You’re both coming?” “No. We’re following.” “That is still coming.” “It’s different.” “It really isn’t.” Finn had already put on his coat. “I’ll be inconspicuous.” “You have a torch clipped to your belt.” “It’s a keyring.” “It says FORENSICS.” “It was a gift.” The bus station was nearly empty. Rain streaked the glass roof. The electronic departures board announced that the 22:45 to Ashby was delayed by an unknown number of minutes, which Maya thought was a surprisingly honest form of public information. Finn and Elsie followed at a distance, though Finn hid behind a vending machine that was too narrow to conceal even one shoulder. Maya climbed the stairs to the upper waiting area. There were rows of plastic seats, a broken ticket machine, and a poster advertising a seaside resort no bus had gone to in years. Leah was sitting beneath the poster. She looked smaller than Maya remembered. Her red-tipped hair was wet and sticking to her cheeks. She wore a hoodie beneath her blazer and had one of the sleeves pulled over her hand. For half a second, Maya’s relief was so strong it hurt. Then Leah saw Finn and Elsie behind her. Her relief disappeared. “I said alone.” Maya stopped three steps below her. “They followed me.” “You let them.” “Finn is difficult to prevent.” “I’m standing right here,” Finn said. Leah’s eyes moved to Elsie. “You brought her?” “She was already involved.” “I was never involved,” Elsie said. Leah laughed once. “That’s the whole problem.” Maya climbed the last steps. “Are you hurt?” “No.” “Have you eaten?” “No.” Finn reached into his coat and produced a cereal bar. Leah stared at it. “What?” he said. “It’s chocolate.” “Everything is chocolate with you.” “That’s not true. Sometimes it’s crisps.” Maya sat opposite Leah. For a moment, neither of them spoke. Below, a bus hissed at the kerb and pulled away empty. “Why did you send the first message?” Maya asked. Leah looked at her. “Not the obvious answer.” “I wanted you to come.” “Why me?” “Because you knew what it meant.” “That’s not enough.” “It was for me.” Maya felt the old anger rise, familiar and almost comforting. “You could have called the police.” “They would ask where I got the evidence.” “You could have called Aaron.” “He doesn’t want to be involved.” “So you involved me.” Leah’s mouth tightened. “You were already involved.” “How?” Leah looked down at the sleeve covering her hand. “Because you were the person I told first,” she said. “Before the school. Before Elsie. Before anyone.” Maya remembered the old rumour. Leah standing beside her locker, saying, *I’m not making this up.* Maya had looked at the floor. “You didn’t believe me then,” Leah said. “No.” “You believed me.” Maya could not answer. A sound came from the stairs. Footsteps. Leah looked past her. Her face changed. “He found me,” she said. Maya turned. At the bottom of the stairs, beneath the flickering station light, stood Mr Venn.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Five of this darkly comic British YA story is a strong piece of craft that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND TONE: The chapter sustains the dry, observational British voice effectively. The humour is earned rather than forced — the library display of "three thrillers about missing girls" is a genuinely good comic observation that also carries thematic weight. Finn's FORENSICS keyring, the departures board announcing a delay "by an unknown number of minutes" (described as "a surprisingly honest form of public information"), and the vending machine too narrow to conceal even one shoulder all land cleanly. These jokes arise from character and situation rather than being grafted on. The balance between comedy and genuine tension is well-managed. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange between Maya and Finn about the journalist is terse and revealing — "You sent it to a journalist." / "That's worse than sending it to the group chat, yes." — and the rhythm feels authentic to teenagers who are frightened and trying not to show it. Elsie's "It's the answer you're getting" is perfectly in character. The Leah reunion scene is emotionally precise: "Because you were the person I told first" lands with real weight, and the callback to the original betrayal ("You didn't believe me then" / "No." / "You believed me.") is handled with admirable restraint. The dialogue does not over-explain. CHARACTERS: All four characters behave consistently with their profiles. Finn's compulsive helpfulness (the cereal bar), his performative bravado (the torch, the FORENSICS label), and his genuine anxiety are all present. Elsie's controlled intelligence and her slightly cold precision ("Your face said it") are well-rendered. Leah's anger-as-fear dynamic is captured in her first reaction to seeing Elsie. Maya's observational quality and her tendency to remember exact words are both present. The moment where Maya realises she "said that in my head" is a small but effective character beat. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The reunion between Maya and Leah is genuinely moving without being sentimental. The line "For half a second, Maya's relief was so strong it hurt" is one of the better emotional beats in the chapter — it's specific and physical without being overwrought. The callback to the original betrayal ("I'm not making this up") is handled with restraint; the reader fills in the weight of it. The chapter earns its emotional moments. PLOT: The chapter follows its plan closely. The phone network connection, the bus station location, the "come alone" message, Finn and Elsie following anyway, the reunion, and Mr Venn's arrival at the end all occur as planned. The pacing is good — the investigation section moves efficiently, and the station scene is given appropriate space. The cliffhanger ending with Mr Venn is not cheap; it's been set up by Leah's warning that he was coming. WEAKNESSES: There are a few minor issues. The line "The memory arrived with such force that she stopped walking" is slightly melodramatic for this voice — it tells us the memory is powerful rather than letting the memory itself do the work. The transition from the school network investigation to the bus station is slightly compressed; the logic of how they connect the access point to the bus station is a little thin (the signal "reaches the front gates" doesn't quite explain why Maya leaps to the bus station). The chapter plan mentions Elsie suggesting the hiding place connected to childhood, but in the chapter it's Maya who makes the leap — this is a minor deviation but actually works better dramatically. The ending with Mr Venn is effective but the chapter plan notes he arrives because "he believes she still has the original recording" — this context is absent from the chapter, making his arrival feel slightly more convenient than it should. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Very little. The chapter is disciplined. "Like an accusation with no punctuation" is a slightly strained metaphor but not egregious. The writing generally trusts its material. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-structured. The three-act movement (school investigation, travel to station, reunion) is clear. FOLLOWED PLAN: The chapter follows the plan closely, hitting all major beats: network connection, bus station, "come alone," Finn and Elsie following, Leah's anger at seeing them, the emotional confrontation about the original betrayal, and Mr Venn's arrival. The cereal bar gag is present. The chapter omits some of the plan's detail about Leah explaining she sent the original message because someone searched her room — this is partially covered but not fully. The plan also specifies Leah says Maya "once promised that if something serious happened, she would believe her" — this is gestured at but not stated explicitly. OVERALL: This is a confident, well-executed chapter that maintains voice, advances plot, develops character, and earns its emotional moments. It is not a masterpiece — some transitions are slightly thin, and one or two beats from the plan are undercooked — but it is genuinely good YA fiction with a distinctive voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six: The Bit Where Everyone Runs Mr Venn stood at the bottom of the stairs with rain darkening the shoulders of his coat. He looked less like a sinister teacher and more like a man who had been awake too long and was beginning to regret every decision that had led him to a bus station. This was inconvenient. Maya had prepared herself to hate him more easily. “Leah,” he called. Leah stood. Maya caught her wrist. “Don’t.” Leah looked at her hand, then at Maya’s face. “I’m not going to attack him.” “I wasn’t worried about that.” “I might say something.” “That’s what I was worried about.” Mr Venn climbed the stairs. Finn moved in front of Maya and immediately looked as though he had forgotten why. “Evening,” he said. Mr Venn stopped. “Finn.” “Mr Venn.” “This isn’t appropriate.” “Neither is following a teenager to a bus station.” Mr Venn looked at Leah. “Your mother is worried.” “My mother thinks I’m at Priya’s.” “Then you should correct that.” “Should I?” Mr Venn sighed. He had the exhausted patience of a man expecting to be admired for having patience. “Leah, whatever you think you’ve found, this is not how you handle it.” “Interesting,” Leah said. “How should I handle it?” “You come back to school. We sit down with your mother and the safeguarding lead. We work through the proper channels.” “You mean the channels where reports disappear?” Mr Venn’s face tightened. Maya stepped forward. “Did you tell pupils to delete the videos?” “I told them not to circulate footage of an injured student.” “That isn’t what I asked.” “I was protecting Aaron.” “Were you protecting him when you told everyone he fell alone?” Mr Venn looked at her then. His expression was not angry. It was more unsettling than anger. It was disappointed. “You don’t understand the situation.” “That’s what everyone says when the situation is simple.” Finn murmured, “Technically, complicated situations also say it.” Nobody looked at him. Mr Venn folded his arms. “The activity was poorly supervised. I accept that. Aaron was injured. We dealt with it.” “You dealt with the video,” Maya said. “We dealt with the situation.” “Why did you ask who had filmed it before asking whether Aaron was all right?” For the first time, Mr Venn had no immediate answer. Leah took a small black memory card from her pocket. Maya saw it and understood. The original recording. Mr Venn saw it too. His face changed—not dramatically, not like a villain in a film, but enough. His eyes dropped to the card. His right hand flexed. “Give that to me,” he said. “No,” Leah replied. “It contains private material.” “I know.” “Then you have no right to keep it.” “Neither did you.” Mr Venn stepped closer. Elsie moved between them. “Don’t,” she said. He looked at her. “Elsie, go home.” “No.” “Your father is extremely worried.” “He sent you?” Mr Venn’s silence answered. Elsie’s mouth twisted. “Of course he did.” “He wants this resolved before it becomes worse.” “It became worse when you let Aaron sign that statement.” “That statement was accurate.” “It said he was alone.” “He was alone when he fell.” Elsie stared at him. Mr Venn looked away first. Maya heard the bus station announcement crackle overhead. **The 22:45 service to Ashby is delayed due to an obstruction on the line.** “Which line?” Finn whispered. “Don’t start.” “People deserve to know.” Leah closed her fingers around the card. “I’m taking this to the awards evening.” Mr Venn’s expression hardened. “You will not.” “I’m going to play it before your safety speech.” “You’re threatening the school.” “I’m describing an event.” “You’re threatening pupils.” “No. You’re threatening pupils. I’m just making it harder to pretend.” Mr Venn turned to Maya. “You know this is not what Aaron wants.” Maya felt the sentence strike. “How do you know?” “Because he told us he wanted privacy.” “He told me he wants the school to admit he wasn’t alone.” “And showing the footage would make him a spectacle.” Maya looked at Leah. Leah’s face had gone still. Mr Venn saw it and pressed on. “You’re using him. You’re all using him. You’ve decided that because something happened to him, you get to decide what happens next.” No one spoke. The accusation was true enough to hurt. Finn looked at the floor. Elsie’s shoulders had folded inward. Leah gripped the memory card so tightly that her knuckles whitened. Maya thought of the journalist’s message. **Is there footage of the injury?** She thought of Aaron’s words. **I don’t want the video of me falling anywhere.** “We don’t play that part,” Maya said. Leah looked at her. “We show the altered report,” Maya continued. “The audio of Mr Venn telling people to delete things. Elsie tells the truth. We don’t show Aaron.” “That won’t be enough,” Leah said. “It has to be.” “People will say it’s edited.” “They’ll say that anyway.” “They’ll say I’m obsessed.” “You are obsessed.” Leah flinched. Maya regretted it immediately, but did not take it back. “You’re not wrong,” Maya said. “You’re just not the only person who gets a say.” Mr Venn stepped toward them. “Give me the card.” Finn grabbed Maya’s sleeve. “We should go.” “Where?” “Anywhere that isn’t here.” It was not a good plan, but it was the only one available. They ran. Not dramatically. Finn’s first step caught on the edge of the stair, and he collided with a recycling bin. Elsie shoved open the fire exit. Leah went through first, Maya behind her. Mr Venn shouted their names. Outside, rain hit Maya’s face. They crossed the bus station forecourt, slipping between parked taxis. “Stop!” Mr Venn called. A bus pulled in. Finn waved at it. The driver shook his head and kept moving. “We’re not at a stop!” the driver shouted through the closed doors. “It’s an emergency!” Finn shouted back. “It’s a bus!” They ran around the side of the station and crouched behind a concrete planter. Finn had lost one shoe. Leah was breathing hard. Maya looked at her. “You’re going to the awards evening?” “Yes.” “With what?” Leah held up the memory card. “You said you had the original recording.” “I do.” “Does it show Aaron?” “Yes.” “Then we use only what he has agreed to.” Leah looked angry, frightened, and very tired. “What if it isn’t enough?” Maya thought of the assembly. Martin Ward telling them responsible communication meant checking facts. The teachers whispering at the edge of the stage. The school’s clean words wrapped around an ugly event. “Then we tell the truth badly,” she said. “But we tell the part that belongs to us.” Finn found his missing shoe beneath the planter. “Can I say one thing?” “No,” Maya and Elsie said together. He put the shoe on anyway. Leah looked towards the station entrance. Mr Venn had disappeared. “My father will stop us,” Elsie said. “Your father is the headteacher,” Finn replied. “He can stop nearly anything.” “Not if we send the evidence first.” Maya looked at him. “To whom?” “The journalist. The safeguarding officer. Aaron’s parents, if they want it. Everyone who needs to know.” “No uploading.” “I know.” “Do you?” Finn met her eyes. “I know now.” Across the road, the school’s awards banner hung above the entrance, bright and untouched by the weather. **SAFETY IS EVERYONE’S RESPONSIBILITY.** Beneath it, someone had taped a handwritten note. **THEN WHY WAS IT HIS?** Maya stared at the words. Leah slipped the memory card into her palm. “Tomorrow night,” Leah said. Maya closed her fingers around it. “Tomorrow night,” she agreed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's intentions, demonstrating considerable craft in several areas while having a few minor weaknesses worth examining. **Dialogue and Character Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. Mr Venn's lines are precisely calibrated to his character profile: he speaks in the language of institutional concern while evading direct accountability. "I was protecting Aaron" followed by Maya's "Were you protecting him when you told everyone he fell alone?" is a clean, effective exchange that reveals character through conflict rather than exposition. The rhythm of the interrogation feels natural and earned. Finn's comic interjections ("Technically, complicated situations also say it") land well because they're brief and don't overstay their welcome. The bus driver exchange ("It's an emergency!" / "It's a bus!") is genuinely funny and tonally appropriate for the darkly comic register the story aims for. Leah's dialogue is consistent with her profile: theatrical, precise, and using exposure as leverage. "I'm not threatening pupils. I'm just making it harder to pretend" is exactly the kind of line her character would produce. **The Central Confrontation** Mr Venn's accusation — "You're using him. You're all using him" — is the chapter's moral pivot, and it's handled with admirable restraint. The narrative acknowledges it lands: "The accusation was true enough to hurt." This is the kind of moral complexity the plan called for, and the chapter delivers it without editorializing excessively. Maya's response — "We don't play that part" — and the subsequent negotiation with Leah is the chapter's best sequence. It dramatizes the central ethical tension (truth vs. spectacle, justice vs. privacy) through action and dialogue rather than internal monologue. **The Chase Sequence** The plan called for an "awkward rather than cinematic" chase, and the chapter delivers: Finn colliding with a recycling bin, the bus driver refusing to stop, the lost shoe. These details are specific and funny without being cartoonish. The concrete planter as hiding spot is appropriately undignified. **Weaknesses** The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states. "Leah looked angry, frightened, and very tired" is a direct statement of three emotions that could have been rendered more obliquely. Similarly, "Maya felt the sentence strike" is slightly on-the-nose, though it's brief enough not to be seriously damaging. The line "He had the exhausted patience of a man expecting to be admired for having patience" is the chapter's most overtly writerly moment. It works — it's accurate to the character — but it's the kind of observation that risks feeling too polished for the close-third-person voice. It's a minor issue. The ending with the banner and handwritten note is effective but slightly convenient — the note appearing exactly when needed for thematic punctuation feels a touch staged. However, the plan explicitly included this detail, so it's a planned beat rather than an improvised one. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely: Mr Venn arrives as a tired, frightened adult rather than a villain; the group escapes awkwardly; the moral debate about Aaron's footage occurs; Finn's earlier mistake is referenced; the memory card is transferred; the awards evening is set up. All major beats are hit. Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Elsie's confrontation with Mr Venn about her father sending him is a strong character moment. Maya's "You're not wrong. You're just not the only person who gets a say" is exactly the kind of sharp, slightly cruel honesty her profile describes. **Overall Assessment** This is accomplished YA fiction with genuine moral intelligence. The darkly comic tone is maintained without undermining the serious stakes. The dialogue is the standout element. Minor weaknesses in showing vs. telling and one or two slightly over-polished observations prevent it from being exceptional, but it's a confident, well-executed chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven: Awards for Everyone The awards evening began with a problem involving the microphone. This was appropriate. The school had spent months preparing a celebration of achievement and could not make a sound come out of a speaker. “Testing,” said Martin Ward. The microphone produced a deep, explosive hum. Parents flinched. A toddler began to cry. “Excellent,” Finn whispered from behind the curtain. “We’ve already established a theme.” Maya stood beside the presentation desk with the memory card in her pocket. Her palms were damp. She had washed them twice in the disabled toilet, which had not helped. The hall had been decorated with red balloons and silver stars. Across the back wall hung a banner: **SAFETY IS EVERYONE’S RESPONSIBILITY** Someone had removed the handwritten note from beneath it. Maya wondered who had done that. A teacher, probably. Someone who had decided that an accusation was untidy. Leah stood near the rear doors in a black jumper, watching the stage. She had refused to wear her blazer. Elsie was backstage, where her father was arranging the certificates. Finn had borrowed a school laptop from the music department and was crouched beneath the presentation table. “Can you see the files?” Maya whispered. “Yes.” “Can you see the folder marked DO NOT OPEN?” “Yes.” “Why is there a folder marked that?” “Because adults are terrible at hiding things.” “Don’t open it.” “I wasn’t going to.” His voice had the wounded quality of someone being accused of a crime he had only considered. The audience settled. Parents rustled programmes. Pupils whispered behind their hands. At the front, Aaron’s parents sat together, his mother holding her handbag on her lap with both hands. Aaron was not there. Maya had asked him to come. He had replied: **I don’t want to be in the room when everyone sees me.** She understood. Martin Ward stepped to the microphone. It worked this time. “Good evening, everyone. It is a pleasure to welcome you to our annual celebration of student achievement.” A parent coughed. Martin smiled. “This year has reminded us of the importance of resilience, responsibility, and community.” Maya felt Leah looking at her. Martin continued. “We have faced challenges, as every community does, but we have emerged stronger through openness, compassion, and a commitment to learning.” Finn’s head appeared briefly from beneath the table. “Is he being serious?” he mouthed. Maya pushed him back down. Martin spoke for another three minutes. He mentioned “a period of reflection” without referring to anything that had happened. He mentioned “appropriate channels” twice. Then he introduced Mr Venn, who received polite applause. Mr Venn came onstage. He looked tired. He had shaved badly, leaving a red patch beneath his jaw. His eyes found Leah at the back of the hall. “Tonight,” he said, “we celebrate the courage our students show every day.” Maya felt the memory card press against her pocket. She looked at Aaron’s parents. His mother was staring at the floor. Maya stood. Her chair scraped loudly. Heads turned. Martin paused. “Maya,” he said, with the smile he used when a student had made an unexpected decision. “Is there a problem?” “Yes,” Maya said. Her voice sounded too quiet. She walked towards the stage anyway. Someone laughed. It might have been a pupil. It might have been a parent who thought this was part of the evening. Martin stepped down to meet her. “This is not the time.” “When is?” His smile disappeared. Maya looked past him at the microphone. “I have evidence that the school’s account of Aaron Bell’s injury was false.” The hall went silent in stages. First the pupils. Then the parents. Then the staff. Mr Venn turned away. Martin said, “Maya, you need to stop.” “No.” It was the clearest word she had spoken all week. Finn emerged from beneath the table and clicked the laptop. The projector screen behind the stage changed. A photocopy appeared. **INJURY SUSTAINED DURING STUDENT LEADERSHIP ACTIVITY.** Beneath it, the sentence: **PUPIL ATTEMPTED TO CLIMB STORAGE PLATFORM WITHOUT INSTRUCTION.** A murmur passed through the hall. Maya held up the original copy. “This report says Aaron was alone. He wasn’t. The school knew he wasn’t.” Martin moved towards the laptop. Finn blocked him. It was a ridiculous arrangement: a headteacher facing a sixteen-year-old boy with one untied shoe and a stolen laptop. “Play the audio,” Maya said. Finn clicked. The hall filled with shouting. The recording was damaged by static, but Aaron’s voice was clear. “I’m not doing it again.” Then Mr Venn: “You don’t get to decide which instructions apply to you.” A crash. Leah’s voice: “Don’t touch him. Call someone.” Then another voice, quieter but unmistakable: “Turn that off.” The audio ended. Nobody moved. Martin looked at the audience. “This recording has been taken out of context.” Elsie came onto the stage. “No,” she said. Her father turned. Elsie looked at him, and Maya saw how hard it was for her to keep standing. “I was there,” Elsie said. “Aaron was told to repeat the activity. He said it wasn’t safe. Mr Venn told him to do it anyway.” Mr Venn shook his head. “Elsie—” “I ran when he fell.” The hall seemed to tilt. “I heard him calling for help, and I ran. Later, Dad told me to say I hadn’t been there.” Martin’s face had gone white. “That is not what happened.” “It is.” “Maya,” he said, turning to her, “you cannot allow this to become a spectacle.” Maya thought of Aaron sitting at home, away from the hall, away from the projector. “We’re not showing the footage,” she said. Leah had the memory card in her hand. Her thumb rested on the edge. For one second, Maya thought she would play it anyway. Leah looked at the audience. At Aaron’s parents. At Maya. Then she put the card away. Mr Venn took a step towards her. “The original recording contains private material,” Maya said. “We will give it to the safeguarding officer and the police. Aaron does not want it shown here.” A woman in the front row stood. “Where is Aaron?” Maya faced her. “At home.” “Why isn’t he here to explain himself?” The question was so ordinary that it felt monstrous. “Because he doesn’t owe you a performance,” Maya said. The woman sat down. At the back of the hall, a door opened. The school safeguarding lead entered with two police officers. Finn had sent them the evidence before the assembly began, but Maya had not known whether they would come. Martin looked from them to Elsie. “Everyone needs to remain calm,” he said. A pupil near the refreshments started clapping. Nobody joined in. The projector flickered. The photocopy disappeared, replaced by a blue screen. Then, for half a second, the old file name appeared. **VID_1710.** Martin reached for the microphone. “This has been an unfortunate misunderstanding.” Maya looked at the stage, the banner, the parents, the red balloons trembling in the heat. “Then explain it,” she said. This time, she was not speaking to Martin. The safeguarding lead came forward. Mr Venn sat down. Elsie removed her prefect badge and placed it on the floor. Leah stayed by the door. The hall lights came up too brightly, revealing every stain, every tired face, every person who had believed the official version because it had been easier. Maya looked at Aaron’s empty seat. For the first time, it did not look like an absence. It looked like a choice.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of a difficult task: a public confrontation scene that must be emotionally resonant without becoming melodramatic, morally complex without being preachy, and darkly comic without undercutting the genuine stakes. Let me examine it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter opens with the microphone failure, which is an excellent choice — it's the kind of mundane institutional incompetence that grounds the scene in recognizable reality before the tension escalates. The line "We've already established a theme" from Finn is perfectly calibrated: funny, character-consistent, and not overplayed. The pacing is notably controlled. The chapter builds through accumulation of small, precise details — Aaron's parents in the front row, his mother holding her handbag with both hands, Aaron's empty seat — rather than through melodramatic escalation. These details do real emotional work without announcing themselves. The dialogue is largely excellent. Martin Ward's "This recording has been taken out of context" followed immediately by Elsie's "No" is a beautifully spare exchange. The woman in the front row asking "Why isn't he here to explain himself?" and Maya's response — "Because he doesn't owe you a performance" — is the chapter's best moment: it captures the story's central theme without being a thesis statement, and it emerges naturally from character. The decision to have Leah almost play the full recording and then not is handled with admirable restraint. The plan called for this as the crucial moral choice, and the execution delivers it without over-explaining it. "Then she put the card away" is exactly the right level of understatement. Elsie's confession is well-handled. "I heard him calling for help, and I ran" is direct and devastating. Her removing the prefect badge at the end is a strong visual gesture that doesn't feel forced. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and thoroughly. Nearly every beat from the plan is executed: the microphone problem (not in the plan but consistent with its spirit), Maya's interruption, the audio recording, Elsie's confession, Leah's restraint, the safeguarding officer's arrival, the clapping pupil who thinks it's part of the ceremony. The final image — Aaron's empty seat looking like "a choice" rather than an absence — is earned and resonant. It reframes the entire chapter's meaning without being heavy-handed. **Weaknesses:** The chapter occasionally tips toward the slightly too-neat. The sequence of events — Maya stands, Finn plays the audio, Elsie appears, the safeguarding officer arrives — is almost too perfectly choreographed. Real confrontations are messier. The plan acknowledged this risk ("The audience reacts unevenly. Some parents understand; some complain about the disruption; one pupil keeps clapping because they think it is part of the ceremony"), and while the clapping pupil appears, the unevenness of the audience reaction is somewhat underplayed. "The hall seemed to tilt" is the chapter's weakest line — a slightly clichéd physical metaphor for emotional shock that doesn't quite fit the otherwise grounded prose style. The safeguarding lead arriving with police officers is a little convenient, and the chapter acknowledges this ("Finn had sent them the evidence before the assembly began, but Maya had not known whether they would come") — but this acknowledgment feels slightly like the author covering their tracks rather than genuinely earning the arrival. Martin's characterization is consistent with his profile — the warm public manner, the disappearing smile — but he becomes somewhat flatter in this chapter than his profile suggests. His "This has been an unfortunate misunderstanding" is good, but he doesn't quite get the moment of genuine self-belief that makes him more than a bureaucratic villain. The humour is present but lighter than in earlier chapters by design — this is appropriate for the climax — though the plan called for more comic texture (parents in wet coats, uncomfortable formal clothes, staff behaving as if nothing has happened). Some of this is present but could be richer. The line "It was a ridiculous arrangement: a headteacher facing a sixteen-year-old boy with one untied shoe and a stolen laptop" is good — it captures the absurdity without deflating the tension. Overall, this is a chapter that executes its plan with skill, maintains tonal consistency, and delivers its emotional and thematic payoffs without overreaching. The prose is clean and controlled. The characters behave consistently with their profiles. The moral complexity is preserved. Minor weaknesses in convenience and one or two slightly flat moments prevent it from being exceptional, but it is genuinely accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight: After The school closed for one day. Not because anybody had died, as Finn pointed out, but because the headteacher had resigned “with immediate effect,” three members of staff were being interviewed by the police, and nobody could agree who had the keys to the science block. On the second day, school reopened. The first message in the Year Eleven group chat was: **OLLIE:** are we still doing the maths test The second was: **PRIYA:** obviously The third was: **FINN:** society has collapsed Maya muted the chat. The external investigation began in a room that smelled of old carpet and peppermint tea. Maya gave a statement twice because the first time she kept saying “sort of” whenever she meant “yes.” Finn gave his statement in one long rush and had to be asked to start again. Elsie sat for nearly three hours without looking at her phone. Leah was interviewed separately. She admitted to entering the science block, copying the first-aid report, threatening Mr Venn, and hiding the recording. She did not admit to being a whistle-blower. She said she had never liked the word. “It makes people sound noble,” she told Maya afterwards. “I was mostly furious.” The journalist published the story carefully. It mentioned the altered report, the leadership event, and the recording. It did not publish Aaron’s injury footage. The comments beneath the article were less careful. Some people said the school had failed Aaron. Some said teenagers were too easily offended. One person claimed to have attended the event and said nothing like that had happened, despite being in Year Eight at the time. Aaron’s mother asked everyone to stop sharing the article. Maya did not know whether the request would work. Mr Venn was suspended while the investigation continued. Martin Ward was placed on leave. The school sent an email to parents saying it took “all concerns extremely seriously,” which Finn read aloud in a solemn voice until Maya threw a cushion at him. Elsie moved in with her aunt for a while. Her father called every evening. Sometimes she answered. Sometimes she watched the phone ring until it stopped. “He keeps saying he was trying to protect me,” she told Maya one lunchtime. “Was he?” Elsie peeled the label from a bottle of water. “He was trying to protect the version of me he could live with.” Maya did not know what to say to that. They were sitting behind the sports hall, where the rain had left the benches wet. Elsie had removed her prefect badge. Without it, she looked younger and less certain of where to put her hands. “Are you going to keep being friends with Leah?” she asked. Maya looked towards the fence. “I don’t know.” “Do you want to?” “I don’t know.” Elsie nodded. “That seems to be the official answer to everything.” “It’s the only honest one.” They sat quietly. Finn arrived carrying three hot chocolates and a packet of crisps. “One of these has no cream,” he said. “I remembered you don’t like it.” “I don’t like marshmallows.” “I knew that.” “You put marshmallows on it.” “I knew that too.” He handed her the cup. Maya took it. Finn sat down. For once, he did not make a joke. “I’m sorry,” he said. Maya looked at him. “For sending the recording.” “You already said that.” “I said it in a message. Messages don’t count as much.” “They count.” “Not if you send them at three in the morning with a frog emoji.” Maya stared into the hot chocolate. A marshmallow had dissolved into a pale, sticky island. “You were scared,” she said. “I was.” “That doesn’t mean you were right.” “I know.” He picked at the crisp packet. “I wanted to do something before everyone changed their minds.” Maya thought about that. It was not a good reason. It was a human one. “I know,” she said. They did not hug. Finn would have made it strange. Instead, he pushed the crisps towards her and took the empty seat beside Elsie. Leah was waiting outside the school gates that afternoon. She wore her red-tipped hair loose and carried no bag. The rain had stopped, but the pavement still shone. Maya nearly walked past her. Leah looked at the school behind them. “They’ve taken down the banner,” she said. “They’ve replaced it with a notice about revised entry procedures.” “Thrilling.” Maya stopped. For several seconds, neither of them spoke. Leah looked thinner than she had at the bus station. Or perhaps less certain. Her hands were tucked into her sleeves. “I’m sorry,” Leah said. Maya waited. Leah exhaled. “I’m sorry I used you.” “That’s not all of it.” “I know.” “Say the rest.” Leah looked away. “I’m sorry I decided you had to fix what happened because I was angry that you didn’t fix it before.” Maya swallowed. “I’m sorry I made Aaron’s life into evidence,” Leah continued. “I thought if people saw everything, they’d understand. I didn’t think about what it would mean if they understood the wrong part.” The apology was not perfect. It was not shaped like one of the messages Leah wrote and deleted. It was awkward and incomplete. That made Maya believe it. “I’m sorry I didn’t believe you,” Maya said. Leah’s eyes flicked back to her. “I did believe you,” Maya continued. “I just didn’t want to be involved.” “That’s worse.” “Yes.” Leah nodded. A bus turned the corner, spraying water over the kerb. “Are we friends?” Leah asked. Maya watched the bus pass. “I don’t know.” Leah gave a small laugh. “Official answer.” “Honest answer.” “Can I walk with you?” Maya considered saying no. She considered saying yes in a way that sounded like forgiveness. Instead she said, “To the bus stop.” They walked side by side. Neither of them spoke much. Leah complained about the pavement. Maya told her she was impossible. Leah said that was not an apology. Maya said it was not meant to be. At the bus stop, Maya’s phone buzzed. A message from Leah. **🩸** Maya looked at it. One blood drop had meant *annoyed*. Two had meant *someone is lying*. Three had meant *this is serious*. The code had never covered what came after. Leah watched her face. “What does it mean?” she asked. Maya could have made a joke. She could have invented something clever. She could have asked whether Leah was being dramatic. Instead, she pressed CALL. Leah’s phone rang in her hand. She answered. Neither of them spoke. The bus arrived. People got off. People got on. The driver glanced at them, waiting. Maya stayed where she was. After a moment, Leah sat beside her on the bench. The line remained open between them, carrying only the sound of breathing and traffic and the faint hiss of the bus doors. Maya looked at the red notification on her screen. She did not know what would happen next. For once, she did not pretend that knowing was the same as being safe.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Eight is the concluding chapter of a darkly comic British YA story, and it must accomplish several difficult things simultaneously: provide emotional resolution without false tidiness, maintain the story's dry comic register, honor the moral complexity established throughout, and deliver a final image that earns its restraint. On most of these counts, the chapter succeeds with considerable skill. The opening is immediately effective. The school reopening after one day, not because anyone died but because of key logistics, is exactly the kind of institutional absurdity the plan promised. The group chat exchange (Ollie asking about the maths test, Priya confirming, Finn declaring societal collapse) is genuinely funny and tonally precise. It captures how ordinary life reasserts itself around crisis without comment, which is one of the story's central preoccupations. Maya muting the chat is a small, perfect gesture. The chapter follows the plan closely. The investigation's aftermath is handled with appropriate brevity and specificity: the statements, the journalist's careful article, the careless comments beneath it, Aaron's mother's request, the suspensions. The plan called for "brief and specific rather than neatly summarised," and the chapter delivers this. The detail that one commenter claimed to have attended the event despite being in Year Eight at the time is a sharp, economical piece of satire about online discourse. Elsie's scene behind the sports hall is one of the chapter's strongest passages. Her line—"He was trying to protect the version of me he could live with"—is genuinely good: it's the kind of insight that feels earned rather than imposed, and it characterizes Martin Ward efficiently without requiring his presence. The detail of Elsie removing her prefect badge and looking younger without it is well-observed showing rather than telling. The Finn reconciliation scene works well. The marshmallow/cream confusion is a small, believable piece of comic business that doesn't overstay its welcome. His apology—delivered in person because "messages don't count as much" and then immediately undermined by the frog emoji admission—is character-consistent and emotionally honest. Maya's response ("You were scared. That doesn't mean you were right. I know.") is exactly the kind of incomplete forgiveness the plan called for. The line "It was not a good reason. It was a human one" is perhaps the chapter's best single sentence: it's not trying too hard, it lands cleanly, and it does real thematic work. The Leah reconciliation is handled with appropriate awkwardness. The apology is "not perfect" and "not shaped like one of the messages Leah wrote and deleted"—the chapter tells us this, which is a mild instance of tell-don't-show, but it's brief and the dialogue itself demonstrates the imperfection convincingly. Leah's apology about making "Aaron's life into evidence" and thinking "if people saw everything, they'd understand" is thematically precise. Maya's counter-admission—"I did believe you. I just didn't want to be involved. That's worse."—is the moral crux of the whole story, delivered without fanfare. The ending with the single blood emoji and the phone call is exactly what the plan specified, and it's executed with real delicacy. The decision not to decode the emoji, to call instead, and then to sit in silence on the bench while the bus comes and goes—this is genuinely moving and earns its restraint. The final line, "She did not know what would happen next. For once, she did not pretend that knowing was the same as being safe," is a strong closing thought, though "pretend that knowing was the same as being safe" is slightly over-constructed compared to the chapter's otherwise clean prose. The chapter is largely free of purple prose and forced metaphor. The "pale, sticky island" of dissolved marshmallow is a small, apt image rather than a strained one. The rain-shining pavement is conventional but not offensive. The writing is consistently clean and controlled. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: some of the emotional beats are telegraphed rather than discovered. The structure of apology-counter-apology between Maya and Leah is a little schematic, moving through its points with almost procedural efficiency. The plan called for an ending that is "morally and emotionally incomplete," and while the chapter achieves this in its final image, the preceding reconciliation scene is perhaps slightly too tidy in its articulation of what went wrong. Real sixteen-year-olds rarely achieve this level of precise mutual acknowledgment, even in the plan's intended register. The chapter is also slightly short on the comic texture that distinguishes the earlier chapters. The group chat opening is excellent, and the Finn scene has good comic business, but the Elsie scene and the Leah scene are played almost entirely straight. This isn't a failure—the chapter is a conclusion and earns its gravity—but the plan's promise of "ordinary school life continues around a serious crisis" is only partially fulfilled here. Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Maya's stillness, her precision with words, her inability to offer easy forgiveness—all present. Finn's practical affection (the hot chocolates, the remembered marshmallow preference, the in-person apology) is exactly right. Elsie's controlled composure cracking into something younger is well-handled. Leah's theatrical quality is present but subdued, which feels appropriate for someone who has been through what she has. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and specifically. Nearly every planned beat is present: the investigation, the journalist's article, Aaron's family's request for privacy, Finn's apology, Elsie's situation with her father, the Maya-Leah meeting, the walk to the bus stop, the single emoji, the phone call, the open line. The final image matches the plan's description almost exactly. Overall, this is a strong, controlled piece of writing that successfully concludes a complex story without cheating on its moral ambiguity. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional tell-don't-show, a reconciliation scene that is slightly too articulate, and a final sentence that reaches slightly too hard. Its strengths—tonal consistency, character fidelity, earned restraint, and genuine emotional intelligence—are considerable. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction. The model has produced something genuinely impressive across eight chapters, maintaining consistent voice, character, and thematic coherence throughout. Let me examine it critically. **Voice and Style** The narrative voice is the story's greatest strength. It achieves something genuinely difficult: a dry, observational British wit that never tips into parody or condescension. Lines like "The radiator continued making its small, bureaucratic clicks" and "The microphone produced a deep, explosive hum. Parents flinched. A toddler began to cry. 'Excellent,' Finn whispered from behind the curtain. 'We've already established a theme.'" demonstrate real comic timing. The humor consistently earns its place rather than being decorative. The Finn character is particularly well-handled as comic relief. His jokes land because they're character-specific rather than generic quips. "It's a medical condition" (about sweating), the FORENSICS keyring, the cereal bars—these accumulate into a coherent personality. Crucially, the model knows when to silence him: "For once, he did not make a joke." **Character** Maya is a well-drawn protagonist. Her central flaw—knowing something is wrong and choosing not to act—is established early and paid off throughout. The line "I did believe you. I just didn't want to be involved" is the emotional core of the piece and it lands because it's been earned across seven chapters. Leah is more complex than she initially appears. The model resists making her purely sympathetic; she's manipulative, controlling, and uses people even when she's right. "I'm sorry I decided you had to fix what happened because I was angry that you didn't fix it before" is a genuinely nuanced apology. Elsie is the most interesting secondary character—a girl complicit in her father's cover-up not out of malice but out of shame and fear. Her arc from antagonist to reluctant ally to someone who removes her prefect badge is handled with restraint. Mr. Venn is the weakest character—he's functional but not particularly dimensional. The model gives him one humanizing moment ("He looked less like a sinister teacher and more like a man who had been awake too long") but doesn't develop it. **Plot and Pacing** The mystery structure works well. The blood emoji code is an elegant device that grounds the supernatural-seeming opening in something mundane and emotionally resonant. The escalation from emoji to Room 3 to bus station to awards evening is well-paced. However, there are some structural issues. The memory card subplot becomes slightly convoluted—it appears, disappears, reappears, and its exact contents shift in ways that require careful tracking. The "you're looking in the wrong place" message with four blood drops in Chapter 3 is never fully resolved; it seems to promise a revelation that doesn't quite materialize. The awards evening climax is satisfying but slightly too neat. The safeguarding lead and police arriving because Finn sent evidence beforehand is a convenient resolution. The model acknowledges this somewhat ("Maya had not known whether they would come") but doesn't fully earn the timing. **Dialogue** The dialogue is consistently strong. The banter between Maya and Finn feels natural and specific. The confrontation scenes have genuine tension. The apology scene in Chapter 8 is particularly well-written—awkward, incomplete, and therefore believable. One weakness: Mr. Venn's dialogue in the bus station scene is slightly too articulate in his villainy. "You're using him. You're all using him" is a good point, but it's delivered too cleanly for a man who's been caught. **Thematic Coherence** The story is genuinely about something: institutional cover-ups, the difference between believing someone and acting on that belief, and the ethics of using someone's trauma as evidence. The Aaron subplot is handled with real care—the model consistently returns to the question of what Aaron wants, and the resolution (not showing the footage) is earned rather than convenient. **Forced Poetry and Purple Prose** The metaphors are generally restrained and appropriate. "The accusation landed because it had already been waiting inside her" is perhaps the most overtly literary line, and it works. The model avoids the trap of over-poeticizing. There are occasional moments where the observational humor tips into slightly self-conscious territory ("the washing machine thumped as if attempting to escape"), but these are minor. **Unearned Transformations** The character arcs are mostly earned. Elsie's transformation is the most gradual and therefore most convincing. Maya's final decision to stay at the bus stop rather than take the bus is a small but meaningful gesture. The friendship between Maya and Leah is left genuinely unresolved, which is the right choice. **Consistency** The piece maintains remarkable consistency across eight chapters. Character voices don't drift. The blood emoji code is established and used coherently. The timeline is clear. This is genuinely difficult to achieve in a long multi-chapter piece. **Weaknesses** The main weaknesses are: Mr. Venn is underdeveloped; the "wrong place" message is a slight red herring; the police arrival is convenient; and some of the comic observations in the early chapters (the googly eye on the fire extinguisher, the IS LEAH DEAD poll) are slightly more whimsical than the darker material that follows, creating a minor tonal inconsistency that the story mostly but not entirely resolves. The ending is strong—the open phone call, the unresolved friendship, the acknowledgment that "knowing was not the same as being safe"—without being falsely redemptive. Overall, this is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA fiction that earns its comedy and its darkness in roughly equal measure. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Pacing: 15 World Building: 14 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 78.0/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 76.5 | Final: 74.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Core Premise Possibilities The story begins aboard a small spacecraft, likely a battered freighter or independent salvage vessel, after the crew discovers a sealed crate drifting in space or abandoned on a frontier world. Someone says: > “Captain, something’s... moving in that crate we picked up.” The crate can contain: - A living alien organism that is intelligent, dangerous, or both. - A child or prisoner from a vanished colony. - A dormant biological weapon. - A shape-shifting creature that imitates crew members. - A relic containing a miniature ecosystem. - A dead outlaw whose body is being reanimated by something inside. - A valuable alien egg. - A human being altered by an experimental corporation. - A ghostly or supernatural entity bound inside a cargo container. - A colony of tiny creatures that can collectively control machinery. - A map or artifact that is “moving” because it is alive. The strongest directions for a Space Western would combine frontier lawlessness, moral ambiguity, an isolated crew, and a choice between profit and mercy. ## Possible Main Character The captain could be: - **Mara Venn**, a disgraced former ranger who now runs salvage jobs. She claims to care only about keeping her ship flying, but still follows an outdated frontier code. - **Elias Rusk**, a charming smuggler and former military officer who is wanted in several systems. - **Captain Jo Calder**, a hard-bitten woman who inherited a ship and a debt from her dead father. - **Silas Vale**, a former corporate security captain who deserted after refusing an order to destroy a colony. - **Nia Ortega**, a young captain trying to prove herself while commanding an older, more experienced crew. A morally conflicted captain would suit the prompt best. The captain should initially see the crate as cargo or salvage, then gradually realize that accepting payment for it would make them complicit in something terrible. ## Possible Crew A small cast would keep the eight-chapter story focused: - **Captain Mara Venn** — practical, guarded, secretly compassionate. - **Tom “Tinker” Bell** — mechanic and comic relief, superstitious about abandoned cargo. - **Dr. Sayeed Kline** — ship’s medic, empathetic and scientifically curious. - **Rook Alvarez** — pilot and former bounty hunter, distrustful of anything alien. - **Moss** — an old mining robot with a dry voice and surprisingly strong moral instincts. - **The crate’s occupant** — perhaps a young alien called Iri, whose movements and communication are initially frightening. An antagonist could be: - **Marshal Dax Harrow**, a company-backed lawman who arrives to reclaim the crate. - **The Helix Combine**, a corporation claiming legal ownership of the cargo. - **A bounty hunter crew**, led by Captain Vey, who wants the reward. - **The colony authorities**, who insist the creature is a dangerous contagion. - **A former crew member**, secretly transmitting the ship’s location. ## Space Western Elements The setting could include: - Dusty frontier planets with failing settlements. - Mining towns built inside asteroid caverns. - Company-owned moons where contracts replace laws. - Saloon stations orbiting red dwarf stars. - Outlaw radio channels and bounty notices. - Railgun duels between ships. - Old revolvers adapted into energy weapons. - Space horses or large alien pack animals. - Frontier marshals who work for whoever pays them. - Terraforming colonies abandoned after corporate collapse. - Religious or superstitious communities that interpret alien life as an omen. - Ship interiors resembling old ranch houses, bunkhouses, or battered stagecoaches. The story should feel less like polished military science fiction and more like a lonely frontier tale: small ships, scarce fuel, unreliable authorities, and people surviving between stars. ## Tone Possibilities - **Tense horror:** The crate contains something that slowly imitates the crew. - **Melancholy adventure:** The occupant is vulnerable, and the captain must deliver it to safety. - **Moral thriller:** Several factions want the crate, and no one agrees about what is inside. - **Dark comedy:** The crew discovers a surprisingly intelligent alien pet while being hunted by bounty hunters. - **Mythic western:** The occupant is an ancient being tied to a dying planet. - **Action-oriented space western:** The crate becomes the center of a chase across multiple systems. - **Quiet character drama:** The crate exposes the crew’s fears and gives them a reason to become a family. A combination of suspense, frontier action, and emotional tenderness would likely work best. The opening can be eerie, the middle increasingly dangerous, and the conclusion emotionally satisfying without becoming too sentimental. ## Possible Plot Direction One: The Alien Child The crate contains a young alien from a species believed to be extinct. A corporation placed the child in cryogenic transport after destroying its homeworld. The crate is marked as hazardous industrial waste. The crew discovers that: - The child is alive but weak. - It can communicate through images and dreams. - The corporation wants to study or weaponize its biology. - The child’s species may be able to terraform worlds. - The child is not merely a survivor; it carries a living seed or genetic memory capable of restoring its people’s home. The captain must decide whether to sell the child, hide it, or transport it to a distant refuge. ## Possible Plot Direction Two: The Living Weapon The crate contains a bioengineered predator created by the Helix Combine. It was meant to be used against rebellious mining colonies. The creature appears dangerous, but it has been deliberately tortured and conditioned. The crew initially fears it, especially after it escapes briefly and kills a shipboard animal or damages the engine. Gradually, they realize it understands language and is trying to communicate. The corporation’s hunters claim the creature is mindless. The captain learns that the corporation has falsified records to conceal its experiments. The climax could involve the creature choosing to protect the crew despite having the power to kill them. ## Possible Plot Direction Three: The Colony in a Box The crate contains a miniature artificial world. Inside is a colony of people preserved in suspended animation, shrunk or compressed by experimental technology. The movement comes from one person waking up. The corporation wants to reclaim the technology. The crew discovers the colony was abandoned because its inhabitants refused to become company property. This direction offers a strange, imaginative premise but may be difficult to explain clearly in only eight thousand words. ## Possible Plot Direction Four: The Shape-Shifter The crate contains an alien capable of perfectly mimicking human beings. It escapes and takes the form of one crew member. The question becomes: who is real? This creates strong suspense, but the story risks becoming familiar. It would work best if the creature is not malicious and is imitating people because it is terrified.
