He does not trust Mara immediately, but he updates his judgment as evidence changes. ## Personality and Idiosyncrasies - He keeps emergency snacks in his patrol bag. - He has a habit of narrating practical tasks under his breath. - He dislikes dramatic people. - He is embarrassed by how much he likes children’s cartoons. - He checks every door twice after a previous ambush. - He says “all right” when he means “I am frightened.” - He is more comfortable treating injuries than discussing his own. ## Flaws Davis follows procedure even when procedure is too slow. He initially hesitates because he believes the wrong report. He is reluctant to accuse other officers of corruption without proof. He becomes protective of Mara and the children before he has decided whether that is wise. ## Physicality Davis is broad, tired, and slightly overweight, with a shaved head and a stiff knee. He moves carefully but knows how to use his size. After being wounded, he becomes frustrated by his dependence on others. He tries to keep giving instructions even when he cannot stand. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Davis’s allegiance is to the people in front of him rather than to the prestige of the department. He wants to keep everyone alive and preserve enough evidence to make the arrest matter. He respects Mara’s judgment but refuses to romanticize her past. He becomes a stabilizing presence for Cal because he gives clear information without pretending everything is fine. ## Voice Samples > “I don’t know how many of them there are. I don’t know if your parents are alive. I do know the door behind me is locked, and that’s something.” > “You can distrust me after we stop the bleeding.” > Davis looked at Mara’s improvised barricade. “You do this often?” > “Only when people are rude.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One: Mr. Buttons Mara Vale arrived eleven minutes late in a car that made a noise like a spoon rattling in a washing machine. The Hart house had three stories, white stone, black windows, and a front door large enough to admit a horse. Mara parked at the curb facing outward. It was an old habit. Most habits had reasons, even the stupid ones. A man in a gray sweater opened the door before she reached it. “You’re Mara?” “Usually.” He looked past her at the car. “Is that safe?” “No.” The man did not laugh. Mara decided he was probably Daniel Hart. Behind him stood a woman with a phone in one hand and a folded dish towel in the other. She was pretty in a tired, expensive way. Her eyes kept moving toward the windows. “Elise,” she said. “Thank you for coming.” “You’re welcome.” “We’re only going to be gone a couple of hours.” Daniel said, “Maybe three.” Elise looked at him. “Two,” he corrected. Mara stepped inside. The house smelled like lemon cleaner, expensive wood, and something burning gently in the kitchen. “Emergency numbers are on the refrigerator,” Elise said. “Cal’s supposed to be in bed by nine-thirty. Poppy can stay up until ten, but not later, because she gets overtired.” “I don’t get overtired,” said a boy from the staircase. “You got overtired yesterday,” Elise called. “I was awake.” “That’s not the same thing.” The boy came down. He was eleven, thin, pale, and wearing a soccer jersey too large for him. His hair stuck up on one side. He looked at Mara’s shoes. “You’re late,” he said. “Good evening to you too.” “You said you were coming at six.” “It’s six eleven.” “That’s late.” “Then we agree.” A small girl appeared behind him carrying a stuffed rabbit by one ear. The rabbit had one plastic eye, a missing button on its vest, and a gray patch of tape across its stomach. “This is Mr. Buttons,” she said. Mara looked at the rabbit. “He seems to be having a difficult day.” “He doesn’t like being put down.” “Neither do I.” Poppy considered this. “Are you a babysitter?” “That’s the rumor.” Cal frowned. “Have you done this before?” “Babysitting?” “No. Being in a house where people might break in.” Daniel went still. Elise’s fingers tightened around the dish towel. Mara looked at Cal. “What makes you ask that?” “Your car.” “That is not an answer.” “It’s a car for leaving.” “Most cars are.” “Not that one.” Mara glanced through the open door at her battered blue sedan. “Your brother’s very observant,” she told Poppy. “He’s not my brother,” Poppy said. “He’s Cal.” “I’m her brother.” “You can be both.” Daniel cleared his throat. “The children know the rules.” “I have rules too,” Mara said. Cal crossed his arms. “What are they?” “No running near stairs. No opening doors for strangers. No throwing things at one another unless everybody agrees beforehand.” “Those are normal rules.” “I’m a normal person.” Nobody answered. Daniel began giving her instructions. Cal hated peas, Poppy was allergic to walnuts, the dog was at the kennel, the back door sometimes stuck, the upstairs bathroom faucet dripped, and the alarm system had been recently updated but was not to be touched. Mara listened while looking at the room. There were cameras in the corners, small black lenses mounted high against the ceiling. One watched the front hall. One watched the staircase. There was another outside, visible through the glass beside the door. The camera aimed at the driveway was pointed slightly too far left. It covered the street but not the parked cars. Daniel noticed her noticing. “Security,” he said. “From what?” “Nothing specific.” Elise looked toward the front window. Mara looked at the clock. “You two expecting someone?” “No,” Elise said. Daniel said, “Not tonight.” Elise turned to him again. He rubbed his mouth. “I mean, no one we know.” Poppy tugged Mara’s sleeve. “Mr. Buttons is hungry.” “Does he eat food?” “He likes toast.” “Then he and I have something in common.” In the kitchen, Mara found the burning smell came from a tray of bread. She removed it before the smoke alarm could begin its high, shrill sermon. Poppy sat Mr. Buttons at the table and gave him a corner of toast. Cal watched Mara put the tray in the sink. “You didn’t use the mitt,” he said. “I have hands.” “You can get burned.” “I’ve noticed.” “You’re supposed to use the mitt.” “You’re welcome to use it next time.” Cal studied her, disappointed that she had not been offended. Daniel checked his phone. The screen lit his face blue. Elise stepped into the kitchen beside him. They spoke quietly, too quietly for ordinary people and not quietly enough for Mara. “Do we still have time?” Elise asked. “We have to go.” “You said—” “I know what I said.” Their eyes met. Something passed between them that had nothing to do with babysitting. Mara set a plate of sandwiches on the table. “Everything all right?” she asked. “Fine,” Daniel said. Elise said, “Yes.” They had answered too quickly and in the wrong order. Mara had spent years around people who said fine when they meant run. Daniel took his coat from the chair. Elise crouched to kiss Poppy, then held her longer than necessary. “Bedtime,” she said. “I know.” “And don’t let anyone take Mr. Buttons.” Poppy hugged the rabbit. Elise looked up at Mara. For half a second, her face was naked with fear. Then it was gone. “Call if you need anything,” she said. “I charge for emergency consulting.” Daniel gave a short, uncertain laugh. Elise didn’t. At the door, Daniel turned back. “Mara.” She waited. “If someone comes by—” “Don’t open the door.” “Right.” “Was there more?” He looked at the children. “No.” It was not convincing. They left. Mara watched through the glass as they crossed the drive. Daniel looked toward the road. Elise looked back at the house. A dark sedan sat half a block away with its lights off. Mara memorized the shape, the plates, the driver’s side mirror. The Hart car pulled away. The sedan remained. “Your parents have interesting friends,” she said. Cal had gone to the window. “They’re not friends.” “How do you know?” “Friends come inside.” Mara closed the curtain. They ate sandwiches. Poppy fed Mr. Buttons crumbs. Cal asked Mara whether she had ever been arrested. “Why?” “You didn’t answer.” “Yes.” “For what?” “Being late.” “That’s not a crime.” “Depends who’s waiting.” At nine, Poppy watched cartoons with the rabbit tucked under her chin. Cal sat beside her, pretending to read while checking the dark window every few minutes. Mara washed three plates and one sticky glass. The house was very quiet. Too quiet, she thought. Not peaceful. Expensive houses made different noises: vents breathing, pipes ticking, machines cooling inside walls. This one had gone still. She dried her hands. Outside, the dark sedan was gone. The front lights flickered. Once. Twice. Then every light in the house went out. Poppy made a small sound. Cal stood up. From the coffee table, Mr. Buttons clicked on by himself. His damaged voice crackled through the dark. “Everybody stays where they belong.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Prose Style and Voice** The writing has a distinctive, clipped rhythm that suits the pulp thriller genre. Sentences are short and declarative, creating momentum. The voice is consistent and controlled. The opening line—"Mara Vale arrived eleven minutes late in a car that made a noise like a spoon rattling in a washing machine"—is specific, grounded, and character-revealing without being showy. The simile works because it's domestic and slightly absurd, which fits the tone. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchanges between Mara and Cal are genuinely sharp: "You're late." "Good evening to you too." This is economical and characterful. The banter doesn't feel forced. Poppy's lines ("He's not my brother. He's Cal.") are exactly right for a six-year-old—literal, slightly surreal, funny without being precious. Victor's absence is felt through the tension in Daniel and Elise's behavior. **Character Consistency** Mara is rendered faithfully to her profile: observant, dry, practical, not warm but not cold. Her noticing the camera angle, the dark sedan, the parents' tells—all consistent. Cal's suspicion and pattern-recognition come through clearly. Poppy's literalism and attachment to Mr. Buttons are well-handled. Daniel and Elise's nervous, slightly off-kilter behavior creates appropriate unease without over-explaining. **Dialogue Quality** The dialogue is largely excellent. The exchanges are specific and character-driven rather than expository. One minor weakness: "Being late" as Mara's answer to what she was arrested for is a bit too cute—it's the kind of deflection that works once but the chapter uses similar deflection patterns repeatedly, which slightly dulls the edge. However, this is a minor complaint. **Plot and Tension** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Mara arrives, meets the children, notices the parents' anxiety, observes the sedan, settles into a false calm, and then the power cuts out with Mr. Buttons delivering the chapter's closing line. The pacing is well-managed. The chapter earns its ending—the rabbit's phrase "Everybody stays where they belong" lands because it's been established naturally and the timing is precise. **Forced Poetry / Purple Prose** The prose is largely clean. There are a few moments where the writing reaches slightly: "pretty in a tired, expensive way" is a bit of a stock phrase. "Her face was naked with fear" is effective but borders on the familiar. The house description ("white stone, black windows") is functional. Overall, the writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. **Creativity and Surprise** The setup is genre-familiar—babysitter in danger, criminals coming for something hidden—but the execution has enough specificity to feel fresh. Cal's observation that Mara's car is "a car for leaving" is genuinely clever and reveals character while advancing plot. The Mr. Buttons payoff is well-constructed. The chapter doesn't reinvent the wheel but executes its genre with intelligence. **Tell vs. Show** The chapter is predominantly showing. The parents' fear is conveyed through behavior (Elise holding Poppy too long, Daniel's phone, their too-quick "fine" responses) rather than narration. Mara's competence is shown through her observations rather than stated. One slight tell: "It was not convincing" after Daniel's final exchange is unnecessary—the reader already knows this. **Earned Tone** The darkly comic tone is well-calibrated. The humor doesn't undercut the tension; it coexists with it. The sandwich-cutting, the burnt toast, the rabbit at the dinner table—these domestic details ground the danger effectively. **Weaknesses** The chapter's main weakness is that the witty deflection pattern (Mara giving a non-answer, the other person accepting it) is used perhaps one too many times. By the fourth or fifth instance, it becomes slightly mechanical. The chapter also doesn't quite establish Mara's interiority beyond her observational mode—we get her competence but not yet her vulnerability, though this is appropriate for a first chapter. The dark sedan is perhaps a touch obvious as a threat signal, but it's handled with enough restraint to work. Overall, this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that honors its plan, establishes its characters faithfully, and delivers on the genre's promise without condescending to it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two: The Wrong Uniform Mara did not move until she knew where the children were. “Poppy?” “Here.” “Cal?” “Here.” “Good. Stay on the couch.” “That’s not a plan,” Cal said. “It’s the beginning of one.” The television had gone black. Mr. Buttons sat on the coffee table with his one plastic eye reflecting the streetlights. His voice box clicked again, but this time nothing came out. Mara crossed the room without turning on her phone’s flashlight. Light made people visible. Darkness only made them guess. She drew the curtains, then crouched beside the front window. The street looked empty. That meant nothing. “Why did the lights go out?” Poppy whispered. “Could be the breaker.” “Is it the bad men?” “Maybe.” Cal looked at her. “You said not to lie.” “I said I wouldn’t lie to you. I didn’t say I’d answer every question.” “That’s almost the same thing.” “No. It’s the difference between a babysitter and a politician.” She moved toward the hall. The security panel beside the door showed a blank screen. Mara pressed the button. Nothing. She tried the landline in the kitchen. The phone had power. Good. She dialed emergency services. A woman answered on the third ring. “Emergency services. What is your location?” Mara gave the address. “Can you tell me what’s happening?” “Power outage. Possible intruders. Two children in the house.” “Are you armed?” Mara looked at the kitchen knives, the cast-iron pan, the heavy ceramic canister. “Not traditionally.” A pause. “Ma’am?” The call clicked. The line went dead. Mara held the receiver to her ear for another second, listening to nothing. “Was that the police?” Cal asked. “Someone.” The doorbell rang. Poppy grabbed Mr. Buttons. Cal’s shoulders came up around his ears. Mara walked to the entryway and looked through the narrow glass panel beside the door. A man stood on the porch in a police uniform. He was broad, pale, and wearing a rain jacket over his shirt. His hands were empty. His shoes were black and polished. “Police,” he called. “We received a distress call.” Mara kept her voice low. “Cal, what does a real police car sound like?” He frowned. “What?” “Think.” “Sirens?” “Anything else.” He listened. “No engine.” The man outside glanced toward the upstairs windows. Mara noticed the movement. It was not much. A real officer checking a house looked for signs of distress: movement, smoke, broken glass, a person signaling for help. This man looked for bedrooms. “Can you show me identification?” Mara asked. The officer lifted a badge toward the glass. He held it too close and at the wrong angle. Mara could not read it. “Open the door,” he said. “Why?” “Ma’am, there may be a dangerous person inside.” “Then you shouldn’t be standing outside.” The officer’s mouth tightened. Mara stepped back from the door. “Is your name on the badge?” she asked. “What?” “Your name. Is it on the badge?” “Open the door.” “That wasn’t an answer.” Behind Mara, Cal whispered, “The camera doesn’t work.” “Which one?” “Front hall. The little red light is off.” The man outside turned his head slightly. He had heard. Mara moved to the wall panel and pressed the intercom button. The system gave a soft electronic chime. “Officer,” she said through the speaker, “I’m calling the station to verify your identity.” “You already called.” “Then you won’t mind waiting.” His expression changed. Not fear. Calculation. He stepped off the porch. Mara watched him walk toward the driveway instead of the street. “Where’s he going?” Poppy asked. “Somewhere he thinks we won’t see him.” Mara took the children by the wrists and moved them toward the kitchen. Cal pulled back. “What are you doing?” “Getting you out of the front room.” “Where?” “Somewhere less obvious.” The back hallway led past the laundry room to the garage. Mara opened the interior door and stopped. The garage was dark. The car’s remote flashed once on the hook beside the door. The garage door began to rise. Mara had not touched it. She hit the wall switch. The door stopped halfway, leaving a black gap beneath it. A hand appeared under the opening. Mara shoved the children backward. The fingers caught the bottom edge and lifted. “Run upstairs,” Mara said. “Where?” “Your rooms.” “That’s not safe,” Cal said. “Neither is arguing.” The door rose another inch. A man’s face appeared in the gap. Mara grabbed the nearest object: a plastic bottle of laundry detergent. She waited until the man’s shoulder came through, then swung. The bottle struck the side of his head with a thick, satisfying smack. He fell against the garage floor. Mara kicked the door switch again. The garage door shuddered downward. The man rolled away just before it slammed onto the concrete. “Upstairs,” Mara said. They ran. Poppy dropped Mr. Buttons. She stopped. Mara caught her around the waist before she could turn back. “No.” “But—” “No.” “He’s scared.” “He’s a rabbit.” “He’s still scared.” Something struck the front window. Glass burst inward. Cal screamed. Mara pulled both children down as a bullet punched through the wall behind them. The sound was enormous. For one second, nobody moved. Then Mara shoved the children toward the stairs. “Go. Go now.” They scrambled upward. Mara followed, keeping low. At the landing, she looked back. The man from the garage was on his knees, one hand pressed to his head. Blood ran between his fingers. He reached for the interior door. Mara shut it and locked the deadbolt. The house alarm suddenly came alive. A siren wailed. The sound rolled through the rooms, sharp enough to hurt. The panel in the hall flashed red, then blue, then went blank again. “Your system is terrible,” Mara said. Cal was crouched beneath the upstairs console table. Poppy sat beside him with Mr. Buttons clutched to her chest. Mara reached for the rabbit. Poppy held on. “Where did you find him?” Mara asked. “He fell.” “I know. Before that.” “Mom gave him to me.” “Did she put anything inside him?” Poppy’s eyes widened. Cal looked at her. “What?” Mara asked. Poppy pressed the rabbit’s taped stomach. “Mom said he was keeping a secret.” The alarm cut off. In the sudden silence, someone tried the front door. The handle turned once. Twice. Then the lock began to splinter. Mara took the children by the shoulders and moved them toward the hallway closet. Behind them, a voice called from downstairs. “Police! We’re coming in!” Cal leaned close to Mara. “That’s not the same man.” “How do you know?” “Different shoes.” The front door burst open. Mara opened the closet and pushed the children inside. “Stay here,” she whispered. “No matter what you hear.” “What about you?” Poppy asked. Mara looked down the stairs. A second man entered the house, carrying a handgun and wearing a clean police uniform. Behind him, the first man dragged himself through the broken doorway. The second man lifted his face toward the stairs. “Where is the rabbit?” he called. Mara closed the closet door. “Now,” she whispered, “we start being rude.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Two of "The Wrong Babysitter" is a tightly constructed action chapter that largely delivers on its plan while maintaining the voice and character dynamics established in the profiles. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange between Mara and Cal is consistently sharp and character-consistent: "That's not a plan." / "It's the beginning of one." / "That's not an answer." These lines feel earned rather than performed. The banter never tips into quippiness that undercuts tension. Cal's observation about the shoes ("Different shoes") is a perfect payoff of his established character trait of watching people's feet rather than their eyes, and it's delivered with economy. Mara's voice is well-maintained throughout. Her practical, observational mode comes through in small details: noticing the man looks at bedrooms rather than signs of distress, the intercom gambit, the detergent bottle. She doesn't announce her competence; she demonstrates it. The line "Not traditionally" when asked if she's armed is exactly the dry humor the profile calls for. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The fake officer, the divided attention, the laundry room fight (relocated to the garage but functionally equivalent), the broken nose (here a detergent bottle to the head), the locked attacker, the shot through the window, the children upstairs, and Cal's revelation about Mr. Buttons all occur as planned. The chapter plan called for Mara to win "through leverage and surprise, not superior strength," and the detergent bottle swing accomplishes this without making her superhuman. The pacing is excellent. Short sentences during action sequences create genuine momentum. The chapter breathes appropriately—there are moments of stillness (listening for the engine, the intercom gambit) before violence erupts. The silence after the alarm cuts off is well-deployed. Poppy's characterization is strong. Her insistence on retrieving Mr. Buttons mid-crisis, her literal attachment to the rabbit's feelings ("He's still scared"), and her revelation that "Mom said he was keeping a secret" all feel true to her profile. The moment where she drops the rabbit and Mara physically prevents her from turning back is both tense and emotionally resonant. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified that Mara "breaks his nose with a detergent bottle, locks him in a utility closet." The chapter has her strike him with the bottle but then the garage door comes down and he rolls away—he's not locked in a closet. This is a meaningful deviation because the plan's next beat (the attacker in the closet telling Mara they're looking for "the toy") is set up by that containment. The chapter ends without this crucial information exchange, which means Chapter Three will need to handle it differently. This is a structural gap. The chapter plan also specified that "one attacker gets inside and Mara has to fight him in the laundry room." The fight is relocated to the garage, which is fine, but the plan's specificity about the laundry room was presumably chosen for domestic-space irony. The garage is less interesting as a setting. The line "Now we start being rude" as a closing line is the chapter's weakest moment. It's the kind of badass declaration the writing plan explicitly said to avoid: "Mara should rarely announce that she is dangerous." It's a slight but real tonal misstep—it tips toward genre cliché precisely where the story has been carefully avoiding it. The phone call to emergency services is handled efficiently, but the "click and goes dead" beat is a familiar thriller trope. The plan acknowledged this risk by noting it "suggests interference without establishing that every authority is corrupt," but the execution doesn't quite distinguish itself from countless similar scenes. The observation "Light made people visible. Darkness only made them guess" is a competent line but slightly over-explains what the action already shows. It's a minor tell-don't-show issue. The chapter is coherent and internally consistent. The security system's unreliability is established without being magical. The children behave like children—inconvenient, frightened, and occasionally observant in ways that matter. The chapter earns its darkness through specificity rather than melodrama. Overall, this is confident, professional pulp writing that honors its plan and characters. The closing line is the most significant flaw, and the structural deviation from the plan (no locked attacker, no "looking for the toy" revelation) creates a gap. But the chapter succeeds at its primary job: establishing threat, demonstrating Mara's competence through action rather than declaration, and keeping the children human rather than convenient. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: No One Is Coming Yet Mara crouched in the hallway outside the closet and listened. The men downstairs were not whispering. Professionals whispered. These men spoke in low, irritated voices, already annoyed that the house had not surrendered itself. “Check the bedrooms.” “Upstairs first?” “She’s got the kids up there.” Mara eased the closet door open. Cal’s eyes were wide in the dark. Poppy held Mr. Buttons against her chest, his one eye pressed into her pajama sleeve. “Everybody out,” Mara whispered. Cal shook his head. “You said stay.” “I changed my mind.” “You said no matter what.” “I was wrong.” He studied her, startled. Mara pointed toward the far end of the hall. “There’s a service passage behind the linen cabinet. Does it lead anywhere useful?” Cal hesitated. “Cal.” “It leads to storage.” “Outside?” “No.” “Then it’s useful.” He crawled out. Poppy followed, dragging the rabbit by its remaining ear. Mara moved the linen cabinet aside. It scraped the floor loudly. Downstairs, someone shouted. “Up!” Mara shoved the cabinet farther. A narrow black opening appeared behind it. “Go.” Cal squeezed through. Poppy followed. Mara brought up the rear and pulled the cabinet back into place. The passage smelled of dust and old paint. It was barely wide enough for shoulders. The children crawled ahead, Mr. Buttons bumping against the floor. “Quietly,” Mara whispered. Poppy’s voice came back through the dark. “I am quiet.” “You’re talking.” “I’m quiet talking.” Mara glanced behind them. A narrow strip of light showed beneath the cabinet. Footsteps reached the landing. She pushed forward. The passage opened into a storage room above the garage. Boxes filled the walls. Christmas decorations, old clothes, framed photographs, a broken exercise machine nobody had thrown away because it had been expensive. Cal pointed to the camera monitor on a shelf. “It’s still on.” The screen displayed the upstairs hallway. Mara watched a man move into view. He wore a police shirt but no body armor. He checked the bedroom doors with the confidence of someone who had studied the house. “Can you use it?” she asked. Cal nodded. “Do you know where the cameras are?” “All of them.” “Can you see downstairs?” “Not from this one.” “Can you switch feeds?” “Yes.” “Do it.” Cal sat in front of the monitor. His fingers moved over the controls. The image changed. Front hall. Kitchen. Garage. The man from the broken door stood beneath the camera, blood drying on his cheek. He had found a handgun and was speaking into a radio. “Second floor clear except the west bedroom.” Mara looked at Cal. “There are no cameras in the west bedroom,” he whispered. “Why did he say that?” “He wants the other guy to think he checked it.” Mara watched the feed. The second man entered the kitchen and opened drawers. “They’re looking for something,” Cal said. “Your mother’s rabbit.” “You think it has something inside?” “Do you?” Cal looked at Poppy. Poppy looked at Mr. Buttons. “He keeps secrets,” she said. Mara took the rabbit gently. Poppy resisted at first, then let go. The stitching on the stomach was newer than the rest of the toy. Cheap black thread crossed the gray tape in uneven loops. Mara slipped a fingernail beneath the seam. A voice sounded from the hall. “Poppy?” It was Elise. Poppy sat up. “Mom?” Mara covered her mouth. The voice came again, softer. “Sweetheart, it’s Mommy. Come out.” Poppy’s eyes filled. Cal shook his head violently. Mara listened. The voice was wrong. Not the words. The rhythm. Elise Hart had a habit of swallowing the ends of sentences, as though she were already moving on to the next thought. This voice was too smooth. A recording. Mara pressed one finger to her lips. The voice changed. “Poppy, I know you’re in there.” Cal whispered, “She doesn’t know.” “No.” “How do you know?” “She called you Poppy.” “That’s my name.” “She calls you Pop when she’s scared.” Cal stared at her. The voice stopped. A floorboard creaked outside the storage room. Mara put the rabbit against her chest and positioned herself beside the door. She found a metal curtain rod leaning against a box. Not ideal. Better than nothing. The handle turned. The door opened three inches. Mara swung. The rod struck a wrist. The man outside cursed and fired. The shot went into the ceiling. Mara slammed the door into him, then kicked his knee. He stumbled backward. She pulled the door shut and dropped the latch. He hit the other side with his shoulder. The frame shuddered. “Can’t hold it,” Mara said. Cal pointed to a second door at the back of the room. “That goes to the garage.” “Can it lock?” “No.” “Then we’ll make it lock.” They moved through the door into the garage storage area. The wounded man was gone. A dark smear of blood marked the concrete. The garage door stood open six inches. Mara ran to the interior control and hit the button. Nothing happened. “Power’s out,” Cal said. “Of course it is.” Poppy pointed beneath a workbench. “There’s a red handle.” Mara dropped to her knees and pulled it. The garage door released with a loud mechanical clatter and began to descend. Someone outside fired through the gap. Mara shoved the children behind the car. The door slammed shut. For three seconds, there was only breathing. Then somebody began pounding on the metal. Mara took the flashlight from a hook and swept the beam across the garage. The camera above the workbench blinked red. The monitor inside the storage room showed the same scene a few seconds late. Cal watched the screen. “They’re coming through the side door.” Mara turned. The side door opened. A man stepped inside. Not in a police uniform. He wore a dark jacket, gloves, and a wool cap. He was older than the others, perhaps forty-five, with a broken-looking nose and a pistol held close to his hip. He saw Mara. She saw him recognize her. His expression shifted from irritation to surprise. “Vale,” he said. Mara did not move. The man’s eyes dropped to the rabbit. “You’re supposed to be dead.” “People say that when they want something.” He lifted the pistol. Mara threw the flashlight. It struck his face. He fired into the ceiling. Mara crossed the space between them, drove her shoulder into his chest, and caught his wrist against the doorframe. He was stronger than she expected. He slammed her into the wall. Pain flashed through her ribs. Cal shouted, “Mara!” The man turned his head. Mara used the distraction. She twisted his wrist, brought her knee up, and drove the heel of her hand beneath his jaw. His gun fell. He went down hard. Mara kicked the weapon beneath the car. The man groaned and tried to rise. “You know Victor?” Mara asked. He spat blood onto the concrete. “You know what he’ll do when he gets here?” The man looked toward the children. “He said you were dead.” “Victor says a lot.” “He said you took the money.” Mara grabbed his collar. “What did he tell you to find?” The man’s gaze fixed on Mr. Buttons. “The toy.” Poppy stepped closer, clutching the rabbit. Mara saw the fresh stitching again. She took the rabbit from her. “I’m going to open him,” she said. Poppy’s lower lip trembled. Mara found a small pair of scissors on the workbench and cut the thread. Inside the rabbit’s torn lining was a black memory card. Poppy stared at the hole in the fabric. “Did you hurt him?” she asked. Mara looked at the little rabbit, then at the men outside the garage beginning to pound on the door. “No,” she said. “But I think someone did.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft in executing a tense, contained thriller sequence. The writing is economical and purposeful, with a strong command of pacing that suits the pulp genre. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is notably strong throughout. The exchange between Mara and Cal about changing her mind ("You said no matter what." / "I was wrong." / He studied her, startled.) efficiently establishes their dynamic while revealing character. Cal's surprise at an adult admitting error is communicated without being stated. Poppy's "I am quiet" / "I'm quiet talking" exchange is genuinely funny and true to a six-year-old's logic without being precious or forced. The chapter earns its lightness through specificity. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The service passage, the storage room, the camera feed with the delay, the discovery of the memory card, and the final line about Mr. Buttons all land as planned. The frightened young attacker from the closet (Niko) is notably absent from this chapter, which is a slight deviation—the plan indicated he would corner Mara and recognize her from the old job. Instead, it's the older operative who recognizes her. This is actually a reasonable adjustment that doesn't damage the chapter, but it's worth noting. Character consistency is excellent. Cal's pattern-recognition ("She calls you Pop when she's scared"), his camera expertise, and his defensive competence all track with his profile. Poppy's literalism and attachment to Mr. Buttons are rendered without sentimentality. Mara's physicality in the fight—not graceful, just committed and irritated—matches her profile precisely. The line "People say that when they want something" is exactly her register. The "Everybody stays where they belong" callback from the plan is absent, which is a minor miss—the chapter plan specifically noted this phrase should recur. The chapter ends instead with the memory card discovery and Poppy's question, which is effective but loses that planned resonance. The fight scenes are handled well. The curtain rod, the flashlight throw, the heel-of-hand strike—these feel grounded and unglamorous, consistent with the intention to avoid action-hero fantasy. The detail of the gun kicked under the car is smart and practical. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is almost too clean. The plan called for Mara to make a tactical mistake (the basement route that fails), but this chapter doesn't include that—it's reserved for Chapter Five. Within this chapter's scope, Mara is largely competent, which slightly undercuts the stated intention of showing her as fallible. The rib injury is present but brief. The older operative who recognizes Mara ("Vale") is introduced and dispatched somewhat quickly. He's an interesting figure—someone from the old operation—but the chapter doesn't fully exploit the moral complexity his presence could generate. He's more of a plot mechanism than a character here. The recording of Elise's voice is a clever beat, but Mara's deduction ("She calls you Pop when she's scared") is slightly too neat. It's the kind of observation that works in the moment but might not survive scrutiny—how would Mara know this after a few hours with the family? The prose is clean and controlled throughout, with no purple passages or forced metaphors. The writing occasionally risks being too spare—some moments could use a beat more texture—but this is a minor complaint in a genre that rewards economy. The final line ("But I think someone did") is slightly soft for a chapter ending. It's emotionally appropriate but lacks the punch of the planned ending with Mr. Buttons' voice speaking from the dark. The chapter plan's ending was more resonant; the actual ending is gentler and less memorable. Overall, this is a well-executed chapter that honors its plan, maintains character consistency, and delivers on the genre's promises without descending into cliché. The dialogue is the standout element. The chapter's main limitation is that it's competent rather than exceptional—it does what it sets out to do without surprising the reader in ways the plan didn't already anticipate. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four: The Part Where Mara Lies Mara slipped the memory card into the lining of her jacket. She did it slowly, in full view of Poppy, because hiding things from a six-year-old while standing three feet away was impossible. Poppy watched Mara’s hands. Children watched hands. Adults watched faces. “What was inside him?” Poppy asked. “Something your mother didn’t want other people to find.” “Is it a secret?” “Yes.” “Is it a bad secret?” Mara looked toward the garage door. Someone was still pounding on it. Another voice called from the house. “That depends who finds it.” Poppy looked at the hole in Mr. Buttons’ stomach. “You hurt him.” “I opened him.” “That hurts.” “Probably.” Poppy’s eyes filled with tears. Mara handed the rabbit back. “I’ll fix him.” “You said probably.” “I’m not good at sewing.” “You’re not good at fixing rabbits.” “No. But I’m good at fixing other things.” “Like people?” “People are harder.” Poppy hugged Mr. Buttons. Mara tore a strip from the hem of an old curtain and wrapped it around the rabbit’s middle. The result looked less like a repair than a medical emergency. Cal watched from beside the car. “You lied,” he said. Mara glanced at him. “About what?” “You said you’d answer every question.” “I said I wouldn’t lie.” “You told Poppy you’d fix him.” “I’m going to try.” “That’s not fixing.” “That’s how fixing starts.” The garage door shook. Mara looked at the wounded man on the floor. He was conscious now, breathing through his nose. “Can you walk?” He laughed once. “You broke my knee?” “No.” “Then yes.” “Good. Stand up.” He did not move. Mara picked up his fallen pistol and pointed it at the floor beside his hand. “Try again.” The man pushed himself upright. He kept one hand against the wall. “What’s your name?” Mara asked. He said nothing. “Fine. I’ll call you Wool Hat.” “Name’s Niko.” “You’re going to sit in the car.” “Which car?” “The one that doesn’t work.” Niko looked at the children. Mara saw the calculation begin again. He was afraid of her, but not enough. He was more afraid of somebody else. A speaker crackled above them. “Mara.” Victor’s voice was warm, almost intimate. Poppy pressed against Cal. Mara looked up at the ceiling. “You have a terrible habit of entering conversations uninvited.” “I’m trying to make this easier.” “You brought armed men to a babysitting job.” “They’re not there for you.” “Then they’ve made an embarrassing mistake.” “They’re there for the rabbit.” Victor paused. The tiny silence was deliberate. “Give it to them.” Mara looked at Poppy. Mr. Buttons was tucked beneath her chin. “No.” “You know who I am.” “Victor Sloane.” “And you know what happens when people refuse me.” “You send people with the wrong shoes.” Niko looked at her sharply. Victor’s voice remained pleasant. “You were always quick.” “Not quick enough, apparently.” “That depends on your definition of survival.” Mara felt the old place open inside her. A warehouse. Concrete. Diesel fumes. A boy crying in the back of a van. Victor’s voice over the radio, telling her to drive. Leave him. She had doubled back. The boy had lived. His father had not. Mara had gone to prison. Victor had gone on giving interviews about community safety. “You can leave,” Victor said. “There’s a car waiting on the north road. Cash, passport, a clean name. You disappear, and the children remain unharmed.” Mara looked at Cal. He was listening very carefully. “Do you always make this offer before or after you kill people?” she asked. “I’m not threatening the children. I’m explaining the consequences of your decisions.” “Sounds like a threat wearing a tie.” “They’re frightened, Mara. You’re making this worse.” For one second, she imagined putting the rabbit into Victor’s hands. The night ending. The children alive. Her own name buried again. Then Poppy touched the tape around Mr. Buttons’ middle. Mara remembered Elise saying, Don’t let anyone take him. Not please. Not be careful. Anyone. “Get in the car,” Mara told Niko. The garage door shook harder. Niko limped toward the sedan parked inside. Mara opened the rear door and pointed. “Lie down.” “You’re not taking me hostage?” “I’m taking you somewhere quiet. The distinction depends on your behavior.” He got inside. Mara turned to Cal. “Can you access the camera system from the storage room?” “Yes.” “Can you see the front hall?” “Yes.” “Can you tell me when they move?” “Yes.” “Then do that.” Cal started toward the door, stopped, and looked back. “Are you going to kill him?” Mara glanced at Niko. “I’m trying not to.” “That’s not reassuring.” “It’s honest.” Cal disappeared into the storage room. Poppy stayed beside Mara. “Do I have to go?” “Yes.” “Why?” “Because when people with guns are looking for something, they usually look at eye level first.” “I’m not at eye level.” “Exactly.” They moved through the service passage and into the house. Mara locked the garage door behind them and dragged a metal shelf across it. The intercom crackled again. “You’re wasting time,” Victor said. “Good.” “Daniel Hart is alive.” Mara stopped. Poppy looked at her. Victor continued. “He’s injured, but alive. Elise has the decryption key. She’s coming back for him.” “Why tell me?” “Because you understand transactions. One thing for another.” “Daniel for the rabbit?” “For the rabbit and the card.” “You know about the card?” “I know what Elise copied. I know where she hid it. I know she selected you because you have a history of making impossible extractions.” Mara’s fingers tightened around the pistol. Victor had not mentioned the card by accident. He wanted her to know the rabbit was no longer the only target. Cal’s voice came through the house speaker, faint and distorted. “Two in the kitchen. One at the stairs. The camera is delayed.” Mara pressed the intercom button. “Victor, you know what I remember about you?” “I’m sure you’ll tell me.” “You hate surprises.” This time the pause was shorter. “Don’t make the children pay for your anger.” “Don’t make the mistake of thinking my anger is the thing you need to worry about.” She released the button. Poppy tugged her sleeve. “Are we going to find Daddy?” “Maybe.” “Is he hurt?” “Victor says he is.” “Do you believe him?” Mara considered the question. “No.” Poppy looked relieved, then worried again. “Why not?” “Because he wants us to move.” A sound came from the front hall. The lock turned. Mara pulled Poppy behind the staircase and raised the pistol. The door opened. A uniformed police officer stepped inside, one hand raised, the other resting near his holster. “Police,” he called. “My name is Officer Davis. I’m here to help.” Mara did not lower the gun. Davis looked directly at her. “I know the dispatcher gave you a bad report,” he said. “She said you were armed and holding two children.” “Are you?” “No.” “Is your name on your badge?” “Yes.” “Read it.” He blinked. “What?” “Read your name.” “Officer Daniel Davis.” “Middle initial?” “R.” “What’s the call number?” He gave it. Mara did not know whether it was correct. Cal might. Davis took one step forward. A shot cracked from outside. Davis jerked and fell against the doorframe. Poppy screamed. Mara lunged, dragged him inside, and slammed the door. Blood spread across his vest. Davis looked up at her. “I’m guessing,” he said through clenched teeth, “this is not standard babysitting.” “No,” Mara said. She pressed her hand over the wound. “Stay with me.” Davis gave a strained laugh. “Was planning to.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Four is a strong piece of pulp thriller writing that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. DIALOGUE AND CHARACTER VOICE The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The exchanges between Mara and Poppy are particularly well-crafted. The rhythm of "You hurt him." / "I opened him." / "That hurts." / "Probably." captures both characters precisely: Poppy's literal moral universe and Mara's reluctant, compressed honesty. The exchange about fixing ("That's how fixing starts") is economical and thematically resonant without being heavy-handed. Cal's voice is consistent with his profile—suspicious, precise, testing. "That's not reassuring." / "It's honest." is a clean exchange that advances their relationship without sentimentality. Victor's intercom dialogue is appropriately chilling. "I'm not threatening the children. I'm explaining the consequences of your decisions" lands exactly as planned—it's lifted almost verbatim from the character profile's voice samples, which is both a strength (consistency) and a minor weakness (it reads as slightly pre-packaged). His warmth-as-menace is well-executed. Davis's entrance and the "Read your name" / "Middle initial?" sequence is clever—it shows Mara's operational thinking without explaining it, and Davis's wounded quip ("I'm guessing this is not standard babysitting") is earned rather than forced. PLOT EXECUTION The chapter follows its plan closely. The memory card is hidden, Victor speaks through the intercom, the offer is made and refused, Niko is managed, Davis arrives and is immediately shot. The plan called for Mara to "nearly refuse to trust him" but ultimately let him in based on dispatcher details—the chapter handles this slightly differently, having Mara verify his badge details rather than dispatcher-specific information, which is a reasonable adaptation that feels more organic. The chapter does not include the text Mara sends to Len ("If I call, don't come alone"), which was planned. This is a minor omission that doesn't damage the chapter but represents a deviation from the plan. The "Part Where Mara Lies" title is paid off—she lies to Poppy about fixing Mr. Buttons, she lies to Victor implicitly by not surrendering, and the chapter is structured around deception as a survival tool. SHOWING VS. TELLING The chapter is predominantly show-don't-tell. The moment where Mara imagines surrendering the rabbit—"For one second, she imagined putting the rabbit into Victor's hands"—is the closest it comes to telling, but it's brief and immediately grounded in action (Poppy touching the tape). The warehouse memory is handled efficiently: "A warehouse. Concrete. Diesel fumes. A boy crying in the back of a van." This is fragmented and sensory rather than expository. PROSE QUALITY The prose is clean and controlled. Sentence fragments are used deliberately and effectively. There's no purple prose. The metaphors are sparse and functional—"a threat wearing a tie" is a good Mara line, in-character and not overwrought. One slightly weak moment: "the old place opened inside her" is a bit vague as a description of Mara's trauma response. It gestures at interiority without quite landing. But it's brief. PACING The chapter moves well. The intercom confrontation is the centerpiece and earns its length. The Niko management is brisk. Davis's arrival and shooting is well-timed as a chapter-ending escalation. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The Poppy/Mr. Buttons material is genuinely affecting without being manipulative. The curtain-strip bandage on the rabbit is a lovely concrete detail. Poppy's question "Do you believe him?" and Mara's "No" / "Why not?" / "Because he wants us to move" is a small masterclass in character-appropriate emotional economy. WEAKNESSES The chapter is very competent but rarely transcendent. It executes the plan faithfully without significantly exceeding it. Some of the Victor dialogue, while good, feels slightly like it's hitting expected beats for a smooth villain. The Niko material is thinner than the plan suggested—he's present but not developed much beyond his earlier characterization. The "Wool Hat" nickname gag is fine but slightly forced. The chapter's consistency is its strength and its limitation: it's reliably good throughout without producing moments of genuine surprise or elevation. FAITHFULNESS TO PROMPT AND PLAN Very faithful to both. The pulp revenge tone is maintained. The domestic details (curtain strip, car in garage, service passage) ground the thriller elements. The chapter advances the plot as planned while maintaining character consistency. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five: The Room Under the Stairs Officer Davis weighed more than Mara expected. Not dead weight. He was conscious and trying to help, which made him awkward. He kept reaching for the doorframe while Mara dragged him across the hall. “Stop doing that,” she said. “I’m trying to walk.” “You’re trying to bleed on my floor.” “It’s not your floor.” “Tonight it is.” She got him into the pantry and pushed the door shut. The room smelled of flour, coffee, and oranges. Poppy crouched beside the shelves, hugging Mr. Buttons. Cal stood watch at the small service window. “Can you sit?” Mara asked Davis. “I can sit.” “Can you tell me how many attackers?” “Four, maybe five.” “Maybe?” “I arrived alone.” “Why?” “Dispatch said there was one armed suspect and two children.” Mara pressed a dish towel against his side. Blood soaked through immediately. Davis sucked air through his teeth. “Your report was wrong,” she said. “I noticed.” “Did dispatch sound normal?” Davis looked toward the door. “Normal enough.” “That’s not an answer.” “It sounded like a dispatcher who’d been given information by someone else.” Mara glanced at Cal. He was listening. “Can you get a signal?” she asked him. “Not in here.” “Try the upstairs landing.” “They’ll see me.” “Then don’t be seen.” Cal looked as if he wanted to argue. He did not. That was new. Mara opened the pantry door a fraction. The hallway was empty. “Stay with Davis,” she told Poppy. Poppy shook her head. “He’s not family.” “He’s injured.” “So?” “So he needs someone to tell him not to fall asleep.” Davis gave Poppy a weary look. “I’m not going to sleep.” “People say that before they sleep.” “That is unfortunately true.” Mara moved into the hall. The house had become a collection of noises. Footsteps above. A cupboard opening downstairs. A soft electronic chirp from the security panel. Somewhere, water dripped steadily. She found Cal at the upstairs landing, lying flat behind the banister. “The camera delay is forty seconds,” he whispered. “Can you watch the front hall?” “Yes.” “Tell me when someone moves.” He pointed to the monitor. “There.” The image showed the front hall empty. Then, forty seconds later, a man crossed it. “Two,” Cal said. “One near the stairs.” Mara looked over the banister. The actual staircase was empty. The monitor showed a man halfway up. “Move,” Mara said. They went down the back stairs, through the laundry room, and toward the basement. The basement door was beneath the main stairs. It had no camera. Mara appreciated that until she opened it. A narrow staircase descended into darkness. “Where does this go?” she asked. “Storage,” Cal said. “Of course.” They brought Davis down between them. He was pale now, but still upright. At the bottom, Mara found a utility room, shelves of paint tins, and a concrete corridor running beneath the house. “Coal chute?” she asked. Daniel’s voice came from somewhere in the dark. “Mara?” Elise’s voice followed. “Don’t answer him.” Mara raised the pistol. A light came on at the far end of the corridor. Daniel Hart sat against the wall, one hand pressed to his forehead. His white shirt was torn and dark with blood. Elise stood behind him with a brass key in her fist. For half a second, Mara thought the night had finally delivered something useful. Then Elise said, “Daniel told them where the rabbit was.” Mara aimed at both of them. Poppy made a small sound behind her. Daniel looked at the children. “I didn’t tell them,” he said. “You told them the house had a service passage.” “I was hurt.” “That’s not an answer.” “They threatened you.” “They threatened everyone.” Elise took one step forward. “Victor has the garage covered.” “Where were you?” “Outside.” “Doing what?” “Trying to get the decryption key to a journalist.” “Did you?” “No.” “Then you were doing nothing useful.” Elise’s mouth tightened. “I came back for my family.” “After hiring me to stand in front of them.” “I didn’t know they would attack tonight.” “You knew they might.” “Yes.” Cal stared at her. Mara saw him understand before either parent did. “You knew?” he asked. Elise looked at him. “Not like this.” “You knew people were coming?” “I knew there was danger.” “And you left us here?” Daniel closed his eyes. Elise said, “I thought Mara could protect you.” Mara laughed once. There was no humor in it. “That’s a lovely thing to say after the bullets start.” The house groaned overhead. Victor’s voice came through a small speaker in the corner. “Daniel, I’m glad you’ve found your family.” Poppy grabbed Cal’s hand. Victor continued, “Mara, bring me the rabbit and the card. I’ll let Elise leave with the children.” “Why would you?” “Because I prefer agreements to waste.” “You’re holding Daniel.” “Daniel is an employee who made an unfortunate error. Elise is a mother. The distinction matters.” “It doesn’t.” “It matters to you.” Mara looked at the brass key in Elise’s hand. “What does it open?” “The second drive,” Elise said. “Where?” “Daniel’s office.” “Is the evidence on the card?” “Part of it. The rest is encrypted.” Victor interrupted. “You’re running out of time.” Mara looked around the basement. The coal chute was sealed with concrete. The utility room had one narrow vent and a drain in the floor. No exit. She had brought them into a box. Cal was staring at the security monitor mounted above the utility controls. The image showed the basement corridor from forty seconds earlier. “Wait,” he whispered. “What?” “The feed.” He pointed. On the screen, two men entered the basement corridor. In the actual corridor, nobody had appeared yet. Mara studied the image. One man stopped near the utility room. The other continued past him. The second man wore a police uniform. The first had the wool cap. They were watching the basement. “Forty seconds,” Cal said. “Enough for what?” “For them to think we’re still here.” Mara looked at the vent, the drain, the shelves, and the old emergency door in the corner. The door had no handle. She crossed to it and pushed. It opened inward two inches before stopping against a stack of paint cans. Behind it was a narrow maintenance crawlspace. Not an exit. But somewhere to hide. Mara turned toward Elise. “You’re going to tell Victor the rabbit is coming.” Elise stared at her. “What?” “You’re going to tell him I’m bringing it to the garage.” “He’ll kill Daniel.” “Maybe.” Daniel looked up. “That’s your plan?” “No. That’s the part where I lie.” Mara took Mr. Buttons from Poppy. Poppy clutched her wrist. “Don’t give him away.” “I’m not.” “Are you sure?” Mara looked into her wet, furious eyes. “No,” she said. “But I’m going to try.” She wrapped the rabbit in a clear plastic evidence bag from a shelf and handed it to Elise. “Keep this visible.” Elise understood first. “You want them to think the card is inside.” “I want them to think they won.” Davis, leaning against the wall, gave a faint smile. “People make mistakes when they win.” Mara looked at him. “You stay here.” “That’s not much of a plan.” “It’s the beginning of one.” She moved toward the stairs with Poppy and Cal behind her. Daniel called after her. “Mara.” She stopped. “I’m sorry.” She looked back at him. “I know.” It was not forgiveness. Above them, footsteps began descending. Mara opened the basement door and led the children into the dark house. Then she picked up the rabbit and walked toward the garage under a white sheet of surrender.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Five is a tightly constructed piece of pulp thriller writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. CRAFT AND STYLE The prose is economical and controlled. The chapter opens with a strong, specific detail—"Officer Davis weighed more than Mara expected. Not dead weight. He was conscious and trying to help, which made him awkward"—which immediately grounds us in physical reality while revealing character. The dialogue is clipped and functional, with genuine wit in exchanges like "It's not your floor" / "Tonight it is." This is the kind of understated banter the plan called for, and it lands consistently. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no strained metaphors or overwrought descriptions. The one slightly self-conscious line—"The house had become a collection of noises"—is borderline but acceptable; it's doing real work establishing atmosphere without becoming decorative. DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. The exchange between Mara and Davis in the pantry is sharp and character-revealing. The confrontation with Daniel and Elise is emotionally loaded and specific: "You knew people were coming?" / "I knew there was danger." / "And you left us here?" This is exactly the kind of understated moral reckoning the plan called for. Cal's dawning comprehension is handled through dialogue rather than narration, which is the right choice. Poppy's voice is well-maintained: "He's not family" and "People say that before they sleep" are both in character—literal, slightly strange, and quietly funny. The line "Don't give him away" / "I'm not." / "Are you sure?" / "No. But I'm going to try." is the chapter's emotional peak and earns its weight. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan closely. The coal chute is sealed (Mara's tactical mistake), the camera delay is used cleverly, Victor speaks through the intercom, Elise arrives with the brass key, and the chapter ends with Mara walking toward the garage under a white sheet. The plan's beats are hit in order without feeling mechanical. The camera delay as a tactical element is genuinely clever and feels earned—Cal has been established as the person who notices these things. The forty-second delay creating a window for misdirection is the kind of practical ingenuity the plan promised. The basement-as-trap is Mara's acknowledged mistake, which the plan specified. The chapter doesn't try to paper over it; Mara explicitly recognizes she's brought them into a box. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Mara is consistent throughout: observant, controlling under stress, dry-humored, and not forgiving Daniel even when she acknowledges his apology. "I know" as a non-forgiveness is exactly right for her character. Davis's admission of uncertainty ("I arrived alone" / "Why?" / "Dispatch said there was one armed suspect") matches his profile. Cal's growth—choosing not to argue when told to go—is noted without being over-explained. The confrontation with Daniel and Elise is the chapter's moral center, and it's handled with appropriate complexity. Elise's admission that she knew there was danger but left the children anyway is damning, and the chapter doesn't soften it. Daniel's "I'm sorry" and Mara's "I know" is a perfect beat—neither redemptive nor dismissive. WEAKNESSES The chapter is somewhat thin on Niko, who appears in the plan as a significant presence but is absent here. This is a minor deviation from the plan. The ending—Mara walking toward the garage under a white sheet—is described in the plan as the chapter's closing image, and it's delivered, but the transition is slightly rushed. The chapter moves from the basement discovery to the plan to the ending in a few quick paragraphs, and the mechanics of how Mara gets from the basement to the garage with the children safely hidden could use slightly more grounding. The line "Mara laughed once. There was no humor in it" is a small tell-don't-show moment—the parenthetical explanation undercuts what the laugh itself should convey. Victor's intercom speech is functional but slightly generic compared to his character profile's promise of something more unsettling. He sounds like a competent thriller villain rather than the specific, peppermint-carrying, frame-straightening character described. OVERALL This is confident, professional pulp writing. It's not trying to be literary fiction and doesn't pretend to be. The chapter delivers on its plan's promises, maintains character consistency, and produces several genuinely good moments. Its weaknesses are minor: a slightly rushed ending, one tell-don't-show moment, and Victor being somewhat underwritten in his intercom appearance. The lightness (Poppy's lines, the Davis banter) is well-earned and doesn't undercut the tension. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six: A Professional Courtesy The sheet made Mara look ridiculous. That was useful. She held Mr. Buttons in one hand and kept the other raised. Poppy and Cal followed behind her, heads down, though Mara had told them to keep their eyes open. The basement door closed softly behind them. “Stay close,” Mara whispered. “That’s not what surrender means,” Cal said. “It means whatever I need it to mean.” They crossed the kitchen. The house had changed again. One window stood open. Rain blew against the floorboards. A chair blocked the back door. Someone had dragged the sofa into the hall. The attackers were not searching now. They were waiting. Mara’s stomach tightened. Waiting meant Victor had brought everyone into position. The intercom clicked. “Do you see the garage?” Victor asked. Mara pressed the button on the wall. “I see a lot of things.” “Bring the rabbit to the center of the floor.” “Do you want it gift-wrapped?” “Just keep walking.” Cal glanced at the camera above the hall. Its red light was on. Mara touched two fingers to her leg. Cal understood. He looked down. The monitor in the kitchen had been left visible through the doorway. Its image showed the hall forty seconds earlier. In the screen, Mara and the children were still standing beside the basement door. The real hall was empty. The delay was working. They reached the garage entrance. Mara stopped before opening it. A voice came from the other side. “Open the door.” Not Victor. A woman’s voice. Mara looked at Cal. He shook his head. It was not Elise. Mara opened the door. The woman standing inside wore a black security jacket and held a compact pistol. Behind her, the garage door was closed. Daniel sat on the concrete beside the rear tire of the Hart family’s SUV, his hands zip-tied. Blood had dried at his temple. Victor stood beside him in a charcoal suit. He was exactly as Mara remembered. Taller than she had expected, thinner than photographs suggested, with the same tidy hair and the same expression of patient concern. He looked at Mr. Buttons. “There,” he said. Mara kept the rabbit behind the clear plastic bag. “Where’s Elise?” “Safe.” “That wasn’t my question.” “Alive, then.” Daniel tried to speak. Victor placed one polished shoe against his knee. Daniel stopped. Poppy made a tiny noise. Victor’s eyes moved to her. “Hello, Poppy.” Mara stepped slightly in front of the children. Victor smiled. “Mara Vale,” he said. “You look well.” “You look expensive.” “I’ve had a good few years.” “I heard.” “I heard you had not.” Mara felt Cal watching her. Victor continued, “You were always difficult to account for. Even when you were working for me.” “I never worked for you.” “You drove for my people.” “I drove a van.” “You drove people.” “Not willingly, as it turned out.” Victor’s smile thinned. “You’re still telling yourself that story.” Mara looked at Daniel. “He told you where the rabbit was?” Daniel’s eyes flicked toward the bag. Mara saw the answer. Victor noticed her notice. “Daniel was under pressure,” he said. “We all are, from time to time.” “Did you hurt him?” “I corrected his priorities.” Mara placed the bag on the hood of the SUV. Victor nodded to the woman with the gun. She took two steps forward. “Open it,” Mara said. The woman looked at Victor. He gave a slight nod. She picked up the rabbit and cut through the plastic. Mr. Buttons’ taped middle sagged open. The woman reached inside and pulled out the memory card. For the first time that night, Victor looked pleased. He took the card from her. “Where is the key?” he asked. “You said Elise had it.” “She does.” “Then why ask me?” “Because you understand transactions.” Mara looked at Daniel. “The card is encrypted?” Victor’s expression did not move, but the woman’s did. She looked toward him. Mara noticed. Victor said, “You already know that.” “I know you need something else.” “The key.” “And Elise.” “And Elise.” “Then this isn’t a trade. It’s a delay.” Victor’s voice became cooler. “You don’t need to understand the entire operation.” “I understand enough.” She took one step toward the SUV. The woman raised the gun. Mara stopped. Victor studied her. “Do you remember the warehouse?” She did not answer. “Concrete floor. Bad lighting. A boy in a red jacket.” Cal stiffened behind her. Victor saw that, too. “You were told to leave him,” he said. “You refused. You turned the vehicle around. His father was killed before you reached the loading dock.” Mara kept her face still. “You took the boy out,” Victor continued. “A noble gesture. Unfortunately, you were the only adult left alive to blame.” “Are you finished?” “No. I wanted you to know that your brother never came to the trial.” Mara’s hand tightened. Victor’s eyes brightened. “There she is.” Mara had spent six years trying not to remember the exact sound of Victor’s voice over the radio. Drive. She had believed the mistake was turning around. Now she understood that Victor had already decided what would happen before she touched the brakes. “You framed me,” she said. “I preserved the structure.” “You killed the father.” “He was a liability.” “You killed the boy.” “No. You saved him.” Victor smiled without warmth. “That is what made you useful. You needed to believe you were better than the work.” Behind Mara, Cal whispered, “Mara.” She heard it. Not fear. Information. The garage camera’s red light blinked twice. Cal pointed toward the ceiling. Mara understood. He had reached the office feed. Elise was moving somewhere inside the house. Victor looked at the rabbit. “Where is the key?” “Ask Daniel.” Daniel laughed weakly. Victor lowered his foot from Daniel’s knee and struck him in the ribs. Poppy flinched. Mara felt something inside herself shift. Not anger. Anger was hot and fast. This was colder. “You don’t touch him again,” she said. Victor looked almost amused. “You’re giving orders?” “No.” She lifted her chin. “I’m making a prediction.” The woman with the gun moved closer. Victor said, “You have no leverage.” Mara glanced at the memory card in his hand. “You think that’s the evidence.” “It is.” “It’s a copy.” Victor went still. “The real card is in the house,” Mara said. “And the key is not with Elise.” His eyes moved toward Daniel. Daniel looked frightened. That was enough. Victor turned to the woman. “Search the house again.” The woman hesitated. “We already—” “Now.” There it was. The first crack. Mara smiled faintly. Victor looked back at her. “You’re bluffing.” “Probably.” He hated the word. Mara knew because his face changed. The lights in the garage went out. Somewhere in the house, an alarm began screaming. Cal had triggered something. The woman raised her pistol. Mara shoved the hood of the SUV into her, caught the gun wrist, and drove her elbow into the woman’s throat. Victor stepped back. Daniel rolled away from the vehicle. Mara reached for the nearest child. “Move!” Cal grabbed Poppy. They ran toward the side door as the garage filled with shouted orders. Behind them, Victor called her name. Not Mara. The name from before. The name she had buried. She did not turn around. But she heard him say, very softly: “You always were bad at leaving people behind.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Six is a tightly constructed confrontation scene that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft in several areas. Let me examine it critically across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Victor and Mara's exchange has the quality of two people who know each other's methods, and the sparring feels earned rather than theatrical. Lines like "I drove a van" / "You drove people" / "Not willingly, as it turned out" are economical and character-revealing. Victor's "I preserved the structure" in response to "You framed me" is exactly the kind of chilling bureaucratic evil the character profile demands. The chapter avoids the trap of having Mara deliver badass declarations; instead, her danger emerges through tactical observation and controlled provocation. The pacing is well-managed. Short paragraphs create tension without feeling like a stylistic tic. The chapter moves through the confrontation at a brisk clip while still allowing character beats to land. Cal's role is handled well. His silent communication with Mara (the two-finger signal, the camera blink) shows the trust that has developed between them without stating it. This is genuine show-don't-tell. The revelation about Mara's conviction—that Victor had already decided the outcome before she turned the van around—is the chapter's emotional core and it lands cleanly. "Drive. She had believed the mistake was turning around. Now she understood that Victor had already decided what would happen before she touched the brakes." This is the kind of recontextualization that gives pulp fiction its backbone. Victor's tell—hating the word "probably"—is a nice callback to the character profile detail and demonstrates consistency. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan called for Elise to appear "bloodied and carrying a small brass key" and to reveal that "Daniel told them where the rabbit was." In the actual chapter, Elise does not appear at all—she is only referenced as being somewhere in the house. The brass key subplot is absent. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan, though the chapter compensates by having Daniel's betrayal implied through his eye movement rather than stated. The plan's promise of Elise's arrival as a chapter-ending revelation is not fulfilled. The chapter plan also specified that Niko "quietly helps Mara move through the house" and that Mara "gives him a chance to leave with the wounded officer." Niko is entirely absent from this chapter. Davis is also absent. These omissions mean the chapter is somewhat simpler than planned, though it remains coherent. The woman operative is introduced without a name or distinguishing characteristic beyond "black security jacket." She functions as a prop rather than a character, which is a missed opportunity given the plan's emphasis on attackers having distinct personalities. The ending—Victor calling Mara by her old name and saying "You always were bad at leaving people behind"—is effective but slightly too neat. It's the kind of villain line that sounds written rather than spoken. It's not egregious, but it edges toward the theatrical. "This was colder" after "Not anger. Anger was hot and fast" is a minor instance of the prose telling us what the prose should be showing. It's brief but present. The chapter's action sequence (Mara disarming the woman) is handled quickly and competently, though it's somewhat abrupt. The physical choreography is minimal, which is appropriate for the style but leaves the escape feeling slightly rushed. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is confident, disciplined pulp writing. The chapter follows the spirit of the plan even where it deviates from specifics. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The dialogue is the best element. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. Its weaknesses are primarily structural (missing characters, unfulfilled plan elements) rather than craft failures. It reads like a chapter from a competent professional thriller rather than a masterpiece, but it's solidly above average work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven: The Wrong Babysitter The side door led into the mudroom. Mara shoved it closed and dropped the bolt. Behind them, the garage erupted with noise: boots, shouted orders, the metallic clatter of someone kicking the door. “Upstairs,” Mara said. Cal grabbed her sleeve. “The office.” “No.” “The real card is there.” “I know.” “Elise is there.” Mara looked at him. “How do you know?” “I saw her on the camera.” “How long ago?” “Thirty seconds.” Mara swore and pulled him toward the stairs. Poppy followed, clutching Mr. Buttons. The rabbit’s taped stomach hung open. Its remaining eye had fallen loose and swung on a thread. The house lights flashed on, then off again. A voice shouted from the garage. “Cut them off!” Mara pushed the children up the stairs. “Stay behind me.” “You said that last time,” Cal said. “And you’re still alive.” “That’s not the same as a good plan.” “No. It’s the part before the good plan.” They reached the landing. The delayed camera feed showed the corridor empty. Mara waited. The screen showed a man entering the hall. Then the real hallway door opened. Mara fired once into the ceiling. The man froze. She kicked the gun from his hand and drove him backward into the wall. He was younger than Victor, older than Niko, and wore a neat black jacket with a company logo stitched above the breast pocket. He looked at Mara and smiled. “You should have stayed dead.” “Everybody keeps saying that.” He swung at her. Mara ducked, caught his wrist, and twisted. He was stronger, trained, and not frightened enough. He slammed his forehead into hers. White light filled her vision. She staggered. The man reached for Cal. Cal bit his hand. The man swore. Mara recovered long enough to drive her knee into his stomach. He folded. She caught the back of his jacket and threw him down the stairs. He landed hard and did not rise. Poppy stared at him. “Is he dead?” “No.” “Is he going to be?” “Not if he’s sensible.” Mara wiped blood from her eyebrow. Her hand came away red. The office door stood open at the end of the hall. Inside, Elise was at the desk, trying to connect a small black drive to Daniel’s computer. “Where’s the key?” Mara asked. Elise held up the brass key. “It’s physical,” she said. “The drive is locked.” “Then unlock it.” “I need Daniel’s password.” “Ask him.” “He won’t answer me.” “Why?” “He’s unconscious.” Mara looked at Cal. “Can you get into the system?” Cal was already at the desk. “I know his password.” Elise turned. “How?” “He uses the same one for everything.” “What is it?” Cal typed. The screen opened. Elise stared at him. “It’s Poppy’s birthday,” he said. “He said it was temporary.” Mara almost laughed. A red light blinked on the computer. Files began copying. From downstairs, Victor’s voice came through the intercom. “You have made this more difficult than necessary.” Mara pressed the office door shut. Victor continued, “I have Daniel. I have your police officer. I have a team between you and the street.” Davis’s voice came through the radio on the desk. “Not anymore.” Mara looked at the small police radio. Davis had somehow reached it from the basement. Victor’s voice sharpened. “You’re going to regret that.” “No one likes a sore winner,” Davis said. The transmission cut out. Elise checked the copy progress. “Thirty percent.” “We don’t have time.” “We need the full file.” “Then make it faster.” “It doesn’t work that way.” “Everything works faster when it’s afraid.” A gunshot sounded below. Poppy covered her ears. Cal stopped typing. Mara put one hand on the back of his neck. “Keep going.” “I can’t.” “You can.” “What if they killed him?” “Then we keep going.” Cal looked at her. He was shaking. Mara lowered her voice. “Listen to me. You don’t have to be brave. You don’t have to make this better. You only have to keep your hands moving.” He looked at the screen. His fingers resumed. The office door handle began to turn. Mara dragged the heavy desk in front of it. Elise grabbed a brass lamp and stood beside her. “Thirty-eight percent,” Cal said. “Can you send what’s done?” “To who?” “Anybody.” Cal opened the mail program. Mara saw a contact list. One name was marked with a star: **Ruth Bell — Northline Journal**. “Send it,” she said. “It’s encrypted.” “Send it anyway.” Cal pressed the button. The file began uploading. The door shook. Victor spoke from the hall. “Daniel told me the truth.” Elise went pale. “What truth?” Mara asked. “That you were hired because of your record. Elise thought a woman with your history would understand how to disappear.” Mara looked at Elise. Elise did not deny it.
