Dark hair.” “That narrows it down to every man who has ever been rained on.” The photographer looked past her. “Can I have a look around?” “No.” He seemed surprised. “Why not?” “Because it’s a bookshop, not a police station.” “It’ll only take a minute.” “Then you can spend that minute outside.” The man held her gaze. Cat held it back. She had dealt with customers who wanted refunds for books they had written in themselves. She had dealt with estate agents, damp inspectors, and a woman who had tried to return a cookbook because the recipes were “too confident.” A man with a camera was not going to make her nervous. The photographer muttered something and left. Cat waited until the bell stopped moving. “Are you gone?” she asked. “No.” “Pity.” Rhys emerged from behind a stack of boxed biographies. He had acquired a cobweb in his hair. “You lied for me.” “I protected my stockroom.” “Of course.” “You’re blocking the aisle.” “I’m still processing the kindness.” “Try processing it while moving.” He stepped out. Up close, he was even more familiar. Cat had seen his face on the sides of buses, on cinema posters, on the cover of a magazine someone had left in the café. He had played a soldier, a duke, a man who fell in love with a woman who owned a vineyard, and a man who fell in love with a woman who owned a vineyard during a war. She had enjoyed the second one. She would rather be buried than tell him. He glanced at the book on the counter. “Have I damaged that?” “You’ve bent the cover.” “I’ll buy it.” “It’s a first edition.” “Then I’ll buy it more enthusiastically.” Cat turned the book over. “You don’t know what it is.” “Of course I do.” “You’ve been hiding behind it.” “It’s a novel.” “It’s a history of nonconformist chapels in West Wales.” He looked at the cover. “A novel with a very committed church.” Despite herself, Cat made a sound. It was not quite a laugh. It was more of a cough that had lost its way. Rhys noticed. His smile returned, gentler this time. “There it is.” “There what is?” “Evidence that I’m not entirely intolerable.” “I haven’t established that.” “Give me time.” “I’m trying not to.” He rested his hands on the counter. They were long-fingered, slightly scraped at the knuckles. His wedding finger was bare. Cat noticed this and immediately resented herself for noticing. “So,” he said. “You know who I am.” “I know your face.” “Not my name?” “Your name is on the cover of several magazines in the bin.” He leaned closer. “You’ve read them?” “I use them to stop the till wobbling.” “Then you’ve studied me extensively.” “I’ve studied the till.” He laughed properly, and Cat looked down at the bent book. The laugh was warmer than it sounded on television. That was inconvenient. “Rhys Morgan,” he said, offering his hand. Cat looked at it. He lowered it. “Right. We’re not doing that.” “I know who you are.” “You said that.” “I meant I know who you are in the sense that I know your name.” “That’s usually how it works.” “It’s a bookshop. We deal in distinctions.” He glanced at the sign above the till. “Cat?” “Catrin.” “Catrin.” He said it carefully. Not perfectly, but carefully enough. She felt an irritating flicker of approval. “Evans,” she said. “Miss Evans.” “Don’t make it worse.” “I’m trying to be respectful.” “You’re trying to flirt.” “I contain multitudes.” “You contain damp.” He looked down at himself. “That’s fair.” Cat rang up the book. “Eight pounds.” “Do I get a discount for fleeing through the rain?” “No.” “For celebrity status?” “No.” “For being charming?” “Especially no.” He took out his wallet. “You’re very hard to impress.” “I’m not trying to impress you.” “That’s what makes it impressive.” He handed her a note. Cat gave him the change. “There.” He counted it, then looked at her. “You’ve given me too much.” “I haven’t.” “You’ve given me change for twenty.” “You gave me twenty.” “I gave you fifty.” She looked at the note still in his hand. He looked at it too. “Right,” he said. “That’s embarrassing.” “It’s been a difficult afternoon.” Outside, the photographers had moved on. The rain tapped the windows, steady and fine. Rhys picked up the book. “Will you be here tomorrow?” he asked. “The shop will.” “And you?” Cat reached for the next customer’s abandoned receipt. “That depends,” she said. “Are you planning to damage anything else?” He smiled, but this time it did not seem designed for anyone outside the room. “No promises.” Then he opened the door, and the bell rang above him as he stepped back into the rain.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of romantic comedy writing that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The rapid-fire exchanges between Cat and Rhys feel genuinely distinct: Rhys's lines have a polished, performative quality that occasionally slips into something more authentic, while Cat's responses are dry, economical, and consistently deflating without becoming cruel. The rhythm is excellent — the five-second countdown sequence, the exchange about the dark coat, the till-wobbling magazines — these feel like real conversational sparring rather than scripted wit. The dialogue earns its lightness because it's grounded in specific, practical details (the bent book, the wrong change, the cobweb in his hair). The exchange about the fifty-pound note is particularly well-handled: it reveals Cat's flustered state through action rather than declaration, and Rhys's response ("That's embarrassing") is appropriately deflated rather than charming. This is exactly the kind of moment the plan called for. **Character Consistency** Rhys is rendered faithfully to his profile: the charm that arrives too quickly, the wounded look when it doesn't land, the genuine warmth beneath the performance. His line "I contain multitudes" is perfectly calibrated — it's the kind of thing someone says when they're trying to be clever and know they're trying too hard. The moment where "without the smile, he looked younger and considerably more tired" is a small but effective crack in the performance. Cat's internal state is conveyed almost entirely through behavior — the wrong change, the sound that "was not quite a laugh," the immediate self-recrimination for noticing his bare ring finger. This is exactly the "show don't tell" approach the plan demanded, and it works. The line "She had enjoyed the second one. She would rather be buried than tell him." is the chapter's best moment of interiority — economical, funny, and revealing. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: the wet entrance, the knocked display, the hiding, the photographer confrontation, the gradual revelation of identity, the wrong change as a tell for Cat's flustered state, and the closing exchange about whether he'll return. The plan called for Cat to recommend a book "solely because she thinks he needs it" — this doesn't quite happen (she doesn't recommend a second book), but the bent book purchase serves a similar function. The ending line "The shop will" is exactly as planned and lands well. **Craft and Originality** The opening description — "thirty-seven books on the history of Welsh chapels leaned together like a congregation losing faith" — is the chapter's one genuinely poetic moment, and it earns its place: it's specific, funny, and tonally appropriate. It doesn't feel forced. The Welsh setting is present but not labored; the rain, the high street, the chapel history books all feel natural rather than decorative. The photographer confrontation is handled with particular skill. Cat's responses ("It's a bookshop, not a police station") feel in character and the comparison to difficult customers grounds her competence without over-explaining it. **Weaknesses** The chapter is almost too polished. The banter is consistently good, which means it occasionally feels slightly airless — there are few genuine stumbles or failed jokes, which the plan specifically called for. Rhys's charm is shown failing, but mostly because Cat deflects it; we don't quite see him overplay his hand and ruin a moment as the plan described. The plan mentioned he would "overplay his charm and ruin the moment" — this is gestured at but not fully realized. The "he said it carefully. Not perfectly, but carefully enough" moment regarding her name is effective but slightly on-the-nose in its significance. The reader is being nudged a little too firmly toward noticing it matters. The chapter is also almost entirely in the register of light comedy, which is appropriate for an opening chapter but means the emotional depth is mostly implied rather than felt. This is a structural choice rather than a flaw, but it means the "emotionally engaging" dimension is more intellectual than visceral. **Purple Prose / Forced Metaphor** The writing is admirably restrained. The chapel congregation metaphor is the only significant poetic reach, and it works. There's no purple prose to speak of. **Overall** This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that establishes both characters clearly, maintains consistent voice throughout, and delivers on the prompt's core requirement of charged, uneven chemistry conveyed primarily through dialogue. It's not a masterpiece — the emotional stakes are kept deliberately low and the banter is almost too consistently good — but it's genuinely skilled work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Two: A Man Who Has Read the Back Cover The bell rang at eleven the next morning. Cat looked up from the stack of invoices beside the till. Rhys Morgan stood in the doorway holding an umbrella that had turned inside out. He wore a navy coat, a grey jumper, and the expression of a man who had spent the morning rehearsing an explanation and had forgotten it halfway down the street. “Good morning,” he said. Cat looked at the umbrella. “Did you lose a fight?” “With the weather, yes.” “Then you should have stayed in London.” “I’m beginning to think Wales has a personal objection to me.” “Wales has never heard of you.” He placed the collapsed umbrella in the stand by the door. “That’s hurtful.” “You’ll recover.” He walked towards the counter, stopping this time before he collided with anything. Cat noticed. “Learning,” he said. “Or fear.” “I prefer to think of it as respect.” “For the books?” “For you.” “Don’t.” He smiled. “You’ve said that before.” “And yet.” “Persistence is one of my better qualities.” “Is it?” “I’m told so.” “By people who want to employ you?” “Mostly.” Cat returned to her invoices. “What do you want?” “Local reading.” “You’ve come to the right place. We have a section called Local Reading.” “I’m preparing for a role.” “You’re an actor. That sounds dangerously plausible.” He leaned on the counter, then seemed to remember yesterday and straightened. “I’m filming near here.” “I know.” “Do you?” “There are three lorries parked outside the post office, a temporary toilet behind the chapel, and someone has put a sign on the road telling us not to look at the famous people.” “That sign was meant to be helpful.” “It has had the opposite effect.” Rhys glanced towards the front window. “I’m playing a poet.” “Are you?” “Yes.” “An actual poet?” “He wrote poems.” “That’s usually the definition.” He took off his coat. Underneath, his jumper was soft-looking and expensive-looking, though Cat could not have said why. He had a faint bruise along one jaw. She noticed it. He saw her notice. “It’s make-up,” he said. “I didn’t ask.” “It’s also a bruise.” “Which is it?” “Both, depending on the lighting.” “Acting must be exhausting.” “Mostly the explanations.” He reached into his pocket and produced a folded sheet of paper. “I need books about him.” Cat took it. The name at the top belonged to a nineteenth-century poet from the west coast, one whose work Rhys’s film had apparently transformed into a sweeping love story. “He did not write about an English heiress,” she said. “He may have.” “He did not.” “Then the film is taking liberties.” “The film is building a new person and giving him somebody else’s poems.” “That’s one way to describe cinema.” “Is it a bad one?” “It’s an expensive one.” Cat looked at the list. “Who wrote this?” “The production’s researcher.” “Have they read any of the books?” “They have very nice handwriting.” “That wasn’t my question.” “No.” She slid the paper back across the counter. Rhys did not pick it up. “I was hoping you might recommend something.” “You have a researcher.” “I have several researchers.” “Then you’re well supplied.” “They tell me what I need to know. I’m trying to find out what I don’t.” The answer was better than she expected. It also sounded suspiciously sincere. Cat turned away before he could see that. “Poetry is over there.” “I can find poetry.” “Can you?” He followed her to the shelves. “You’re going to stand there and watch me?” “I’m going to make sure you don’t shelve anything under adventure.” “That happened once.” “It happened yesterday.” “I was distracted.” “You were hiding.” “I can multitask.” Cat pulled out a slim collection and handed it to him. “Start with this.” Rhys read the title. “Is it depressing?” “It’s poetry.” “That wasn’t an answer.” “It’s Welsh poetry. It’s depressing in at least two languages.” He opened the book, flipped through it, and frowned at a page. “Do you think the poet was happy?” “Occasionally.” “How do you know?” “He wrote a poem about eating toast.” “That could be metaphorical.” “Not if it has butter.” He smiled, then read the back cover. “I recognize this name.” “You should. You’re playing him.” “I meant the translator.” “The translator is from Cardiff.” “I’ve met him.” “You’ve met everyone.” “I’m very approachable.” “You’re very famous.” “That isn’t the same thing.” “No,” Cat said. “It’s generally more inconvenient.” He looked at her over the top of the book. Something in his expression shifted, as if she had said more than she meant to. Before he could reply, the back door opened and Mabli came in carrying two paper bags. Mabli was Cat’s aunt, though she introduced herself as the shop’s “senior consultant,” which made her sound more official than the business warranted. She was seventy-one, wore red lipstick at ten in the morning, and had never once been impressed by a man with a famous face. She stopped when she saw Rhys. “Is this him?” Cat closed her eyes briefly. “Good morning to you too.” “The actor.” “I gathered.” Mabli set the bags on the counter and looked Rhys up and down. “You’re taller on television.” “Good morning,” Rhys said. “You’re thinner, as well.” “I’ve been working.” “Acting?” “Mostly.” Mabli nodded as though confirming a suspicion. “You’ll want tea.” “I’d love some.” “That wasn’t an offer.” Rhys glanced at Cat. Cat said, “She does that.” “Does what?” “Offers things she has already decided you’re having.” Mabli pointed at a box near the door. “Move that.” Rhys looked behind him. “Me?” “No, the poet.” He moved the box. Cat went back behind the counter. Mabli began unpacking the bags, which contained bread, cheese, and a quantity of apples that suggested she had been shopping for a household rather than a bookshop. Rhys carried the box to the back room. When he returned, there was dust on one sleeve. “Where does this go?” he asked. “On the bottom shelf by the desk.” “I can manage that.” “Then why are you asking?” “I’m trying to grow.” Mabli gave a short approving grunt. Cat disliked that she felt pleased. Rhys placed the box down. “I’m staying nearby for six weeks.” “Condolences,” Cat said. “Thank you.” “Is the film going to be here the whole time?” “Mostly. Some location work inland.” “Then you’ll learn where the good coffee is.” “I was hoping you’d tell me.” “I don’t know why.” “Because you own a bookshop. You know things.” “I sell things people know.” “Same difference.” “No.” He studied her for a moment. “Do you always argue with customers?” “Only the ones who keep coming back.” A pause. Not a large one, but enough. Rhys looked unexpectedly pleased. “So I’m a customer.” “You’ve bought one book.” “Two.” “You bought one yesterday.” “I bought another today.” Cat looked towards the poetry shelf. The book was tucked beneath his arm. He had not paid for it. “That one,” she said, pointing. He looked down. “Right.” “Unless you were planning to read it here.” “Could I?” “You could, if you sit quietly and don’t ask me questions.” “I’m not good at that.” “I know.” He brought it to the counter. Cat rang it up. When she handed him the receipt, their fingers touched. It was barely contact, but Rhys glanced down at their hands, then back at her. Cat became suddenly aware of the fluorescent light, the damp at the hem of his trousers, and the fact that Mabli was standing three feet away pretending not to listen. Rhys folded the receipt. “Do you think I’ll understand him?” he asked. “The poet?” “Yes.” “No.” He laughed softly. “But you might stop pretending you do,” Cat added. The smile faded from his face, though not completely. “That would be a start.” He tucked the book into his coat. At the door, he turned. “Miss Evans?” Cat looked up. “Further instruction may be required.” “I charge by the hour.” His grin returned. “I had a feeling you did.” The bell rang as he left. Mabli waited until the door shut. “He’s pretty.” “He’s famous.” “That wasn’t my question.” Cat picked up the next invoice. “He’s also badly read.” “Those things aren’t mutually exclusive.” “I’m aware.” Mabli leaned over the counter. “You’re going to see him again.” Cat stared at the figures on the page. They swam slightly. “He’s filming here.” “That isn’t what I said.” Cat reached for the red pen. Outside, Rhys’s broken umbrella turned inside out again. She saw it through the window and, despite herself, began to laugh.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Two of this story demonstrates considerable craft, particularly in its dialogue-driven approach and character consistency. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. DIALOGUE AND VOICE: The chapter's greatest strength is its dialogue. The exchange between Rhys and Cat crackles with genuine wit and subtext. Lines like "Persistence is one of my better qualities." / "Is it?" / "I'm told so." / "By people who want to employ you?" capture Rhys's charm-as-performance and Cat's deflection with economy and precision. The rhythm is sharp without feeling artificially polished. Crucially, the dialogue doesn't always land perfectly for Rhys—his attempts to impress Cat are met with dry corrections rather than admiration, which is exactly what the prompt and plan require. The introduction of Mabli is handled well: "That wasn't an offer" is a perfect character beat that establishes her voice immediately. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Both characters behave in ways consistent with their profiles. Rhys straightens before leaning on the counter ("Learning," he said), showing self-awareness mixed with performance. His question about whether he'll understand the poet—and Cat's answer that he might stop pretending he does—is a genuinely good moment where his fragile ego and her refusal to flatter him intersect meaningfully. Cat's internal flustering is conveyed through the brief finger-touch moment and the swimming figures on the invoice, which is appropriately restrained. The final image of her laughing at his broken umbrella is earned. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. Rhys returns with the "local reading" pretext, gets a basic fact wrong (the poet and the English heiress), is corrected without ceremony, Mabli arrives and immediately treats him as another customer (asking him to move a box), and the chapter ends with the note "Further instruction may be required" delivered verbally rather than written. The plan specified a written note, but the verbal delivery works equally well and perhaps better. The plan's beat about Rhys trying too hard to win Mabli's approval is present but understated—he's more bemused than desperate, which is a slight deviation but not a damaging one. SHOWING VS. TELLING: The chapter largely shows rather than tells. Cat's attraction is conveyed through noticing the bruise, the finger-touch awareness, the swimming invoice figures, and the final laugh. Rhys's ego-need is shown through his pleasure at being called "a customer" and his glance at their touching hands. There's minimal direct statement of emotion. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is almost too smooth. The plan called for "specific, imperfect conversation rather than constant witty repartee," but this chapter leans heavily toward witty repartee. Nearly every exchange is a successful comic beat. The plan also mentioned Rhys getting a basic fact wrong and being corrected—this happens (the English heiress), but it's handled so quickly and lightly that it barely registers as a failure for Rhys. His ego isn't genuinely threatened; he absorbs the correction with grace. The plan wanted him to "try to impress Cat with his literary knowledge and get a basic fact wrong"—the researcher's list is blamed rather than Rhys's own knowledge, which slightly deflects the intended character beat. The Mabli section, while charming, is perhaps slightly too efficient—she arrives, establishes her voice, makes Rhys move a box, and exits the scene cleanly. The plan suggested she "charms him easily, which irritates Cat until she realizes he is trying too hard to win Mabli's approval." This beat is present but compressed; Cat's irritation is barely visible. The prose itself is clean and controlled. There's no purple prose, no forced metaphor. The final image (umbrella turning inside out, Cat laughing) is a nice callback to the opening description and earns its lightness. The "swimming figures" on the invoice is the one moment that edges toward slight over-signaling, but it's restrained enough to work. OVERALL: This is a well-executed chapter that delivers on its core promises—sharp dialogue, consistent characters, restrained attraction, and a light comic touch. Its main limitation is that it's almost too polished; the plan's aspiration for imperfect conversation and genuine failure moments is only partially realized. The chapter is more uniformly witty than the plan intended, which makes it slightly less textured than it could be. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Three: Not a Tour Guide On Saturday afternoon, Cat found Rhys standing outside the archive with a paper cup of coffee and an expression of betrayal. “This is not coffee,” he said. “It’s from the machine.” “That doesn’t answer the question.” “It answers it completely.” He looked at the cup. “The woman at the desk said it was coffee.” “The woman at the desk is eighty-two. She has survived rationing. Her standards are different.” Rhys took another sip, bravely. “I’m learning so much.” “You haven’t even gone inside.” “I’ve learned not to trust local beverages.” “You’re playing a Welsh poet.” “I’m playing a Welsh poet who has presumably had coffee.” “Perhaps that’s why he was unhappy.” The archive occupied two rooms behind the old library. Its windows looked out over the harbour, where the tide had gone out and left the boats leaning in dark mud. A bilingual sign hung beside the door. Rhys glanced at it. “Can you read that?” he asked. “Yes.” “Both parts?” Cat looked at him. “They’re not two different signs.” “I know.” “You’re asking whether I can read Welsh.” “I’m asking whether you want to make me feel guilty about not being able to.” “I haven’t decided.” He smiled, but the smile did not quite reach his eyes. “I can read it.” “Can you?” “I can recognize several of the letters.” “That’s promising.” “I did Welsh at school.” “So did I.” “Were you good?” “Better than you.” “That seems unnecessarily personal.” “It’s an archive. We should be honest.” He held the door open. “After you.” Inside, the air smelled of dust, polish, and damp wool. A woman behind the desk looked up from a magnifying glass. “Catrin,” she said. “You’re early.” “I’m rarely early.” “You’re early today.” Cat glanced at Rhys. “He made me.” Rhys offered his hand. The woman ignored it. “This is Mr Morgan,” Cat said. “I know who he is,” the woman said. “My granddaughter has his photograph on her wall.” Rhys brightened. “Really?” “Beside a horse.” His expression faltered. Cat turned away before he could see her smile. The woman led them to a table covered with folders. The poet Rhys was playing had lived in a nearby village and written frequently about the sea, though not, as Cat had warned him, about heiresses in white dresses. Rhys bent over the papers. He became quiet. It was one of the first things Cat had noticed about him: his silence was never empty. Even when he appeared relaxed, part of him was listening for an audience. Here, confronted with the poet’s handwriting and a life that could not be reduced to a film poster, he seemed to forget he was being watched. Cat watched him anyway. “You’re frowning,” he said without looking up. “I’m not.” “You are.” “I’m concentrating.” “On the documents?” “On the fact that you’ve got ink on your thumb.” He looked at his hand. “That’s a serious flaw in my image.” “It may be your most interesting feature.” He turned his thumb over. “I have others.” “I’m sure the horse agrees.” He laughed quietly. The archivist returned with a box of letters. “He wrote to his brother in English,” she said. “The poems were mostly Welsh.” Rhys looked at the letters. “Why?” “Because his brother didn’t understand Welsh.” “And he did?” “He understood himself in both languages.” Rhys absorbed this. Cat leaned against the table. “You can use that in the film.” “That makes it sound simple.” “It isn’t.” “No,” he said. “It doesn’t.” For the next forty minutes, the archivist corrected the film’s assumptions. The poet had not been a solitary romantic genius. He had argued with his publisher, borrowed money from cousins, and written furious letters about a broken stove. His most famous poem had been drafted on the back of a grocer’s bill. Rhys asked questions. At first, some were the questions of an actor looking for useful details. Then they became less useful and more curious. “Did he really hate London?” he asked. “He hated being asked whether he hated London,” the archivist replied. Rhys glanced at Cat. “Sounds familiar,” she said. He folded his arms. “I have never hated London.” “You once said it had the emotional warmth of a hotel corridor.” “I was tired.” “You were on breakfast television.” “I was very tired.” When they left the archive, the rain had started again. Cat opened her umbrella. Rhys looked at it. “Don’t say it,” she said. “I was going to say you have a very dignified umbrella.” “You were going to ask whether we could share it.” “I was.” “You’re taller than me.” “I’d noticed.” “You can get your own.” He produced no umbrella. Cat stared at him. “I seem to have misplaced mine.” “You seem to have destroyed yours.” “That’s a more accurate description.” She moved beneath the umbrella. After a moment, he joined her, bending his head to avoid the metal spokes. His shoulder pressed against hers. Cat became conscious of everything: the rain ticking on the fabric, the warmth of his coat, the clean smell of soap and cold air. She adjusted the umbrella so it covered him more than her. He noticed. “Your sleeve is wet,” he said. “Your hair is dripping.” “I’m an actor. I can pretend it isn’t.” “That’s the first convincing thing you’ve said.” They walked towards the chapel hall where the local poetry group met on Saturdays. The hall door was propped open. A handwritten notice asked visitors to remove muddy shoes and not to use the piano as a table. Inside, six people sat in a circle with paper cups of tea. One of them, a broad-shouldered man in a rugby shirt, looked at Rhys and said, “You’re the actor.” Rhys smiled. “So I’ve been told.” “You’re playing Ieuan Prys.” “That’s right.” “Why?” Rhys hesitated. Cat sat beside the radiator. “Careful.” He looked at her. “Because he’s a compelling character.” “That’s not why,” said the man. “No,” Rhys admitted. “Because the script was good.” “Was it?” Rhys considered lying. Cat saw the moment he decided not to. “Parts of it.” The man nodded, apparently satisfied. “At least you’re honest.” “Don’t encourage him,” Cat said. The reading began. Rhys listened, though his attention wandered whenever someone used a phrase he did not understand. Cat translated quietly. Once, he repeated a word under his breath. “Your pronunciation is wrong,” she whispered. “I know.” “Then why are you saying it?” “I’m trying to improve.” “You’re trying to say it under your breath.” “That’s how improvement works.” “It isn’t.” At the break, he asked her how to say a sentence. She gave it to him slowly. He repeated it, badly. “No,” she said. “I’m close.” “You’re in another county.” He tried again. She reached for his wrist without thinking and corrected the position of his hand as though language were a physical thing. They both stopped. His skin was warm beneath her fingers. Cat let go. Rhys looked at her hand, then at her face. “You could have just told me,” he said. “I did.” “You’re very hands-on for someone who objects to flirting.” “I was correcting you.” “Of course.” The corner of his mouth lifted. Before she could answer, his phone rang. He looked at the screen and sighed. “I have to take this.” Outside, he answered in a different voice. “Hi. Yes, I’m fine. No, I know. I understand. Tell them I’ll be there.” Cat watched the change happen. His shoulders straightened. His expression became smooth. He was suddenly the man from posters and interviews, the man who could make inconvenience look charming. When he ended the call, he turned back towards her. The rain had eased. A pale strip of sky showed above the harbour. “You’re very good at that,” Cat said. “At what?” “Becoming someone else.” His smile flickered. “I’m an actor.” “Yes,” she said. “That’s what worries me.” He stepped closer. “Does it?” Cat looked at his mouth, then away. “Your coffee is getting cold.” “It was never coffee.” “Then you’ve lost nothing.” She walked towards the bookshop. Behind her, Rhys remained beneath the chapel awning for a moment, holding the empty paper cup and watching her go.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft in its execution of the writing plan. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element—it moves with genuine wit and economy, and the voices of Rhys and Cat are consistently distinct. Rhys's charm-as-defense mechanism is rendered through small, telling moments: his brightening at the granddaughter's photograph, then deflating at "beside a horse." Cat's dry precision ("You're in another county") feels earned rather than performed. The banter has texture because it occasionally fails or misfires, which is exactly what the plan called for. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The archive visit, the poetry group, the moment of accidental physical contact (Cat correcting his pronunciation by touching his wrist), the near-miss before the publicist's call, and Rhys's transformation into his public persona—all are present and handled with restraint. The plan's instruction that "Cat steps away before he can turn back" is rendered here as Cat walking toward the bookshop while Rhys stands under the awning, which is a slightly softer version but captures the same emotional beat. Character consistency is strong. Rhys's ego is visible in small calibrations—his hesitation before admitting the script was only partly good, his instinct to lie before deciding against it. Cat's attraction manifests through the wrist-touch, which she immediately releases, and through her looking at his mouth before looking away. These are exactly the kinds of physical tells the character profile specified. The archivist's dismissal of Rhys's handshake and the rugby-shirted man's blunt questioning of his motives add texture to the town's reaction. The publicist phone call is well-handled. The shift in Rhys's voice and posture is shown rather than told, and Cat's observation—"You're very good at that. Becoming someone else."—lands as both a compliment and a warning. His response, "I'm an actor," followed by her "Yes. That's what worries me," is the chapter's best exchange: economical, emotionally loaded, and character-true. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the umbrella scene, while charming, is a familiar romantic-comedy beat (sharing an umbrella in the rain) that doesn't quite transcend its genre conventions. The line "language were a physical thing" is a mild instance of the kind of explanatory nudge the plan said to avoid. The ending image—Rhys holding the empty paper cup and watching her go—is effective but not surprising; it's the expected closing image for this kind of scene. The Welsh cultural material is handled with appropriate lightness. The archivist's corrections of the film's assumptions, the bilingual poetry group, Cat's admission that her own Welsh is inconsistent—these feel inhabited rather than decorative. The poet's detail about drafting his most famous poem on a grocer's bill is a nice specific touch. The prose style is clean and controlled. There is no purple prose, no forced metaphor. The occasional descriptive passage ("the air smelled of dust, polish, and damp wool") is brief and functional. The chapter doesn't overreach stylistically, which is a virtue given how easy it would be to over-romanticize the setting. Minor weaknesses: the chapter is perhaps slightly too consistently successful in its banter—almost every exchange lands, which slightly undercuts the plan's intention to include "failed lines, interruptions, irritation, and moments when neither knows what to say." There are moments of genuine awkwardness (the wrist-touch, the phone call), but the dialogue rarely truly misfires. This makes the chapter feel slightly more polished than the plan intended, though it remains engaging throughout. Overall, this is accomplished, disciplined work that serves the story's larger architecture without overreaching. It is not a masterpiece—the umbrella scene is conventional, the ending image is predictable, and the banter is almost too smooth—but it is genuinely good romantic fiction with real character work underneath the wit. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Model Output:
## Chapter Four: Things That Don’t Go on the Top Shelf By Monday afternoon, Rhys had been employed by Gorse & Ink for forty-three minutes and had already been demoted twice. “You’re putting the children’s poetry beside the gardening,” Cat said. “It’s a shelf.” “It’s a category.” “It’s a shelf with aspirations.” “It’s a shelf with three books about compost.” He looked at the books in his hands. “They’re poems about compost.” “They’re gardening books.” “Some of them rhyme.” Cat took the books from him. “You can carry boxes.” “I’ve been carrying boxes.” “You’ve been carrying one box around while talking to it.” “It had a difficult shape.” Mabli, sitting on a low stool near the till, glanced up from her crossword. “He’s better at talking than lifting.” “I can hear you,” Rhys said. “Then you know where the boxes go.” The shop was preparing for the literary evening on Friday. They had borrowed folding chairs from the chapel, arranged a table for local writers, and received three cartons of children’s books from a school in the next village. The shop looked less like a shop than a room that had been asked to host a wedding and declined. Rhys picked up another box. Cat pointed towards the back. “Those are for the storeroom.” “Where in the storeroom?” “On the left.” “There are three lefts.” “The left after the boiler.” “That’s not a left. That’s an object.” “Take the box.” He did. He carried it through the narrow aisle, ducked beneath the crooked doorframe, and disappeared. Mabli watched him go. “He’s trying very hard,” she said. “He’s being paid in tea.” “He’s not being paid at all.” “Exactly.” A crash came from the back. Cat closed her eyes. “I’m fine,” Rhys called. “Are the books fine?” A pause. “Some of them are emotionally shaken.” Cat went to investigate. Rhys stood beside a collapsed stack of paperbacks, one hand braced against the boiler. A box had split open, scattering novels across the floor. “You’ve killed a detective,” Cat said. “He was already dead.” “He was in a box.” “That’s where they keep most detectives.” She crouched to gather the books. Rhys crouched opposite her. Their hands reached for the same paperback. Neither moved. The title was printed in large silver letters: *The Murder at the Summer House*. “Subtle,” Rhys said. “It’s not mine.” “You sell it.” “I don’t endorse everything I sell.” “That must be difficult for you.” “Not as difficult as it is for you.” He smiled. “You’ve been waiting to say that.” “I’ve had to work around your schedule.” They gathered the books. Rhys kept putting them in the wrong piles. Cat corrected him three times, then stopped. “You’re doing that deliberately.” “I’m creating conversation.” “You’re creating inventory problems.” “Same thing, in a shop.” She looked at him. He was grinning, but there was tiredness beneath it again. He had a bruise beneath one eye now, darker than before. “Have you slept?” she asked. He glanced up. “That sounds like concern.” “It sounds like a question.” “I slept in the car.” “Were you driving?” “No.” “Then that’s not reassuring.” “We had an early call.” “You could say no.” “I could also ask the sun not to rise.” “That’s not the same thing.” “It feels similar at five in the morning.” The bell rang at the front of the shop. A man in a rugby shirt came in, carrying a damp newspaper. He saw Rhys and stopped. “You’re the one from the film.” Rhys stood. “That’s what people keep saying.” The man looked at the scattered books. “You’re working here?” “Apparently.” “Can’t be much of a film if they’ve got you shelving books.” Cat turned away to hide her smile. Rhys did not. The man picked up a flyer for Friday’s event. “Are you reading?” “Possibly.” “People will come for you.” “They might.” “They’ll come for the famous face and leave when the poems start.” The remark was delivered casually, almost kindly. That seemed to make it worse. Rhys laughed. “Well, at least I’ll have a captive audience.” The man shrugged. “You were better in the one with the vineyard.” “I was in two with vineyards.” “The first one.” “That narrows it down.” The man bought a paperback and left. Rhys remained standing. Cat stacked the books. “You were better in the second one.” He looked at her. “You saw them?” “I’ve seen posters.” “You said that yesterday.” “I’m consistent.” “The first one was critically better.” “The second one had the vineyard.” “That was the first one.” “Then you were better in the second one.” He laughed, but it came out thin. Cat watched him put the books down. His hands were too careful. “You don’t have to agree with him,” she said. “I don’t.” “You’re agreeing with him very intensely.” “I’m not.” “You are.” He turned towards her. “What do you want me to do? Argue with every person who thinks I’m not very good?” “No. You could let one person think it without acting as if the roof has come down.” “I’m not acting.” “That’s the trouble. You are.” The words landed. Rhys’s face closed. Mabli shifted on her stool. “I’m going to take the kettle somewhere else.” She lifted it and walked into the back room, leaving the two of them among the books. Rhys folded his arms. “You enjoy this.” “Enjoy what?” “Finding the weak spot.” “I’m not looking for one.” “You keep pressing.” “Because you keep pretending there isn’t one.” His expression sharpened. “You know, most people are capable of having a conversation without turning it into an assessment.” “Most people don’t arrive in my shop with photographers behind them and expect me to lie for them.” “I didn’t expect anything.” “You asked.” “And you said yes.” “I said no, then changed my mind because you looked like you were about to be trampled.” “Thank you.” “You’re welcome.” He looked towards the front window. Outside, the clouds had lowered over the harbour, turning the road grey. “I don’t expect everyone to like me,” he said. Cat waited. He gave a short laugh. “All right. I expect them to like me a little.” “That’s more honest.” “Is it so terrible?” “No. It’s just exhausting.” “For you?” “For you.” He looked back at her. Cat wished she had not said it. Not because it was untrue. Because he looked as though she had put her finger on a bruise. “I don’t know what you want from me,” he said. “That’s not true.” “It is.” “You want me to tell you you’re good.” He flinched. Cat softened, but only slightly. “You probably are.” “That’s generous.” “I didn’t say you were brilliant.” “Cruel.” “You’re good. You’re also a man who has spent a great deal of time being watched. That’s different.” His mouth opened, then shut. For once, no answer arrived. The shop grew quiet around them. Somewhere in the back room, the kettle clicked off. Rain began against the windows. Rhys looked down at the paperback in his hand. “If someone doesn’t like me, I assume they’ve noticed something I’d rather they hadn’t.” Cat leaned against the counter. “That sounds tiring.” “It is.” “Then stop trying to make everyone like you.” He smiled faintly. “You say that as if it’s a decision.” “It is.” “Not for me.” She studied him. The statement was too naked to be a joke, and too quiet to be a performance. “What happens if someone doesn’t?” she asked. He looked at her. “Like you.” His eyes dropped to the books between them. “I don’t know,” he said. The bell above the door shifted in the draft. After closing, they finished setting out chairs. Mabli left with a pointed reminder that Rhys was not to touch the good biscuits. Cat was carrying a stack of books when Rhys reached for it. “I can manage,” she said. “I know.” He took half anyway. They worked in silence for a while. Then Rhys said, “You’re the first person in years who talks to me as if I’m not interesting.” Cat glanced sideways at him. “You are interesting.” His face changed. “Occasionally,” she added. “That’s almost a compliment.” “Don’t get used to it.” He stepped closer. The shop was dim now, the windows black with rain. Cat could smell dust, damp wool, and the faint citrus of his soap. “You know,” he said, “I’m beginning to think you like me.” “You’re mistaken.” “Completely?” “Almost.” “Almost is promising.” “It isn’t.” He smiled. Cat meant to tell him to stop. Instead, she looked at his mouth. Rhys noticed. The kiss was not graceful. His shoulder struck a shelf. A paperback slid to the floor. Cat made a startled sound against his lips, and he pulled back immediately. “Sorry.” “Don’t apologize.” “I hit the shelf.” “The shelf’s fine.” “I meant—” “I know what you meant.” Neither moved. The shop bell trembled above them, though nobody had opened the door. Rhys glanced towards it. “Was that atmospheric?” “No. The latch is broken.” “Of course it is.” Cat looked at the book on the floor. Neither of them picked it up.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of dialogue-driven romantic fiction that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. DIALOGUE AND VOICE The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The opening exchange about the children's poetry and gardening books is genuinely funny and establishes the rhythm immediately. The banter has a natural, unforced quality: "It's a shelf with aspirations" / "It's a shelf with three books about compost" / "Some of them rhyme." This is witty without being precious. The voices are distinct: Rhys deflects with humor, Cat corrects with precision. The exchange about the detective novel ("He was already dead" / "He was in a box" / "That's where they keep most detectives") is similarly well-executed. The chapter also handles the shift from banter to genuine emotional confrontation with skill. The conversation about being liked—"I don't expect everyone to like me" / "All right. I expect them to like me a little"—is the emotional core of the chapter and it lands. The line "If someone doesn't like me, I assume they've noticed something I'd rather they hadn't" is the most nakedly honest thing Rhys says, and it works precisely because it arrives without fanfare. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Both characters are consistent with their profiles. Rhys's ego is fragile in exactly the way described: the man with the rugby shirt delivers a casual dismissal ("They'll come for the famous face and leave when the poems start") and Rhys's laugh "came out thin." His hands become "too careful." Cat notices. This is well-observed. Cat's attraction manifests through physical awareness—she notices the bruise, the smell of his soap, looks at his mouth—while her dialogue remains dry and controlled. This is precisely what the profile calls for. Mabli's brief appearances are well-used. Her line "He's better at talking than lifting" and her strategic exit with the kettle are economical and effective. THE KISS The kiss is handled well. It is awkward, interrupted by a shelf, and neither character names it afterward. The shop bell trembling in the draft is a nice touch—and the author wisely undercuts any atmospheric pretension immediately: "Was that atmospheric?" / "No. The latch is broken." This is exactly the right instinct. The chapter plan called for the kiss to be "interrupted by the shop bell moving in a draft and a stack of paperbacks sliding to the floor" and "neither calls it a kiss afterward." Both are delivered. WEAKNESSES The chapter is not without flaws. Some of the banter in the opening section runs slightly long—the box-carrying sequence with "three lefts" and "that's not a left, that's an object" is amusing but perhaps one exchange too many before the emotional material arrives. The chapter plan called for a child asking whether Rhys is from a superhero film; this is absent, replaced by the rugby-shirt man, which actually works better dramatically. The line "The shop grew quiet around them" is a mild cliché. "The words landed" is a tell-don't-show moment, though a minor one. The observation that Rhys "looked as though she had put her finger on a bruise" is a slightly overworked metaphor but not egregiously so. The ending—"Neither of them picked it up"—is a good closing image, though it risks being slightly too neat as a symbolic gesture. The book on the floor standing in for the unacknowledged kiss is clear enough to work without being heavy-handed. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The chapter is emotionally engaging. The shift from comedy to vulnerability is managed without melodrama. The moment where Rhys says "I don't know" in response to what happens if someone doesn't like him is genuinely affecting. Cat's softening—"You probably are" (good)—followed immediately by "I didn't say you were brilliant" (cruel) is perfectly calibrated. FOLLOWING THE PLAN The chapter follows its plan closely. The plan called for: Rhys being bad at practical tasks but enthusiastic (delivered), a local man dismissing his acting (delivered), Rhys becoming unsettled and withdrawn (delivered), Cat telling him he cannot demand universal affection (delivered), the argument being sharper than earlier banter (delivered), Rhys admitting he doesn't know how to be disliked (delivered), Cat's response not being flattering (delivered), and the kiss between bookcases interrupted by the bell and falling paperbacks (delivered). The child from the plan is absent, but the replacement works better. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is confident, well-crafted work. The dialogue is the best element: specific, funny, and emotionally honest. The characters are consistent and the chapter advances their relationship credibly. The weaknesses are minor: occasional over-reliance on cliché, one or two tell-don't-show moments, and a slightly prolonged opening sequence. But these are small complaints against a chapter that largely achieves what it intends. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Five: No Comment Is Still a Comment The photographs appeared on Tuesday morning. Cat found out because Mabli came into the shop carrying a newspaper in one hand and a tray of coffees in the other. She put the coffees down. Then she put the newspaper face-down. “Don’t,” Cat said. “I haven’t said anything.” “You’ve brought the newspaper in like it contains a body.” “It contains Rhys.” “That’s worse.” Mabli turned it over. There he was, blurred slightly by rain, standing between two bookshop windows with his mouth on Cat’s. One of his hands was at her waist. Cat’s hand was curled in the front of his coat. The photograph made them look deliberate. They had not been deliberate. They had been startled, off-balance, and standing beside a shelf of detective novels. The headline read: **STAR’S SECRET WELSH ROMANCE?** Cat picked up one of the coffees. “Which one is mine?” “The one without sugar.” “You put sugar in yours.” “I need it.” Cat took a sip. It was hers. The shop bell rang. A woman in a raincoat entered, saw Cat, and immediately looked at the newspaper. “Is it him?” she asked. “No,” Cat said. The woman blinked. “Sorry,” Cat added. “Yes.” “Is he here?” “No.” “Will he be?” “Not if he has any sense.” The woman looked disappointed. She bought a postcard of a lighthouse and left. Mabli unfolded the newspaper. “You’re very pale.” “I’m annoyed.” “You’re pale when you’re annoyed.” “I’m not a tomato.” “No. You’re more of a mushroom.” Cat took the newspaper from her and shoved it beneath the till. The phone rang. She answered. “Gorse & Ink.” “Hello,” said a woman’s voice. “Is this the bookshop where Rhys Morgan has been staying?” “He hasn’t been staying here.” “Is he there now?” “No.” “Can I speak to Catrin Evans?” “You’re speaking to her.” “Oh. I’m from *Celebrity Watch Weekly*. We’d love a quote about your relationship.” Cat looked at Mabli. Mabli mouthed, “Charge them.” “I don’t have a relationship,” Cat said. There was a pause. “The photographs suggest otherwise.” “The photographs suggest poor lighting.” “Would you describe Rhys as your boyfriend?” “No.” “Are you seeing each other?” “Not currently.” “Are you denying the relationship?” “I’m ending this call.” She hung up. The phone rang again. Mabli picked it up. “Gorse & Ink.” She listened. “No,” she said. “The actor is not available. Nor is the woman he was kissing. Try the theatre.” She hung up and pushed the phone beneath the counter. “It’s going to be a long day,” she said. At noon, three young women came in and bought nothing. One of them asked Cat whether Rhys was as handsome in person. Another asked whether he had paid for the books. The third took a photograph of the poetry section without permission. Cat asked them to leave. At half past one, a man from a local radio station stood outside the window holding a microphone. At two, Rhys arrived through the back door. Cat was in the storeroom, trying to locate a box of Welsh-language children’s books. She heard his voice before she saw him. “I’m sorry.” She turned. He had no coat on, and his hair was damp. His face looked drawn. “That’s not a good enough opening,” she said. “I know.” “You’ve had time to prepare?” “I’ve had six hours.” “Then I’m disappointed.” He stopped just inside the door. “The photographs were taken after the event.” “I know when they were taken.” “I didn’t know anyone was there.” “There are people outside now.” His expression tightened. “I can deal with that.” “That’s what I’m afraid of.” He took a breath. “My publicist wants to put out a statement.” “No.” “I haven’t agreed.” “Good.” “She thinks it would be better to control the story.” Cat laughed once. “There isn’t a story.” “There is now.” “There are photographs.” “Which is how stories start.” “Not in my shop.” “Cat.” She looked at him. He almost never used her name. It had an effect she did not appreciate. “I don’t want them turning this into something ugly,” he said. “They’ve already turned it into something stupid.” “I can keep it stupid.” “That’s a compelling promise.” He rubbed a hand over his face. “They’re going to keep asking. If I say nothing, they’ll decide what the silence means.” “Let them.” “They won’t just leave you alone.” “They are already not leaving me alone.” “I know.” “No, you don’t. You can walk out of here and get into a car. I have to keep selling books while strangers ask whether you’ve slept in my bed.” His eyes dropped briefly. Cat saw it. “Don’t,” she said. “I wasn’t—” “You were.” “I was thinking that I haven’t.” “That doesn’t improve anything.” “No.” He stepped further into the storeroom. “There’s an interview this afternoon. They’ll ask about you.” “Then don’t talk about me.” “I can say you’re a friend.” “I’m not your friend.” The words came too quickly. His face altered. Cat hated the small silence that followed. “I mean,” she said, “I’m not a public category.” “I know.” “Do you?” “Yes.” “Because you keep talking as if the solution is finding the right wording.” He looked towards the boxes, the shelves, anywhere but her. “If I say nothing, they’ll invent something.” “If you say something, they’ll print it.” “I can keep your name out of it.” “You can’t keep me out of it if you’re standing in front of a camera talking about the woman in the bookshop.” “They already know your name.” “Because you came here.” He looked back at her. “That’s not fair.” “No,” she said. “It isn’t.” For a moment neither spoke. Then Rhys said, “I came here because I needed somewhere no one knew me.” Cat stared at him. “People knew you.” “You know what I mean.” “Yes,” she said. “They knew the version of you that you brought in.” He flinched. She continued, quieter. “You came in because I wasn’t impressed. You liked that. You liked being able to stop performing. But the moment the world noticed you were here, you started trying to manage what I would become in the story.” “I’m trying to protect you.” “From what?” “From them.” “You don’t get to protect me by making decisions about me.” “I’m not making decisions about you.” “You’re deciding what can be said, what can’t, what the public should think, and which version of this is safest.” “That’s my job.” “It’s not mine.” His mouth tightened. “I can’t pretend none of this is happening.” “I’m not asking you to pretend.” “What are you asking?” Cat looked at the back door, at the rain beyond it. “I’m asking you not to turn me into a solution.” He said nothing. The phone rang in the shop. Neither moved. It rang again. Rhys took one step towards the door. “Don’t,” Cat said. He stopped. “Not yet.” “Cat—” “Please.” The word changed the room. It was the first time she had asked him for anything without making it sound like an instruction. Rhys nodded. The phone stopped ringing. After a moment, he said, “I’ll tell them you’re important to me.” Cat’s stomach tightened. “No.” “I won’t say your name.” “That isn’t the point.” “I need to say something.” “Why?” “Because if I don’t, they’ll say I’m ashamed of you.” Cat stared at him. He looked genuinely frightened now. Not of the photographers. Of the story. “You’re afraid they’ll think badly of you,” she said. His jaw worked. “That’s the truth.” “Yes.” He looked down. Then he nodded once. “Yes.” The admission should have softened her. Instead, it made her tired. “Go home, Rhys.” “Cat.” “Go.” He stood there for another second, then left through the back door. Five minutes later, a car pulled away from the alley. Cat returned to the shop. Mabli was behind the till, holding the telephone receiver. “Another journalist,” she said. Cat took it from her and hung up. Outside, a camera flashed. That evening, Rhys gave an interview. He did not name Cat. He said she was “someone very important” and that he hoped people would respect their privacy. By midnight, the phrase was everywhere. By morning, somebody had found her full name.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Five is a strong piece of dialogue-driven romantic drama that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest asset. The exchange between Cat and Rhys in the storeroom is genuinely accomplished — it moves through several emotional registers without feeling mechanical. Lines like "You're afraid they'll think badly of you" landing as a revelation rather than an accusation, and Rhys's admission "Yes" after a pause, feel earned and psychologically true. The chapter avoids the trap of making either character simply right or wrong. The opening sequence with Mabli is excellent. The newspaper-as-body metaphor ("like it contains a body") is organic and character-consistent rather than forced. The mushroom/tomato exchange is the right kind of light comedy — it doesn't undercut the tension but provides necessary breathing room. Mabli's line "Try the theatre" when answering the phone is a small gem. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The publicist's controlled narrative, Rhys's ambiguous statement, the invasion of Cat's privacy, and Cat's accusation that he turned her into a solution — all are present and handled with more nuance than the plan's summary suggested they would be. The pacing is well-managed. The chapter moves from the newspaper reveal through escalating intrusion (the customers, the radio man, the phone calls) to the confrontation, and finally to the devastating coda where Rhys's statement backfires exactly as Cat predicted. This structural irony — he says he'll protect her, she says he can't, and then he fails to — is clean and effective. Cat's line "I'm not your friend" followed by the correction "I'm not a public category" is one of the chapter's best moments. It shows her catching herself, which is consistent with her character profile's note that she reveals attraction through small mistakes and corrections. The ending is particularly strong. "By morning, somebody had found her full name" is a genuinely cold, effective final line. It doesn't editorialize; it simply delivers the consequence. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states. "His face looked drawn" and "His expression tightened" are functional but not especially vivid. The plan called for Cat's inward fluster to appear through physical reactions rather than declarations — this is mostly honored, but there are moments where the narration states emotional conditions rather than dramatizing them. "The admission should have softened her. Instead, it made her tired" is a slight lapse into telling. It's not egregious, but it's the kind of sentence that explains what the reader should already be feeling from the dialogue. The "Please" moment — "The word changed the room" — is the chapter's most overtly literary gesture and it's borderline. It works, but it's the kind of sentence that draws attention to itself in a way the rest of the chapter doesn't. It's not quite purple prose, but it's the closest the chapter comes. The three young women who come in at noon feel slightly schematic — they exist to demonstrate the invasion of privacy rather than as individuals. This is a minor complaint given the chapter's length and purpose. Rhys's motivation for giving the statement is clear, but the chapter could have done slightly more to show his genuine belief that he was protecting her rather than just his fear of looking bad. The plan notes he "believes he is protecting her by refusing to name her" — the chapter does convey this, but his self-deception could be rendered with a touch more complexity. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Both characters are highly consistent with their profiles. Rhys's fear of being seen badly ("You're afraid they'll think badly of you") is exactly the ego fragility described. Cat's precision under pressure, her refusal to be managed, and her one moment of vulnerability ("Please") are all true to her profile. The chapter correctly shows Rhys trying to manage the situation rather than listen, which is his central flaw. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely and executes most beats with more texture than the plan's summary suggested. The plan's note that "Rhys arrives with solutions — security, a publicist, a possible legal warning — and begins managing the problem without asking what she wants" is slightly simplified in execution (he doesn't bring security or legal warnings), but the emotional truth is preserved. OVERALL: This is a well-crafted chapter that demonstrates genuine skill in dialogue, pacing, and character consistency. Its weaknesses are minor — occasional telling, one slightly self-conscious sentence, and schematic minor characters. The ending is genuinely effective. It's not a masterpiece, but it's solidly accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Six: The Things He Almost Buys Rhys left Penmorfa before dawn. He told himself it was because the production had moved inland, that he had a call time, that people were waiting for him. All of this was true. None of it was the reason he drove past Gorse & Ink without stopping. At seven that morning, Nia found him sitting in the trailer with his script open on his knees. “You’ve been looking at the same page for twenty minutes,” she said. “I’m learning it.” “You’re holding it upside down.” He turned the script over. Nia put down the coffee she had brought him. She was three years younger than Rhys, shorter, sharper, and unimpressed by most forms of celebrity, including his. “How bad is it?” she asked. “What?” “The thing you’re pretending not to think about.” “I’m thinking about the scene.” “You’re thinking about Cat.” “I can think about two things.” “You can barely think about one before lunch.” He picked up the coffee. “She’s angry.” “She should be.” “I know.” Nia sat opposite him. “That’s new.” “What is?” “You admitting you’ve done something wrong without first explaining why you were forced to do it.” “I wasn’t forced.” “No.” He stared into the coffee. “I was trying to keep them from making it worse.” “You made it worse.” “I know.” Nia leaned back. “You also gave a very romantic interview about a woman whose surname you refused to say.” “It wasn’t romantic.” “It was on every entertainment programme before breakfast.” “They’re exaggerating.” “They’re doing their jobs.” “They’re not.” “No,” Nia said. “They’re doing what you do. Making something appear more controlled than it is.” Rhys looked at her. “That’s unfair.” “It’s accurate.” He wanted to argue. He could have explained the photographers, the publicist, the questions, the panic that had filled the small room behind Cat’s shop. He could explain that he had meant to protect her. He could explain that the phrase had seemed harmless when it was in his mouth. Instead, he said, “I hurt her.” Nia nodded. The production assistant knocked on the trailer door. “They’re ready for you.” Rhys stood. “Try not to charm anyone out of a serious conversation today,” Nia said. “I don’t do that.” “You’re doing it now.” At Gorse & Ink, Cat was trying to assemble a display of children’s books while three people watched her through the window. The story had moved on from Rhys’s mystery romance. It had become a local curiosity. Someone had discovered her old university photograph online. Someone else had posted a review of the shop complaining that the owner was “arrogant and clearly using the actor for publicity.” Cat deleted the review from the screen and continued arranging books. Mabli came in carrying a basket of bread. “You’ve put the dragons beside the geography.” “They’re both travel.” “Dragons aren’t real.” “Neither is half the geography section.” Mabli set down the basket. “The landlord called.” Cat’s hands paused. “About the rent?” “About the rent.” “How much?” Mabli named the figure. Cat stared at the display. A picture book about a girl who built a lighthouse had slipped sideways. “That’s not possible.” “It is. He’s written it down.” “I know what rent is.” “You’ve been saying that for three weeks.” Cat pushed the book upright. “The event will help.” “The event will raise some money.” “It might raise enough.” “It might raise enough for the new boiler.” Cat looked at her. Mabli sighed. “I’m not saying give up.” “The children’s group is expecting it.” “I know.” “The writers have arranged transport.” “I know.” “Then it’s happening.” “I know.” Mabli began unpacking the bread. “You’re very impressive when you’re furious.” “I’m not furious.” “You’re slicing cheese with the concentration of a murderer.” Cat looked down. The knife had gone through the cheese and into the chopping board. She removed it carefully. At noon, Rhys’s publicist called. Cat let it ring. At twelve fifteen, she called again. Cat answered. “What?” “Catrin? It’s Amelia Price. I represent Rhys.” “I know.” “I wanted to discuss the recent coverage.” “I don’t.” “I understand you’re upset.” “No, you don’t.” There was a pause. Amelia had the polished patience of someone who had been trained to survive famous people. “Rhys is very concerned about your privacy.” “Then he should stop talking about me.” “He was trying to make clear that the relationship is meaningful.” “It isn’t a relationship.” Another pause. “I’m not asking you for a statement,” Amelia said. “I’m asking whether you would consider a controlled photograph. Something tasteful. It would discourage speculation.” Cat laughed. “A photograph of me and a man I’ve asked not to come into my shop?” “It could be framed positively.” “I don’t want to be framed.” “I’m not sure that’s what I meant.” “It’s what you said.” Amelia exhaled. “Rhys is not used to this kind of attention.” Cat looked at the newspaper beneath the till. “Rhys is extremely used to attention.” “Not this kind.” “Neither am I.” “We could arrange security.” “I own a bell and a broom.” “Catrin—” “Do not call me that as if you’re about to offer me a settlement.” “I’m trying to help.” “So is everyone.” She hung up. Five minutes later, she found an envelope pushed beneath the front door. Inside was a cheque. No note. No name. The amount was large enough to cover the event, the boiler, and several months of rent. Cat sat on the floor behind the till. Mabli found her there. “What is it?” Cat handed over the cheque. Mabli read the amount and whistled. “Absolutely not,” Cat said. “I didn’t say anything.” “You were thinking it.” “I was thinking that your landlord would accept it.” “It’s from him.” “Is it?” “Who else?” “It could be the Pope.” “The Pope hasn’t been kissing me in a bookshop.” Mabli considered the cheque. “He’s trying.” “He’s trying to buy his way out.” “Perhaps he’s trying to help.” “He doesn’t get to decide what help looks like.” Mabli looked at her. “You say that as if you don’t decide what help looks like for everyone else.” Cat took the cheque back. “I’m returning it.” “Good.” She put it in an envelope. Then she sat there for another ten minutes without moving. That afternoon, Rhys filmed a scene in which his character stood on a cliff and declared that love required courage. On the third take, the director stopped him. “You’re saying the line,” she said, “but you’re not in it.” “I’m in it.” “You’re looking at the sea as if it owes you money.” The crew laughed. Rhys did not. He looked down at the script. The line was highlighted. Love required courage. He thought of Cat saying, *You don’t get to protect me by making decisions about me.* He thought of the cheque. At the end of the day, he asked Nia to cancel it. “Did she send it back?” Nia asked. “She hasn’t seen it.” “Then you need to tell her.” “I can’t.” “You can.” “I’ll make it worse.” “You already did that.” He looked at her. Nia picked up her coat. “You keep trying to find a way to help without having to stand in front of the person you hurt.” “I don’t know what to say.” “Good.” “What does that mean?” “It means you might finally have to say something true.” That night, Rhys opened the poetry book Cat had recommended. A receipt fell from between the pages. On the back, in Cat’s handwriting, she had written: *Read the whole thing before deciding you understand it.* He smiled despite himself. Then he turned to the first page. He read slowly. Not because the poems were difficult, but because he had spent years looking for the line he could use. This time, he tried to read without taking anything. Near midnight, he closed the book. The next morning, he drove back to Penmorfa. He did not call Cat. He did not tell Amelia. He did not bring flowers, security, or another cheque. At half past six, he stood outside Gorse & Ink in the rain, looking at the dark windows. The shop bell was still. Rhys put his hands in his pockets and waited.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Six is a transitional chapter that handles the difficult work of keeping both characters sympathetic while Rhys is in the wrong and Cat is in pain. The model executes this with considerable skill, though not without some weaknesses worth examining. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The Rhys-Nia exchanges are particularly well-crafted. Nia functions as a truth-teller without becoming a mouthpiece for the author's moral agenda. Lines like "You're holding it upside down" and "You're doing it now" when Rhys denies charming people out of serious conversations are economical and sharp. The rhythm of their exchanges feels genuinely conversational rather than constructed. The observation that Rhys admitting wrongdoing "without first explaining why he were forced to do it" is "new" is a precise character note that rewards the reader's investment in the character profiles. The Cat-Amelia phone call is also well-handled. "I don't want to be framed" / "I'm not sure that's what I meant" / "It's what you said" is a small gem of dialogue that reveals Cat's precision under pressure without making her seem merely combative. The exchange earns its lightness. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The anonymous cheque, Nia challenging Rhys about secrecy, the poetry book with Cat's note, and Rhys returning to stand outside the shop in the rain are all present and executed with reasonable fidelity to the plan's intentions. The plan specified that Rhys should withdraw the offer before it can be accepted, and the chapter handles this correctly, though the mechanism (Rhys asking Nia to cancel it after filming) is slightly compressed. The chapter's weaknesses are worth noting. The cliff scene where Rhys films a declaration that "love required courage" is the most obvious structural choice in the piece - the ironic juxtaposition of performed romantic sentiment against genuine emotional failure is a well-worn device. It doesn't fail exactly, but it doesn't surprise either. The director's line "You're looking at the sea as if it owes you money" is a good line, but the scene as a whole is the chapter's most predictable moment. The Mabli-Cat exchange about the cheque is good but slightly over-explains. "He doesn't get to decide what help looks like" / "You say that as if you don't decide what help looks like for everyone else" is a strong reversal, but the chapter then has Cat sit for ten minutes without moving, which is a tell-don't-show moment that undercuts the preceding dialogue's work. The reader has already understood Cat's conflict; the extended pause announces it. The ending - Rhys standing outside the shop in the rain, hands in pockets, waiting - is effective and earns its quiet. The plan called for him to arrive after closing and wait outside in the rain, and the execution is restrained. The final line "The shop bell was still" is a nice callback to the bell motif without being heavy-handed. The receipt falling from the poetry book with Cat's handwritten note is a small, well-placed detail. It's the kind of thing that feels true to both characters: Cat's note is practical and slightly combative even in absence, and Rhys's response (smiling despite himself, then reading without trying to extract usable material) shows genuine movement without announcing it as a breakthrough. The chapter is not amateurish. The prose is clean and controlled. There is no purple prose and very little forced metaphor. The "murderer" line about Cat slicing cheese is the chapter's best comic moment and lands cleanly. The writing is occasionally slightly flat in its transitions, but this is a minor complaint. One structural concern: the chapter covers a lot of ground (Rhys on set, Cat in the shop, the publicist call, the cheque, the filming scene, Nia's second conversation, the poetry book, the return) and some of these beats feel slightly rushed. The plan's intention was approximately 1,000 words, and the chapter is longer, but the pacing still occasionally feels like it's checking boxes rather than dwelling where it should. Overall, this is a competent, often genuinely good chapter that serves its narrative function well. It is not a masterpiece, but it is professional, character-consistent, and emotionally honest. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Seven: The Apology Without Flowers Cat found him at seven in the morning. She had come to the shop early because the boiler had begun making a noise like a trapped animal, and because she had slept badly, and because she did not want to wonder whether he would appear. Rhys stood beneath the shop awning, damp at the shoulders. He had no umbrella. “You look ridiculous,” she said. “Good morning.” “You’ve been here all night?” “No.” “How long?” “Long enough to regret wearing suede.” She looked at him through the glass. He did not smile. That was worse than the rain. Cat unlocked the door. “Come in before you start attracting sympathy.” He stepped inside. The bell rang above them. For a moment, they stood on opposite sides of the shop, surrounded by half-filled boxes and chairs waiting for Friday’s event. “I brought the cheque back,” he said. “I didn’t send it.” “I know.” “Then why are you telling me?” “Because I was going to give it to you.” “That’s not better.” “No.” He took a breath. Cat noticed that he had prepared something and was trying not to say it in the prepared order. “I’m sorry,” he said. She waited. He looked at the counter, then at her. “That’s not enough.” “No.” “I talked about you without asking. I thought not using your name meant I was protecting you. It didn’t. I made you part of a story because I was frightened of what people would say if I didn’t.” Cat leaned against the till. “What were you frightened they’d say?” “That you were using me.” She blinked. He gave a small, humorless laugh. “That sounds ugly when I say it out loud.” “It was ugly before you said it.” “I know.” “And did you believe it?” “No.” “But you thought other people might.” “Yes.” “Why did that matter?” He looked away. Cat waited. “Because if they believed it,” he said, “then I would have to convince them they were wrong. And if I couldn’t, it would mean they were right about me.” “That you’d been used?” “That I’d been stupid.” The answer was so plain that Cat did not know what to do with it. Rhys rubbed his thumb over the edge of the counter. “I thought if I controlled the story, I could control what it meant. I didn’t think about what it would mean for you.” “No.” “I didn’t.” The boiler clicked behind them. Cat glanced towards it. “You can hear that, can’t you?” “Yes.” “It’s going to explode.” “Is that a metaphor?” “It’s a boiler.” “Right.” She nearly smiled. Stopped herself. He noticed but did not try to claim it. “I’m not asking you to forgive me,” he said. “Good.” “I’m asking what I can do.” “Nothing.” “That can’t be the whole answer.” “It might be.” He nodded, though his face tightened. Cat looked at the boxes. “The event is still happening.” “I know.” “The sponsor withdrew.” “I know.” “You know a great deal for someone who isn’t allowed to talk about me.” “I’ve been reading the local paper.” “Why?” “Because I was trying to understand what I’d done.” “That’s not how you understand things.” “No,” he said. “Usually I make a phone call and arrange a solution.” “And did you?” “I tried.” Her gaze sharpened. “I sent a cheque,” he said. “Without asking. I canceled it.” “Why?” “Because Nia said it was another way of deciding for you.” Cat looked towards the window. The rain had softened to mist. “That was the right answer,” she said. “I know.” “You don’t get credit for eventually arriving at the right answer.” “I know.” He said it without bitterness. That was new. She walked to the nearest box and opened it. “The chairs need collecting from the chapel.” Rhys looked at her. “You said you wanted to know what you can do.” “Yes.” “You can carry chairs.” “I can carry chairs.” “And the microphone needs testing.” “I can test a microphone.” “You’ve never tested a microphone in your life.” “I’ve spoken into several.” “Then you’re overqualified.” A faint smile appeared. She pointed at the box. “No flirting.” “I wasn’t.” “You were about to.” “I was about to ask where the chairs go.” “Downstairs.” “Of course they do.” They spent the next hour moving furniture. Rhys was, Cat discovered, good at carrying chairs and terrible at stacking them. He listened when corrected. He did not make jokes every time she spoke. Once, he put a chair down in the wrong place, saw her expression, and moved it without being told. At the chapel, an elderly volunteer recognized him and asked whether he was the man from the vineyard. “The second one,” Rhys said. “You were better in the first.” “Thank you.” Cat watched him accept the judgment with a bow.
