Arthur has not become rich. - He has not liberated all of Mars. - But the corporations can no longer claim that Mars has no legal voice. - Final image: - Arthur repairs an old geological gauge while the first Martian assembly begins broadcasting from underground. - Beneath the settlement, the sealed Azra vein continues its slow, dangerous decay. - Optional final line or implication: - A distant transmission reports that an off-world observatory has detected the Azra signature. - The immediate gambit has succeeded, but the wider interstellar game has only begun.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Human Approach A human writer approaching this piece would probably begin less with the full political architecture and more with a felt situation: Arthur is sitting in a transfer vessel, under arrest without anyone admitting that he is under arrest. He is tired, angry, and trying to determine whether he has made a terrible mistake. He does not initially think in terms of “interstellar strategic resources.” He thinks about his house, whether Mara will have remembered to shut the west pressure door, whether the men who took him touched his tools, and whether his daughter is safe. That human starting point matters. The premise is large, but the story should be experienced through one person’s partial knowledge. The reader should learn about Azra, corporations, Antares, labor contracts, and the arms race because Arthur is forced to encounter them—not because the story needs to provide a complete briefing. A human writer might also allow the story’s understanding to evolve during drafting. The original prompt offers a rich set of possibilities, but a finished short story should not feel as though it is displaying all of them. The writer would likely select one emotional and thematic center: - Arthur discovering that the thing beneath his home is more valuable than his life. - Arthur realizing that every faction speaks about “protecting Mars” while treating Martians as property. - Arthur being forced to decide whether the truth is safer than a useful lie. - Arthur’s practical knowledge becoming politically dangerous. The story should be built around the pressure of that choice rather than around the need to visit every planned faction and subplot. ## Starting with Character, Not Information A human approach would ask: - What does Arthur want before the story begins? - What ordinary life is interrupted? - What does he fear losing? - What does he misunderstand? - What private shame or regret makes the political offer tempting? Possible answers: - Arthur wants to finish reinforcing an old mine shaft before the next dust season. - He wants to buy out his daughter’s labor contract. - He has spent years avoiding political involvement because every previous dispute ended with someone losing oxygen, wages, or a home. - He believes that keeping his head down has protected his people. - He is secretly proud of having found Azra and ashamed that he immediately calculated what it might be worth. Those details will make the larger stakes believable. If Arthur begins as a fully formed symbol of Martian independence, he will feel designed rather than lived-in. ## Letting the World Be Seen Imperfectly A human writer would probably resist explaining colonial Mars all at once. The reader can infer much from small frictions: - Arthur’s land grant is printed on paper because the settlement’s computer archive was corrupted. - His workers speak of “lease time” rather than employment. - The armed retinue knows exactly where the spare oxygen cylinders are kept. - An Antares officer apologizes for entering Arthur’s home without removing his boots because the floor is already contaminated with dust. - Arthur’s legal status depends on a charter written before most current settlers were born. - A corporate representative knows his daughter’s contract number but not her name. These details create a society without a lecture. The story’s big-picture world building should emerge from the way institutions intrude into ordinary life. ## Human Dialogue The opening meeting should not consist of characters stating their positions in polished thematic language. Humans evade, interrupt, misunderstand, flatter, and become petty at inappropriate moments. Arthur might ask a straightforward question and receive three different answers: - “Am I under arrest?” - “You are being escorted.” - “That wasn’t my question.” - “No.” - “Then why are there six armed men in my kitchen?” - “For your protection.” - “From whom?” - “That is what we hope to establish.” That kind of exchange carries information while preserving the awkwardness of real speech. A human writer would also give characters distinct conversational habits: - Arthur answers questions with questions when he feels cornered. - Vale uses legal euphemisms and rarely says “we want.” - Sato starts correcting technical inaccuracies, then stops himself because he realizes the correction might be dangerous. - Mara speaks in clipped practical terms because the communications system is slow and unreliable. - Orra uses warmth as a sales technique and becomes visibly irritated when Arthur refuses to be impressed. The dialogue should not make every character equally articulate. If all characters express the story’s themes with perfect clarity, the result will feel like a panel discussion rather than a crisis. # Telltale LLM Approaches and Why They Could Fail Here ## 1. Safe, Symmetrical Morality A common LLM approach is to make every faction “complicated” in a balanced way: - The corporations are bad but understandable. - Antares is authoritarian but well-intentioned. - The workers are oppressed but occasionally unreasonable. - Arthur sees the validity of all sides. - The final settlement gives everyone a little of what they want. This can produce fairness without drama. Moral ambiguity is not the same as moral symmetry. Some institutions may be sincerely concerned about war while still using armed coercion and indentured labor. The story should not soften exploitation merely to appear nuanced. A stronger approach would let the factions have rational motives but unequal moral standing. The corporations can fear catastrophe and still be responsible for treating people as leased equipment. Antares can prevent an arms race and still be pursuing imperial control. Their motives explain their behavior; they do not excuse it. ## 2. Wrapping Everything Up The current plan risks ending with: - A treaty. - An elected Martian authority. - The end of indenture. - The mine safely sealed. - Arthur restored to his home. - A hopeful message about citizenship. That is emotionally satisfying, but potentially too neat. In a colonial setting, a single mine crisis should not solve a system built over generations. The story can end with a genuine victory while leaving the larger situation unstable. A more human ending might include unresolved consequences: - The treaty recognizes Martian authority only provisionally. - Some workers are freed, but others remain trapped in corporate jurisdictions. - Arthur’s homestead is legally protected but physically damaged. - Sato’s scientific data has been published, but rival powers can now develop safer Azra technology. - The new Martian authority is already arguing over who gets representation. - Arthur has won a political opening and lost the private life he hoped to preserve. Hope is stronger when it has a cost. ## 3. Treating the Plan as a Checklist The eight-chapter plan contains many attractive elements: - Transfer vessel. - Armed retinue. - Antares offer. - Corporate offer. - Labor representative. - Scientific disaster. - Secret messages. - Prototype drive. - Mine rescue. - Public broadcast. - Treaty. - Possible final transmission. The danger is that the story becomes a sequence of required appearances. Each chapter may perform its assigned function, but the narrative could feel mechanically assembled: 1. Introduce mystery. 2. Explain offer. 3. Reveal legal problem. 4. Introduce rival. 5. Reveal scientific danger. 6. Formulate plan. 7. Execute plan. 8. Resolve consequences. Human stories often contain unevenness. One meeting may last longer than expected. A minor character may become important. Arthur’s plan may emerge from a mistake rather than a formal strategic calculation. A chapter may end not on the planned revelation but on an emotional rupture. The outline is useful as scaffolding, but the finished story should not announce its structure. ## 4. Exposition Disguised as Dialogue The plan correctly calls for a dialogue-heavy early scene, but there is a risk that every question Arthur asks exists only to let another character explain the world. For example: - “What is Azra?” - “It is a rare metal that accelerates radioactive decay.” - “Why is it valuable?” - “It can fuel drives and weapons.” - “Who wants it?” - “Corporations, Antares, and the colonies.” This is efficient but artificial. Arthur is a mining engineer and already knows something about Azra. He should not ask questions the reader needs answered unless the answer is genuinely useful to him. Better exposition would involve disputes over facts: - Vale calls the vein “a strategic deposit.” - Arthur calls it “a pressure trap.” - Sato says both descriptions are incomplete. - Arthur realizes the official reports use the wrong term because the wrong term gives Antares legal advantages. - A technical detail becomes evidence of political manipulation. The reader learns the science because different characters are trying to use different versions of it. ## 5. Impressive Language at the Expense of Readability The premise invites grand prose about Mars, empire, and radioactive decay. An LLM may overproduce phrases such as: - “the red planet’s blood-dark veins” - “the cold arithmetic of empire” - “the metallic ghost of a dead sun” - “history had come knocking in a helmet of polished steel” One or two vivid images can help, but sustained metaphorical language would make Arthur’s world feel generic and over-written. Classic science fiction often gains force from plain statements placed beside strange facts. A human writer might write: > The men had brought rifles into his kitchen. They had apologized for the inconvenience. That is more memorable than an elaborate description of authoritarianism. The story should trust concrete details. ## 6. Generic Retro-Futurism The current plan includes many retrofuturist objects: - Vacuum tubes. - Punch cards. - Analog gauges. - Magnetic tape. - Mechanical doors. - Transfer ships. If used indiscriminately, these become decorative tokens rather than part of the world. Human world building asks what those technologies do socially. Examples: - A punched legal record is difficult to alter, which makes it valuable evidence. - Magnetic tape degrades in radiation, so Arthur has kept duplicate records in a sealed cabinet. - The transfer vessel’s mechanical controls are old but more trustworthy than the new automated systems. - Corporate systems use antiquated computers because the colonial charter requires “human-readable” records. - A pressure door cannot be opened remotely because a worker died when a remote override failed. The retrofuturist technology should create constraints, not simply atmosphere. ## 7. Artificially Precise Science The outline says that Azra accelerates radioactive decay, destabilizes nearby isotopes, and may affect materials around it. This is a strong premise, but adding new effects whenever the plot needs a danger could make the science feel arbitrary. A common LLM failure is pseudo-technical escalation: - Azra accelerates decay. - Then it ages metal. - Then it affects batteries. - Then it destabilizes geology. - Then it alters time or matter. - Then it produces a planetary chain reaction. The story should establish a limited set of rules early and obey them. If metal becomes brittle, the reader should understand that this results from radioactive contamination or isotope imbalance, not from a new supernatural property. If a containment system fails, the failure should be mechanically traceable. The more extraordinary the premise, the more disciplined the consequences should be. ## 8. The Genius Protagonist Trap Arthur’s proposed gambit risks making him too competent. He may: - Understand the science. - Read the law. - Outsmart Antares. - Outsmart corporations. - Coordinate the miners. - Engineer the containment solution. - Deliver the political settlement. That creates a protagonist who is useful everywhere and vulnerable nowhere. A human character should have uneven competence. Arthur might understand mining but not diplomacy. He may misread Vale’s motives. He may mishandle his first public statement. His gambit may partially succeed because Mara, Sato, and the workers contribute things he cannot. He should also make a mistake with consequences. Perhaps he initially accepts the Antares offer because it would free his workers, then realizes the agreement legally transfers control of the settlement. Or he believes the corporate extraction team can be stopped by shutting down the machinery, only to discover that the workers are trapped below. Arthur’s intelligence will be more convincing if it operates within limits. ## 9. Last-Minute Revelations The anonymous messages and hidden Antares faction are useful, but they could become a familiar mystery mechanism: - A warning appears. - Arthur follows it. - Someone knows a secret sample detail. - The “real” Antares scientists are revealed. - The true threat is explained. This risks making the plot feel like a succession of withheld facts. A human story usually lets the reader understand some things before Arthur does and leaves other things uncertain. The first warning should perhaps be less useful than it appears. It might say: > You have been taken for the wrong reason. Arthur assumes this means the retinue has confused him with someone else. Later he realizes it means the opposite: Antares believes it knows what he found, but does not understand why it matters. Similarly, Sato should not simply become the source of the correct scientific explanation. He may have participated in concealing information and therefore be personally compromised. ## 10. Convenient Public Broadcasting The plan’s climax relies on Arthur broadcasting the geological data, contracts, and evidence to every faction simultaneously. That is narratively efficient but potentially convenient. The story should establish why this is possible: - Mara has access to a redundant settler radio network. - Corporate and Antares communications are incompatible, but a maintenance beacon can transmit on all of them. - The broadcast reaches only Mars, not the entire interstellar community. - The data is incomplete, and Arthur must choose what to release. - Broadcasting the information has a cost, such as revealing the location of vulnerable settlements or exposing workers to retaliation. The broadcast should be a risky action, not a magic “truth button.” ## 11. Overly Polished Themes The intended themes are strong: - Ownership versus stewardship. - Empire versus citizenship. - Knowledge as leverage. - The individual caught between systems. - Cooperation forced by mutual danger. But if characters repeatedly articulate those themes, the story may feel like an essay. Arthur should not need to say, “A resource that can only be owned by a tyrant is not freedom.” That is a useful planning sentence, but in the finished story it may be more powerful if he says something ordinary: > “You can’t lease a shaft you can’t safely enter.” Or: > “If your plan depends on everyone else dying before you do, it isn’t a plan. It’s a preference.” The thematic meaning can emerge from choices and practical language. # Deep Critique of the Intention and Plan ## The Central Premise Is Strong, but the Emotional Center Needs Sharpening The plan has a compelling political and scientific engine, but Arthur’s personal stakes remain somewhat interchangeable. The outline mentions: - Workers. - Family. - Daughter. - Mara. - His home. - His land. - Martian independence. A short story cannot give equal emotional weight to all of these. It needs one primary personal bond or responsibility. Possible centers: ### Arthur and His Daughter Arthur discovered the deposit partly because he was trying to buy out his daughter’s labor lease. The Antares offer promises to free her, while the corporate offer promises to make her wealthy. Her situation forces Arthur to confront the temptation of private escape. This gives the negotiations a personal measure. When officials discuss “labor obligations,” Arthur knows exactly whose life they mean. ### Arthur and His Workers Arthur’s plot is legally independent, but he still employs people under an exploitative system. He may believe he treats them fairly while benefiting from the same structure. The discovery forces him to confront his own complicity. This would make the story morally richer. Arthur cannot simply stand outside the colonial system as its innocent victim. ### Arthur and Mara Mara may be the person who stayed behind, running the settlement while Arthur is taken away. Their communications can reveal their differing strategies: - Arthur wants to negotiate. - Mara wants to organize resistance. - Arthur thinks in terms of contracts and containment. - Mara thinks in terms of trust and people. Their conflict can make the final settlement feel earned. At present, the plan gestures toward all three. A stronger version should choose one as primary and use the others sparingly. ## The Antares Faction Needs a More Specific Contradiction “Diplomatic in name only” is an excellent prompt element, but the plan currently makes Antares broadly imperial and coercive. Their particular contradiction could be sharper. Possibilities: - Antares genuinely abolished indenture in its own territories but believes foreign colonies must be placed under temporary supervision. - Vale’s authority is technically illegitimate because he is part of a breakaway faction. - Sato knows Antares is trying to prevent war, but Vale has been ordered to use the crisis to secure a permanent military presence. - The armed retinue is not officially Antares at all; it is a private security corps using diplomatic insignia. Vale knows this and allows Arthur to believe otherwise. - Antares fears that Mars’s independence would create a model other colonies might imitate. A specific contradiction gives Vale sharper motivations than “he is a smooth imperial negotiator.” ## The Corporation Is Still Too Generic “The corporation” and “corporate negotiator” are recognizable but abstract. Niles Orra needs an individual pressure point. He might: - Have been born on Mars and sincerely believe corporate control is the only reason the settlements survived. - Have once worked with Arthur and know precisely how to appeal to him. - Be the son of a settler who died in an unregulated mine. - Be personally trapped by the corporation’s contract system. - Be trying to prevent a rival corporation from seizing the deposit. - Offer Arthur the contract because he believes it is the least bad option. His humanity does not require making him secretly good. It requires giving him a history that makes his position more than a speech. ## The Laborers Risk Remaining Props The plan makes indentured workers central to the setting but not yet central to the action. They are at risk of becoming evidence of exploitation rather than characters affected by it. A human approach would give one worker a distinct decision: - A worker refuses evacuation because leaving would void the family’s land claim. - A labor representative rejects Arthur’s offer of “protection” because it still places workers under his authority. - Workers sabotage the extraction machinery before Arthur reaches the mine. - A worker chooses to save a corporate supervisor, complicating the expected moral alignment. - The workers know a tunnel route that Arthur does not because the company has never recorded it. This would make labor agency part of the plot, not just the politics. ## The Scientific Danger Should Be Personal Before It Is Planetary The plan currently escalates from a drive test to station damage to planetary risk. The danger will feel more real if it first affects something intimate: - Arthur’s old pocket radiation meter begins ticking faster near the sample. - A worker’s implanted medical isotope decays prematurely. - Mara’s communication battery dies while she is transmitting a warning. - A familiar mining machine becomes dangerously brittle. - Arthur’s stored food sterilization system fails because its radioactive timing source has decayed. The reader should understand the danger through a thing someone touches or depends on before hearing about the threat to Mars. ## The Gambit Needs a Cost The title suggests a strategic maneuver, but a gambit usually involves risking something. Arthur’s current gambit risks political failure, but the personal cost is not yet definite. Possible costs: - Arthur must publicly lie about the mine and permanently damage his reputation. - He must allow a small number of people to believe he has betrayed them. - He must destroy the most accessible Azra, eliminating the fortune he could have claimed. - He must expose his own earlier complicity in labor exploitation. - He must risk Mara’s position in order to use the settler radio network. - He must choose which group receives incomplete data, knowing the wrong choice could cause deaths. - He must make a false promise that becomes legally binding. The best cost would connect to Arthur’s character. If he begins as someone who believes property and technical control can protect people, the gambit should require him to surrender ownership and accept collective uncertainty. # Brainstorming Ways to Make the Story More Human ## 1. Give Arthur a Mundane Immediate Problem Before the larger crisis, Arthur should be occupied with something ordinary: - A cracked water reclamation valve. - A shipment of replacement seals that has not arrived. - His daughter’s upcoming contract hearing. - A dispute over a boundary marker. - A broken heater in the workers’ dormitory. - A stubborn borehole that will not yield a reliable sample. The Azra discovery should interrupt a life already in progress. ## 2. Let Arthur Be Wrong Early Possible early misjudgments: - He assumes the armed men are corporate because corporations usually act first. - He believes Antares offers neutral protection. - He thinks the stolen sample is a bluff. - He assumes the mine’s danger can be solved by sealing the shaft. - He believes Mara will support his negotiation strategy. - He expects the workers to want him to speak for them. A believable story allows the protagonist’s understanding to change. ## 3. Use Specific Mars Rather Than Generic Mars The story should include details that are not just “red desert” imagery: - Dust infiltrating the pressure seals despite constant maintenance. - Low gravity changing how workers carry heavy equipment. - Long communication delays making arguments arrive after decisions have changed. - The horizon appearing too close through a pressure dome. - Old settlement modules buried under new construction. - Water allocations enforced by rotating valves and stamped permissions. - A settlement built around a failed terraforming project. - The psychological fact that no one on Mars hears wind without a suit or habitat wall between themselves and the planet. Mars should be a place where people have adapted, not just an empty backdrop. ## 4. Give Technology History Instead of listing retro devices, attach stories to them: - The transfer vessel still uses a navigation computer designed for the first wave of settlers. - Arthur trusts an analog meter because its digital replacement was altered by a corporation. - The legal punch-card system is obsolete, but it preserves a record the corporations thought they had deleted. - The mine’s pressure doors were designed by Arthur’s father or first mentor. - The old radio network was built by settlers before Antares recognized Mars as strategically important. Technology becomes human when it carries memory. ## 5. Make the Meeting Physically Uncomfortable The offer scene should not occur in a generic conference room. Details can create tension: - The station’s gravity is slightly uneven, causing cups to slide. - Arthur has not slept since leaving Mars. - The air smells faintly of hot insulation. - Vale sits beneath an Antares seal while Arthur’s chair has no restraint. - Sato keeps glancing at a radiation indicator. - The room is designed to be luxurious, but every surface is bolted down. - Arthur’s boots still carry Martian dust, which a station attendant repeatedly tries to clean away. Physical circumstances can reveal power relations without speeches. ## 6. Include Silence and Miscommunication People do not always respond immediately. Arthur may not understand a legal phrase. A transmission may cut off at the crucial moment. Sato may refuse to answer because answering would incriminate him. Useful human devices: - A delayed message arrives after Arthur has already made a decision. - Mara speaks while a corporate officer is listening, so she uses an old mining code. - Arthur hears only half of an argument through a closing pressure door. - Vale leaves a silence long enough that Arthur fills it with an admission. - A worker’s name is mispronounced repeatedly by officials, and the worker eventually stops correcting them. ## 7. Preserve Contradictory Feelings Arthur can feel several things at once: - Fear and pride. - Suspicion and gratitude. - Anger at exploitation and shame about benefiting from it. - Desire to leave Mars and refusal to abandon it. - Relief when the armed retinue arrives because at least someone has recognized the danger. - Resentment that his discovery is being taken seriously only because it is valuable. Characters become human when they do not experience one clean emotion per scene. ## 8. Allow Humor, but Keep It Dry A little understated humor can prevent the story from becoming solemn: - A diplomatic officer apologizes that the station’s “luxury accommodations” have only one functioning shower. - Arthur observes that every government calls its soldiers “security personnel” until the shooting starts. - Mara says the corporate men have arrived in polished boots, which means they have never worked near a drill. - A legal clause defines a person as “a mobile labor asset,” and Arthur remarks that at least the machinery receives maintenance rights. The humor should arise from character and institutional absurdity rather than from jokes. ## 9. Make the Final Political Change Legally Specific Rather than ending with a broad declaration that Mars is now free, show a concrete legal alteration: - The word “lease” is removed from one colony’s labor registry. - A settler council gains authority to deny access to a mine. - The old charter’s phrase “unclaimed planetary resources” is challenged in court. - A worker receives a copy of a contract with the word “void” stamped across it. - Arthur’s homestead is entered into a new registry under “occupied civic land” rather than “private concession.” Specific institutional change feels more credible and more satisfying. ## 10. Let the Final Image Resist Closure The final image should contain both hope and danger. Possible endings: - Arthur repairs the same gauge that first detected Azra, but its readings now flicker beyond the scale. - The Martian assembly begins while corporate ships remain in orbit. - A worker who has just been freed asks what happens to the old contract debts. - The sealed mine emits a faint mechanical hum. - Arthur receives a request to testify before a tribunal and realizes the next struggle will be legal rather than physical. - A distant transmission indicates that the Azra signature has been detected elsewhere, but Arthur switches it off before hearing the full message. A human ending does not necessarily answer every question. It leaves the character and reader with a changed world that still demands work. ## Recommended Refinement of the Existing Plan The existing plan is structurally sound, but the eventual writing should treat it as a flexible pressure map rather than a sequence of mandatory reveals. The most important refinements would be: 1. Select one primary personal relationship for Arthur. 2. Give the corporation and Antares factions distinct histories and internal contradictions. 3. Make at least one worker an active participant rather than a representative symbol. 4. Establish strict, limited rules for Azra. 5. Make Arthur’s gambit costly and partly dependent on other people. 6. Reduce the number of anonymous revelations. 7. Ensure every major exposition scene contains conflict, not just explanation. 8. Make the ending provisional rather than fully resolved. 9. Use retrofuturist technology as lived infrastructure. 10. Let the story preserve some messiness: mistrust, incomplete victories, legal uncertainty, and emotional residue. The strongest version will not be the one that explains the most about Mars or Azra. It will be the one in which the reader can feel the weight of a mining gauge in Arthur’s hand, understand why a contract matters to one worker, and see how a man who only wanted to keep his home becomes responsible for deciding who gets to call a planet their own.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **“The Azra Gambit”** will be a compact, dialogue-led political science-fiction story about ownership, coercion, and the difference between extracting a resource and living with it. The story will follow **Arthur Neegan**, a first-generation Martian mining engineer whose apparently worthless homestead sits over the largest known Azra deposit. Arthur will not be a chosen one, revolutionary leader, or universal genius. He is a practical man who understands rock, machinery, pressure, and the habits of mining companies. His authority will come from that knowledge—and from his refusal to let people who have never worked underground decide what can safely be done beneath Mars. The story will begin **aboard a transfer vessel**, with Arthur already being taken away from Mars by an armed retinue wearing insignia resembling the Antares diplomatic corps. The retinue’s arrival will be revealed through Arthur’s memories and fragments of dialogue. This will establish the required opening situation while placing the reader immediately inside Arthur’s uncertainty. The opening meeting with the unknown party will remain primarily dialogue. It will be a negotiation rather than an exposition scene. The unknown party will make a genuine offer: protection for Arthur’s land and freedom for the workers attached to it. But the offer will conceal an attempt to place Mars’s mineral rights under Antares control. Arthur will understand that he is not being invited because he is important as a person; he is important because his legal status and engineering records can legitimize someone else’s claim. Azra will have a limited, consistent scientific function: - It accelerates the radioactive decay of unstable isotopes. - In controlled systems, this makes compact propulsion and rapid energy release possible. - In concentrated or poorly contained systems, it rapidly damages reactors, batteries, instruments, and structural materials. - Arthur’s deposit is unusually large because it is naturally confined by Martian geological pressure. - Removing the material without understanding that confinement could turn a mine into a spreading industrial hazard. The story will not turn Azra into an alien artifact, magical substance, or device with a new power whenever the plot requires one. Its danger will arise from the consequences of its single defining property. The main personal relationship will be between Arthur and **Mara Venn**, his neighbor, former survey partner, and communications technician. Mara remains on Mars while Arthur is taken away. She knows the settlement, the workers, and the local radio network better than Arthur does. She also distrusts Arthur’s instinct to negotiate with powerful institutions. Their disagreement will provide the story’s emotional counterpoint: Arthur believes technical facts can force a rational settlement; Mara knows that institutions often understand facts perfectly well and exploit them anyway. Arthur will have one important personal obligation beyond the mine: he has been trying to buy out the labor contract of **Tomas Valez**, a young worker who has been repairing Arthur’s older equipment and whose family lease is tied to Arthur’s land. Tomas will not be a passive victim. He will participate in the resistance to the extraction operation and make at least one consequential choice without Arthur’s permission. This will keep the labor system from remaining background decoration. The Antares representative, **Director Lorne Vale**, will be courteous, intelligent, and dangerous. He will sincerely fear an interstellar arms race, but he will also believe that Mars cannot be trusted with a strategic resource. His language will be bureaucratic rather than openly villainous. He will use phrases such as “temporary trusteeship,” “stability administration,” and “protected development.” The story will show that polite language can conceal force without making every diplomat a cartoon tyrant. The corporate representative, **Niles Orra**, will be a former Martian settler who rose through the mining authority. He will believe that corporate infrastructure is the only reason Mars survived its early decades. He will be personally persuasive and may once have worked with Arthur. His argument will be plausible: centralized control prevents chaos. His flaw will be that he treats people as variables in a logistical system and considers coercion acceptable if the projected survival figures improve. Arthur’s gambit will not be a perfect master plan. It will be a desperate strategy assembled from incomplete information, aided by Mara, Tomas, and Dr. Ilyan Sato, an Antares physicist who has concealed some of what he knows. Arthur will exploit the fact that nobody can reliably determine the Azra vein’s exact size or stability. He will make the rival powers uncertain about what the others possess, then release enough evidence to make unilateral extraction politically and physically dangerous. The gambit will cost Arthur the private future he initially wanted. He could become wealthy by granting exclusive rights to the vein, and he could leave Mars with his family or workers protected. Instead, he will sacrifice personal ownership and destroy the most accessible section of the deposit to prevent a catastrophe. His victory will be limited but real: a provisional Martian authority will gain control over the sealed deposit, and the workers directly involved will be released from indenture. The ending will not solve colonialism, establish instant independence, or make every faction cooperate permanently. The immediate crisis will produce a legal and political opening. Antares will remain powerful. Corporate claims will continue in court. Other worlds will learn that Azra exists. Mars will have gained a voice, not complete freedom. The voice will be clear, restrained, and concrete. The story will aim for a classic science-fiction feeling through: - Dialogue that carries tension, character, and ideas. - Vivid but economical description. - Practical retrofuturist technology. - A movement between intimate details and large political consequences. - Scientific reasoning presented through disagreement and danger rather than lectures. - Dry humor and institutional absurdity where appropriate. The story will avoid: - Overwritten metaphors. - Long political briefings. - Symmetrical “both sides are equally right” morality. - A checklist-like succession of revelations. - Too many named factions. - Conveniently flawless broadcasting or legal solutions. - A completely tidy ending. - Having Arthur solve every problem without help. Each chapter will be approximately 1,000 words, so every chapter will center on one major scene or tightly connected sequence. Each should end with a concrete turn: a warning, a discovery, a betrayal, a decision, or an irreversible action. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter One: “The Polite Arrest” **Purpose:** Begin in motion, establish Arthur’s voice and circumstances, and create the mystery surrounding the armed retinue. - Open with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, watching Mars through a scratched observation port. - He notices the vessel’s age: brass trim, vacuum-tube indicators, mechanical shutters, and a navigation computer that clicks rather than speaks. - Arthur reflects that the men who took him had been very polite. - Recount the arrival at his homestead in brief, vivid fragments: - Armed officers in his kitchen. - An officer apologizing for bringing weapons indoors. - A demand that Arthur accompany them for “a consultation.” - Arthur asking whether he is under arrest. - The answer: “Not under any jurisdiction we recognize.” - Arthur notices the insignia resembles Antares diplomatic markings but has an unfamiliar black band. - He agrees to leave because the officers imply that refusal would make his workers “security liabilities.” - On the vessel, Arthur finds that his tool case and geological notebooks have been searched. - A steward delivers a meal and calls him “Mr. Neegan” rather than “prisoner.” - A message appears on the cabin’s old paper terminal: **DO NOT SIGN ANYTHING.** - End as the vessel enters orbit and Arthur sees corporate ships gathering above Mars. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur shifts from anger about being abducted to fear that the people who took him know more about his discovery than he does. ## Chapter Two: “The Offer” **Purpose:** Deliver the central off-world meeting and reveal the initial political stakes. - Arthur is taken to a diplomatic station above Mars. - The station is luxurious in a worn, practical way: bolted furniture, weak artificial gravity, old Antares banners, and radiation shielding hidden behind decorative panels. - Introduce Director Lorne Vale and Dr. Ilyan Sato. - Vale insists that Arthur has not been arrested and that Antares is trying to prevent corporate seizure of his land. - Arthur points out that men with rifles escorted him from his bed. - Their exchange should be tense, courteous, and primarily dialogue. - Vale offers: - Protection of Arthur’s homestead. - Release of Tomas and the other workers attached to his plot. - Cancellation of their lease obligations. - A substantial payment to Arthur. - A guaranteed Martian share in future Azra technology. - In return, Arthur must grant Antares “temporary stewardship” over the Azra deposit and publicly support the arrangement. - Arthur asks why Antares needs his signature. - Vale explains that Arthur’s land grant is one of the few independent claims with a legal chain of ownership recognized by both Mars and Earth. - Sato interrupts to correct Vale’s language: Arthur is not merely signing a mining agreement; he is validating Antares jurisdiction. - Sato also reveals that Arthur’s sample has behaved differently from known Azra. - Vale receives a message and briefly loses his composure. - End with Arthur being told that corporate fleets have arrived and that one of them is already claiming emergency authority over Mars’s mining zones. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur recognizes that the offer could genuinely free people he cares about, but accepting it would make him the instrument of a new form of control. ## Chapter Three: “The Old Lease” **Purpose:** Bring the human cost of the larger struggle into focus and let Arthur begin resisting the offer. - Arthur uses a delayed communications link to speak with Mara. - The connection is poor; they repeatedly talk over one another. - Mara reports: - Corporate surveyors have surrounded the homestead. - Tomas has been detained over an alleged equipment violation. - Workers are being told their contracts are now strategic labor obligations. - Arthur tries to reassure Mara that he is negotiating. - Mara replies that people who arrive with rifles usually consider the negotiation finished. - Arthur examines the Antares agreement using an old punch-card legal reader. - He discovers a clause granting Antares authority over “associated settlement personnel” and “support infrastructure.” - He understands that freeing his workers would be temporary and conditional. - Arthur remembers that he has spent years trying to buy Tomas’s contract out of his own wages, revealing his personal connection to the labor system. - He also realizes that he has benefited from the same structure, even if he has treated his workers better than the corporations do. - A second message reaches him through the station’s maintenance network. It contains a technical detail from his core samples that Antares should not possess. - Sato admits that someone in Antares has copied Arthur’s data. - Arthur refuses to sign the offer and demands to meet the corporate representatives. - Vale warns him that this will make him a public asset in a dispute between powers. - End with Mara saying that the main corporate drill has begun on Arthur’s land. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur stops thinking only about preserving his home and begins to understand that his own authority over the workers is morally compromised. ## Chapter Four: “The Second Set of Hands” **Purpose:** Introduce the corporate alternative and complicate the political conflict. - Arthur meets Niles Orra in a Martian administrative dome or orbital customs platform. - Orra is familiar with Arthur’s work and speaks to him as an equal. - He offers: - Full private ownership of the land and mineral rights. - Enough money to settle all outstanding debts. - Immediate release of Tomas and the workers under Arthur’s contract. - A senior engineering position for Arthur. - Orra argues that Antares is preparing to turn Mars into a protectorate. - He claims corporations, despite their abuses, built the habitats, water systems, and mines that keep Mars alive. - Arthur asks whether the workers can leave after being released. - Orra avoids the question. - A labor representative, **Jessa Kline**, appears through a station relay and reveals that the corporation plans to transfer the workers to a sealed extraction camp. - Orra calls this a temporary safety measure. - Arthur sees the same phrase—“temporary”—being used by both Antares and the corporation. - The corporate contract contains a clause allowing “emergency labor reassignment.” - Antares officers interrupt the meeting and attempt to remove Arthur. - Orra claims this proves Antares intends to seize the deposit. - During the confusion, Mara helps Arthur access an engineering feed from the mine. - The stolen Azra sample has begun destroying its containment case. - A radiation alarm starts across the platform. - End with a corporate announcement that its Azra propulsion prototype will be tested immediately, before the sample or the mine can be taken from them. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur sees that the rival powers are not opposites. They use different language to justify the same assumption: people and land can be managed without consent. ## Chapter Five: “The Decay Engine” **Purpose:** Establish the scientific danger through a concrete crisis and force Arthur into action. - Arthur, Sato, and Mara investigate the corporate prototype through stolen technical data and a damaged station control room. - Sato explains the basic principle of Azra without delivering a lecture: - It does not create energy. - It makes unstable matter surrender its stored energy and decay quickly. - It is useful only when the decay can be controlled. - Arthur notices that the prototype’s containment geometry is based on a mining diagram he recognizes as wrong. - The corporate engineers treated the Martian deposit as a uniform ore body. - The test begins. - The result is immediate and physical: - A battery dies. - A steel handrail crumbles under a technician’s grip. - A station clock’s radioactive timing source expires. - Emergency doors fail to cycle. - Sato admits that the large Martian vein is held in a natural pressure configuration. Removing too much of it may destabilize the surrounding material. - Arthur realizes the corporations have reached the deposit without understanding the thing that makes it safe. - The danger becomes personal when a worker trapped in the test chamber begins losing the function of a medical isotope implant or life-support regulator. - Arthur and Sato save the worker by shutting down the test, but the station suffers serious damage. - Mara reports that the corporate team on Mars has opened a main shaft. - End with Arthur stating that the question is no longer who owns the Azra. It is whether anyone can stop the mine from becoming an uncontrolled decay engine. **Key emotional movement:** The political conflict becomes an immediate physical emergency. Arthur’s technical knowledge is no longer merely leverage; it is necessary for survival. ## Chapter Six: “The Gambit” **Purpose:** Allow Arthur to construct a risky, imperfect strategy with help from others. - Arthur, Mara, Sato, and Jessa compare what each faction knows. - Arthur concludes that the rival powers are all acting on incomplete or manipulated data. - The exact size and stability of the vein cannot be measured because Azra is interfering with the instruments. - Arthur proposes a plan: - The corporations will be told that Antares has secured a stable sample. - Antares will be told that the corporations have a second deposit and intend to weaponize it. - The settlers and workers will receive the full data. - Arthur will publicly refuse exclusive ownership and demand a joint technical authority. - Mara objects that this is still manipulation. - Arthur acknowledges that it is, but says every powerful institution already believes it is entitled to manipulate Mars. - Jessa insists that workers must have representation in the proposed authority, not merely protection from it. - Sato reveals that Antares can suppress the scientific data if it chooses. He agrees to help Arthur transmit it because he has decided that secrecy is now more dangerous than disclosure. - Arthur prepares a broadcast using the old settler radio network. - The broadcast cannot reach every world and may expose the settlements to retaliation, so Arthur must choose which evidence to send. - He transmits: - The mine’s true hazard. - The altered contracts. - The corporate prototype failure. - The legal basis of the independent land claims. - Each faction receives the broadcast and interprets it according to its own fears. - End with Arthur returning to Mars alongside representatives of Antares, the corporation, and the workers as the mine’s pressure alarms begin to sound. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur accepts that he cannot preserve his private property and remain morally outside the system. He chooses a collective solution while knowing it may cost him everything. ## Chapter Seven: “The Vein” **Purpose:** Deliver the physical climax and make Arthur’s gambit depend on collective action. - Arthur arrives at the homestead as the mine begins to fail. - The settlement is under emergency lighting; dust has entered the air filters, and old machinery is decaying faster than it can be repaired. - Corporate guards attempt to seal the shaft and abandon trapped workers. - Tomas, having escaped or been freed during the confusion, leads workers through an undocumented maintenance route. - Arthur realizes he cannot order the workers to follow him; they are already acting on their own. - He and Tomas disagree over whether to save the extraction machinery or the people below. - Arthur chooses the workers and destroys the accessible Azra equipment. - Using his knowledge of the original mine design, he identifies the pressure-control chamber. - Sato calculates that the only safe course is to seal the upper vein and leave the deeper deposit untouched. - The plan requires: - Closing mechanical pressure doors. - Filling a shaft with regolith and neutralizing material. - Redirecting the mine’s power into a stabilizing field. - Manually resetting a control system because the automated controls are decaying. - Vale and Orra argue outside over jurisdiction while their own equipment fails. - Mara uses the settler radio network to keep workers coordinated. - Arthur sends the final data broadcast, including the fact that he is sacrificing his claim to the accessible deposit. - He tells the assembled factions that anyone who wants Azra must first accept that Mars—not Antares, not a corporation, and not Arthur—must decide how it is used. - The chamber seals, the decay slows, and Arthur is trapped behind a failed pressure door. - End with Mara hearing Arthur’s voice cut off mid-sentence. **Key emotional movement:** Arthur’s plan stops being a negotiation and becomes a sacrifice. His gambit works only because workers, scientists, and settlers act alongside him. ## Chapter Eight: “A Mars of Its Own” **Purpose:** Resolve the immediate crisis while preserving political uncertainty and emotional consequences. - Arthur survives in an old maintenance chamber and is rescued after the decay front stabilizes. - The accessible mine is ruined; the deeper Azra vein remains sealed. - The immediate political settlement is negotiated under pressure: - No corporation may claim the deposit privately. - No foreign power may administer it without Martian representation. - All future extraction requires scientific review and worker participation. - The workers tied to Arthur’s plot are released from indenture. - The old emergency labor clauses are suspended pending a broader legal challenge. - The agreement is provisional, not revolutionary. - Antares denies responsibility for the armed retinue. - The corporation threatens lawsuits and economic retaliation. - Jessa points out that the workers are free from one contract but still trapped by debts and housing obligations. - Arthur understands that the struggle has changed form rather than ended. - Vale offers Arthur a role on the new authority, expecting his technical credibility to legitimize it. - Arthur refuses a permanent political position but agrees to testify and help establish safety standards. - He returns to his damaged homestead with Mara. - Mara asks whether he regrets losing the fortune. - Arthur admits that he had imagined leaving Mars before the retinue arrived. - He now knows he might still leave someday, but not because someone bought the right to decide who lives there. - Final image: - Arthur repairs the old radiation gauge that first revealed the Azra. - The gauge works, but its needle trembles beyond the marked scale. - In the distance, the new Martian assembly begins its first broadcast. - Above Mars, an off-world observatory detects the Azra signature and quietly alerts another power. - End on an opening rather than a bow: Mars has secured a voice, but the wider Azra struggle has only begun.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Arthur Neegan Arthur Neegan is fifty-three, a first-generation Martian settler, born during the long transfer voyage that brought his parents and several hundred other colonists from Earth. He has never visited Earth. His childhood was spent in half-finished habitats, construction camps, and mining settlements where every object was repaired until it became a different object. He trained as a mining engineer because mining was the only profession on Mars that offered both technical authority and a chance to remain independent. He learned to read rock through vibration, pressure, and sound. His father taught him that a mine could lie in its reports but never in its walls. Arthur inherited a small land grant from his parents. The land was considered worthless because earlier surveys found no profitable deposits. Arthur stayed because the land was his, because moving would mean accepting a corporate lease, and because he had spent thirty years building the homestead’s systems himself. His personality is practical, watchful, and stubborn. He is not naturally charismatic. In a room full of politicians, he tends to notice the condition of the ventilation grilles before he notices who is speaking. He dislikes abstractions unless they can be connected to a measurement, a contract, or a person who will suffer from them. He is capable of tenderness but expresses it through maintenance: - He replaces Mara’s worn radio valves without telling her. - He keeps Tomas’s work gloves patched and dry. - He remembers which workers get headaches when the oxygen mix is slightly high. - He leaves a cup of hot mineral broth outside his daughter’s room when they have argued. Arthur has a deep suspicion of people who use courtesy as a weapon. He distrusts anyone who says “for your protection,” “temporary,” or “under the circumstances.” He also has an unfortunate tendency to become more polite when he is most furious. His flaws are substantial: - He mistakes endurance for virtue. - He has stayed out of politics because he believes technical competence should be enough. - He has benefited from the labor system he dislikes. His workers are better treated than corporate laborers, but they are still bound to his land through contracts. - He can be controlling. He often believes that because he understands the machinery, he should make the decision. - He withholds bad news until he has a solution, which means people sometimes discover danger too late. - He is slow to apologize and often begins an apology by explaining why he was technically correct. He initially wants three things: 1. To protect his homestead. 2. To secure freedom for Tomas and the workers attached to his plot. 3. To avoid becoming responsible for a political movement he does not understand. The Azra discovery forces him to realize that these goals conflict. Protecting his property through Antares could place Mars under foreign rule. Selling it to a corporation could free his workers temporarily while strengthening the system that enslaved them. Concealing the truth could prevent panic but leave others vulnerable. His most important relationship is with Mara Venn. They worked together on surveys decades earlier and have been close without ever quite deciding what to call the relationship. Mara knows Arthur’s habits well enough to identify his lies by the way he cleans his glasses. His relationship with Tomas is more complicated. Arthur sees Tomas as someone he must protect, but Tomas resents being treated as a responsibility rather than an adult. Arthur’s attempt to buy Tomas’s contract is generous but also paternalistic. Arthur assumes freedom can be purchased on Tomas’s behalf rather than won by Tomas himself. Arthur carries himself like a man accustomed to low gravity and narrow corridors. He leans slightly forward when walking, as if expecting the floor to shift. He is broad-shouldered but wiry, with a permanently sun-browned face despite the habitat walls. His hair is mostly gray and usually cut with a utility trimmer. He has a burn scar across two fingers of his left hand from an old drilling accident. He touches machinery while thinking. He taps a casing, runs his thumb along a seam, or places his palm against a wall to feel vibration. In conversation, he often looks past people toward the nearest gauge or structural joint. When frightened, he becomes very still. **Dialogue:** > “You can call it stewardship if you like. A lock is still a lock when a diplomat carries the key.” > “I know what the rock is saying. The trouble is that everyone else has decided they prefer the contract.” **Internal monologue:** > The men had been polite. That was the part he disliked. Men who meant to kill you were usually careless with manners. Men who meant to own you took the time to apologize. --- # Mara Venn Mara Venn is fifty-one, a communications engineer, surveyor, and long-time neighbor of Arthur’s. She came to Mars as part of the second major settlement wave, when the colony was no longer an experiment but not yet a functioning society. Her parents expected her to become a systems administrator in one of the corporate domes. She instead learned field surveying and spent years mapping forgotten tunnels, pressure conduits, and unofficial routes between settlements. Mara knows Mars socially rather than geologically. Arthur knows what lies under the ground; Mara knows who has access to the radio tower, who owes whom a favor, which corporate clerk drinks too much, and which settlement administrator quietly resents Earth. She has repaired the old settler communications network over many years. The network uses obsolete transmitters and a patchwork of relays, but it remains difficult for corporations to monitor because it was never built according to one system. Mara is decisive, irreverent, and emotionally direct. She has little patience for the language of official institutions. Where Arthur wants to inspect a contract, Mara asks who is standing outside the door with a gun. She is not fearless. Her courage often appears as irritation. She becomes sharper and more sarcastic when frightened. She dislikes being admired for bravery because she considers most of her decisions the least unpleasant option available. Her idiosyncrasies include: - She keeps a collection of obsolete radio components sorted by color rather than function. - She names communication relays after people who died while repairing them. - She drinks bitter chicory substitute and insists real coffee is a colonial myth. - She refuses to sit with her back to an airlock. - She hums old Earth songs but never remembers the verses in the correct order. - She leaves handwritten notes in equipment rooms because she distrusts digital logs. Mara’s flaws: - She is quick to assume that institutions are irredeemable. - She can mistake suspicion for intelligence. - She sometimes acts without telling Arthur because she expects him to argue rather than help. - She is impatient with people who need time to understand what she already sees. - She has a tendency to reduce complicated human motives to power and fear. - She resents Arthur’s desire to protect people, partly because it resembles an attempt to control them. Her core motive is to preserve the possibility of a genuinely Martian society. She does not necessarily seek immediate independence. She wants Mars to become a place where people can make decisions without first asking which distant authority will approve them. Her allegiance is to the settlers, workers, and local networks that have kept the colony alive. She distrusts both Antares and the corporations, though she knows some individuals within each institution are sincere. Her relationship with Arthur is intimate, argumentative, and unfinished. They have spent years relying on one another without discussing what that reliance means. Mara is one of the few people who can tell Arthur that he is being selfish and expect him to listen eventually. She has a strained relationship with Arthur’s daughter, who believes Mara has too much influence over her father. Mara worries that she has become part of Arthur’s life without earning the right to be there. Mara is tall and narrow, with dark skin weathered by years of surface work. She wears her hair short because long hair catches on helmet seals. One side of her face has a faint network of pale scars from a decompression accident. She moves quickly and economically, with an engineer’s habit of reaching for the correct tool without looking. She makes eye contact more aggressively than Arthur does. When someone lies to her, she often smiles first. When she is genuinely afraid, she becomes unusually quiet and checks the nearest exit. **Dialogue:** > “If they call it temporary, ask how long the temporary contract lasts. If they say they don’t know, count on your grandchildren paying it.” > “You keep thinking the truth will make them reasonable. Arthur, they know the truth. They just don’t have to live where it lands.” **Internal monologue:** > Arthur trusted walls because walls obeyed pressure. Mara trusted people only after they had failed in a way she could understand. --- # Tomas Valez Tomas Valez is twenty-eight and was born on Mars to a family whose labor contract began with the construction of the southern water reservoirs. His parents were never able to complete the payments required to release the family from corporate obligation. Tomas’s contract is technically personal, but several clauses bind his wages and movement to the settlement where he works. He is a mechanic and underground equipment specialist. Arthur hired him because Tomas could hear a failing motor before the instruments registered a problem. He has worked on Arthur’s drilling equipment for six years and knows the machinery almost as well as Arthur does. Tomas respects Arthur’s skill but does not idolize him. He has seen Arthur repair machines while postponing conversations with workers. He knows Arthur is better than the corporations and still benefits from the same system. Tomas is observant, quick-witted, and more politically aware than Arthur initially assumes. He has learned to appear less educated than he is when dealing with corporate supervisors. He keeps copies of every contract amendment, wage statement, and disciplinary notice in a hidden metal box. His small habits include: - Folding discarded forms into paper birds and leaving them in corporate offices. - Counting steps in tunnels. - Sleeping with one boot on because emergency alarms often fail. - Refusing to use the phrase “my employer” when speaking about Arthur; he says “the man who holds the lease.” - Collecting broken machine tags as reminders of equipment that failed and people who were blamed. His flaws: - He is suspicious of generosity because generosity often becomes another form of obligation. - He can be reckless when someone in authority challenges him. - He underestimates how much danger his defiance creates for others. - He has difficulty accepting help without interpreting it as condescension. - He wants freedom immediately and sometimes ignores the practical costs of achieving it. Tomas’s motive is not abstract independence. He wants the right to choose where he works, whether he stays on Mars, and what happens to his family. He does not want Arthur to buy his freedom; he wants the contract system invalidated. His allegiance is first to his family and coworkers, then to the broader labor movement. He is skeptical of settler leaders who speak about freedom while assuming someone else will keep the machines running. His relationship with Arthur begins with loyalty mixed with resentment. Arthur has been fair by the standards of Mars, but Tomas believes “fair by the standards” is how unjust systems survive. He cares about Arthur and does not want him killed, but he refuses to let Arthur speak for him. Tomas becomes central during the mine crisis. He knows an undocumented maintenance route because workers have used it for years to bypass corporate checkpoints. He chooses to return into the failing mine to rescue trapped workers, even after Arthur tells him to stay out. This is not simply rebellion; it is Tomas claiming authority over his own life and over the people who have trusted him. He is compact and muscular from years of working in low-gravity tunnels. His hair is usually shaved close because dust collects in it. He has grease permanently embedded in the creases of his hands. He moves with controlled speed, conserving energy and avoiding wasted gestures. He rarely raises his voice. His anger appears as precision. He becomes more formal when he is most upset. **Dialogue:** > “You can buy the contract, Mr. Neegan. You cannot buy the years it taught me that I was owned.” > “The machine is not broken. It is telling us the truth in the only language it has.” **Internal monologue:** > Arthur had always opened doors for him. Tomas was beginning to wonder whether that meant Arthur had also been the one standing in front of them. --- # Dr. Ilyan Sato Dr. Ilyan Sato is forty-four, a nuclear physicist and systems engineer attached to the Antares diplomatic mission. He grew up on Luna in a family of reactor technicians. He entered research because he believed the peaceful uses of nuclear science could make the colonies independent of Earth. His early work involved isotope recycling and long-duration propulsion. When Azra was discovered, he was assigned to study its effect on reactor systems. He soon learned that Antares was classifying much of the research under military security regulations. Sato remained because he believed he could limit the damage from inside the institution. This is the source of his guilt. He has not personally built a weapon, but he has signed secrecy orders and allowed misleading public statements to stand. Sato is precise, restrained, and uncomfortable with direct confrontation. He tends to correct terminology even when the correction is socially unhelpful. He has a habit of saying “strictly speaking” before making a statement that will upset everyone in the room. His idiosyncrasies: - He keeps a mechanical slide rule despite having access to far more advanced computing equipment. - He writes equations on scraps of packaging and then loses them. - He dislikes artificial gravity and prefers to work with his feet braced against a table. - He counts silently when alarms sound. - He apologizes to machines after striking them. - He always reads the warning labels on objects everyone else assumes are familiar. His flaws: - He confuses withholding information with preventing panic. - He is too willing to believe that technical experts should govern technical problems. - He has remained inside Antares because leaving would mean abandoning access to the data. - He is capable of intellectual arrogance and underestimates workers’ practical knowledge. - He uses qualifications and precision to delay moral decisions. Sato’s motive is to prevent Azra from becoming a weapon before its behavior is understood. He initially supports Vale’s temporary trusteeship because he believes a centralized authority is better than corporate competition. He gradually realizes that Antares’s secrecy is accelerating the very arms race it claims to prevent. His allegiance is divided. He feels loyalty toward colleagues who genuinely want to prevent catastrophe, but he is increasingly loyal to the facts themselves and to the people endangered by institutional secrecy. His relationship with Vale is formally respectful but personally strained. Vale values Sato as an expert and assumes that technical expertise will eventually support Antares authority. Sato knows Vale is not stupid or cruel, which makes opposing him more difficult. His relationship with Arthur begins with professional curiosity. Sato expects a local engineer to be technically competent but politically naïve. He gradually understands that Arthur’s practical knowledge has revealed flaws the laboratory models missed. Sato is thin, pale, and slightly stooped from years of working over instruments. His hair is black with a distinct white streak at the temple. He wears old-fashioned wire spectacles even though they are unnecessary. His hands are delicate but scarred from laboratory work. He stands very still when listening. In low gravity, he braces one hand against a surface without seeming to notice. When he finally makes a decision, he often becomes physically awkward, as if his body has not caught up with his conscience. **Dialogue:** > “The equation is not wrong. The assumptions are wrong, which is more expensive.” > “I thought secrecy would buy us time. It appears to have purchased a market.” **Internal monologue:** > Sato had spent ten years preventing dangerous information from reaching dangerous men. He had not considered what happened when every man in the room believed himself the exception. --- # Director Lorne Vale Lorne Vale is fifty-nine and serves as the Antares representative responsible for “colonial stability” in the Mars region. He was born on Antares Prime, a heavily administered world where government and corporate authority are so interwoven that most citizens no longer distinguish between them. Vale’s background is diplomatic, but he has spent much of his career negotiating after military interventions. He has learned that the most effective threat is one that allows the other person to describe the surrender as a reasonable choice. He does not think of himself as a tyrant. He believes in order, continuity, and preventing local disputes from becoming interstellar wars. His central error is that he regards consent as desirable but secondary to stability. Vale is cultivated, patient, and observant. He remembers names, family details, and small preferences. He knows when someone has not slept. He can make a threat sound like an accommodation. His idiosyncrasies: - He never sits beneath a camera if he can avoid it. - He carries an old-fashioned fountain pen, though it contains a recording device. - He dislikes unfiltered Martian coffee but drinks it when speaking to settlers. - He straightens objects on tables while listening. - He refers to weapons as “security instruments” even in private. - He has a habit of thanking people immediately after threatening them. His flaws: - He genuinely believes that educated administrators should make decisions for less stable societies. - He assumes that because he understands people, he understands what is best for them. - He is willing to use force as long as the paperwork is prepared beforehand. - He cannot accept that some people would choose danger over supervised safety. - He sees Arthur’s moral resistance as a form of provincial pride. Vale wants Antares to prevent a war over Azra. He also wants Antares to control the deposit because he believes only Antares has the infrastructure to regulate it. His allegiance is to Antares, but more specifically to the bureaucratic order that has made his career meaningful. He respects Arthur’s intelligence and finds Mara dangerous because she refuses to treat institutional language as neutral. He sees Sato as a necessary conscience until Sato becomes an obstacle. Vale’s relationship with Arthur shifts from attempted recruitment to frustrated admiration. He does not want Arthur dead; he wants Arthur persuaded, because a voluntary signature would be more valuable than a military seizure. Physically, Vale is slender and immaculate, with silver hair and a narrow face. He wears a formal Antares uniform beneath a plain gray diplomatic coat. His clothing is always perfectly adjusted, even when the station loses gravity. He rarely moves quickly. When angered, his voice becomes softer. He looks directly at people during threats and away from them during promises. **Dialogue:** > “Mr. Neegan, force is what happens when negotiation fails. We are offering you the opportunity to call this negotiation.” > “A free decision made under catastrophic conditions is not necessarily a wise one.” **Internal monologue:** > Vale had met revolutionaries, smugglers, generals, and three varieties of king. Arthur Neegan was more troublesome than any of them because he still believed the truth had standing. --- # Niles Orra Niles Orra is forty-eight, the senior legal and strategic representative of the Helix Mining Combine. He was born in a Martian construction camp and spent his childhood moving between corporate settlements. His father died in a shaft collapse that the company classified as “operator error.” Orra’s mother accepted a settlement that required the family to remain under corporate employment. Orra became a lawyer because he discovered that contracts could kill people at a distance and without noise. He rose through the corporation by learning how to make exploitation appear stable, predictable, and legally defensible. Unlike Vale, Orra does not pretend that the corporation is benevolent. He believes it is necessary. He sees every settlement as a system of inputs and failures. In his view, labor contracts are cruel but preferable to starvation, and corporate monopoly is preferable to military conflict. He is personable, practical, and occasionally funny. He remembers what food people like and sends gifts that reveal he has studied their biographies. His charm is not entirely false. He likes people, but he likes systems more. His idiosyncrasies: - He polishes his boots before difficult negotiations. - He keeps an old mining lamp from his father’s camp on his desk. - He dislikes elevators and uses service lifts whenever possible. - He quotes safety regulations from memory. - He never says “worker” when “contracted specialist” will do. - He takes handwritten notes, then discards the page after memorizing its contents. His flaws: - He believes every moral objection must eventually become a budgetary calculation. - He underestimates how much people hate being managed. - He treats his own rise through the corporate system as proof that the system is fair. - He thinks giving Arthur wealth is equivalent to giving him freedom. - He is unwilling to admit that the corporation may have caused the crisis by rushing the Azra extraction. Orra’s motive is to secure the deposit before Antares does. He believes that if Helix does not control Azra, a foreign power will, and Mars will lose what little autonomy it possesses. His allegiance is to Helix, but also to the corporate infrastructure that raised him. He has a complicated relationship with Arthur. They worked together briefly early in their careers, when Orra was still a field legal officer. He remembers Arthur as a man who wanted everyone to be paid fairly but never challenged the contract system itself. He considers Arthur an old friend who has become politically naïve. He sees Tomas as a dangerous but understandable young man. He is willing to free him from Arthur’s contract because doing so costs Helix nothing; he is not willing to abandon the broader labor structure. Orra is heavyset, well-fed, and physically comfortable in a way most Martian workers are not. He wears tailored work clothes rather than formal suits, hoping to appear practical. His hands are soft except for a single old callus on his right thumb from the mining lamp he keeps. He smiles often, but the smile disappears completely when someone challenges the corporation’s legal authority. He becomes most persuasive when discussing disasters that have not yet happened. **Dialogue:** > “A corporation is not a government, Arthur. That is why we can admit what a government cannot: sometimes people are safer when someone else owns the machinery.” > “You think ownership is the sin. I think it is the responsibility. The trouble is that responsibility and control are the same word once the oxygen fails.” **Internal monologue:** > Orra knew what the company had taken from Mars. He also knew what it had built. The difficulty was that the second fact never appeared in the speeches of people condemning the first. --- # Jessa Kline Jessa Kline is thirty-six and serves as a labor organizer among the independent and corporate mining settlements. She is the daughter of two indentured miners and spent her childhood moving between habitats when their contracts were transferred. Jessa began as a maintenance worker and became an organizer after a pressure-door failure killed six people in a corporate shaft. The company paid compensation to the families only after Jessa found and distributed a duplicate copy of the safety report. She is not a romantic revolutionary. She does not trust speeches, manifestos, or heroes. She trusts lists, names, shift rosters, spare oxygen cylinders, and people who show up when they say they will. Her personality is dry, disciplined, and intensely practical. She is often the calmest person in a room because she has already imagined the likely failures. Her anger is controlled and administrative. Her idiosyncrasies: - She carries a small notebook containing the names of every worker who has died under a disputed contract. - She marks important dates with small red squares. - She refuses to use corporate job titles. - She keeps emergency rations in places where supervisors would never look. - She calls Arthur “Engineer” when she wants to remind him that technical authority is not moral authority. - She can identify most people by their footsteps in a corridor. Her flaws: - She expects betrayal and can be unfair to people who have not yet betrayed her. - She is willing to endanger a few people to expose a larger injustice. - She dislikes compromise so much that she sometimes fails to recognize useful partial victories. - She has difficulty accepting that workers may want different things. - She holds Arthur accountable for the entire system because he is close enough to blame. Jessa’s motive is to end indenture and secure worker control over the conditions of labor. She does not initially care who controls Azra except insofar as all factions want to use workers as instruments. Her allegiance is to the miners and their families. She distrusts Arthur’s paternalism but recognizes that his technical knowledge may save lives. She distrusts Mara’s political instincts but values the communication network. Jessa becomes important in Chapter Six because she refuses to let Arthur’s proposed authority become another body that merely “protects” workers from above.
